CuratorialText:
In the early 1940s, decades into his artistic career, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) turned his focus to a new medium: cut paper. 

He had become bedridden and unable to paint following an excruciating abdominal surgery, and cut paper allowed him to continue to create in his relatively sedentary state. Encouraged by his friend, the book publisher Tériade (the pseudonym of Stratis Eleftheriades), Matisse furthered his exploration of this new technique. He mined his memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and produced a series of 20 cut-paper maquettes.
persona3_adapted:
In the early 1940s, after many decades as an artist, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) began working in a new way: cutting shapes out of paper.

He had become confined to bed after a painful stomach surgery and could no longer paint. Cutting paper allowed him to keep making art while staying still. Encouraged by his friend Tériade (a book publisher whose real name was Stratis Eleftheriades), Matisse explored this new technique further. He drew on memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folk stories, and myths, and created a set of 20 paper-cut models.

CuratorialText: These vibrant original compositions were then faithfully reproduced using pochoir (stencil) and combined with an original text written by Matisse to form an unbound book, Jazz, that was published in 1947—when Matisse was 77 years old. Jazz caused an immediate international sensation and reinvigorated the aging artist, setting him on a new course of artistic discovery.
persona3_adapted: These bright, original artworks were carefully copied using a hand-stenciling technique called pochoir. They were paired with a new text written by Matisse himself to create an unbound book titled *Jazz*, published in 1947 when Matisse was 77 years old. The book caused an immediate stir around the world and gave the aging artist a fresh burst of energy, leading him in a new direction of artistic exploration.

CuratorialText: Matisse’s extraordinary images of Jazz are not only captivating and provocative in their own right but also reflect a practice devoted to the exploration of color and line. Throughout the artist’s decades-long career, he pushed color from the purely visual to the nearly transcendental and progressively simplified his line to the most elemental. But until his cut-outs, his color and line existed separately, one modifying the other but never fully unified—at least not to Matisse’s own satisfaction.
persona3_adapted: Matisse’s *Jazz* images are striking and thought-provoking on their own, but they also show an artist dedicated to experimenting with color and line. Over his long career, Matisse moved color beyond just what we see, making it feel almost spiritual, while his lines became simpler and more basic. However, before he started using cut-out shapes, his color and line remained separate—each one changed the other, but they never fully came together in a way that satisfied Matisse himself.

CuratorialText: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s Jazz will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important livres d’artiste (artist books) of the 20th century, Jazz joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles to showcase the famed artist’s commitment across his 50-year career to continual innovation and the expressive power of color and line.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition is the first time the Art Institute has shown Matisse’s *Jazz* in full. The museum bought the work in 1948. *Jazz* is one of the most important artist-made books of the 1900s. It is joined here by more than 50 other works from the museum’s collection—paintings, sculptures, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles. Together, they show how Matisse, over his 50-year career, always tried new things and used color and line to express powerful feelings.

CuratorialText: From 6th-century gilt bronze Buddhist sculpture through Joseon dynasty painting and white porcelain and contemporary paintings of the late 20th century, the works of art in this exhibition demonstrate the artistic legacy produced on the Korean peninsula over millennia.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition brings together art created on the Korean peninsula over more than a thousand years. You’ll see a gold-covered bronze Buddhist statue from the 500s, paintings and white porcelain from the Joseon dynasty, and works from the late 1900s. Together, they show the long and rich artistic tradition of Korea.

CuratorialText: Once privately held, these artworks now belong to the Korean people thanks to a single groundbreaking gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021 the family donated over 23,000 works to the Korean government for public audiences to study and appreciate in perpetuity. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected exceptional and storied objects from throughout Korean history as a means of preserving and celebrating the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition marks the first time the works are on public display.
persona3_adapted:
These artworks used to belong to one family, but now they belong to the people of Korea. That’s because of a very generous gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, the late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021, his family gave more than 23,000 works of art to the Korean government so that the public could study and enjoy them forever.

Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected rare and meaningful objects from throughout Korean history. They did this to help preserve and celebrate Korea’s cultural heritage. This exhibition is the first time these works have ever been shown to the public.

CuratorialText: Among the 140 artworks on view—including modern and historical painting, ceramics, and objects made for Buddhist worship and scholarly study—22 are officially recognized as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These objects are recognized as remarkable examples of their type and distinguished for their exceptional historic, artistic, and academic value.
persona3_adapted: Of the 140 artworks in this exhibition, 22 are officially designated as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These pieces include modern and historical paintings, ceramics, and objects used in Buddhist worship and scholarly study. They are considered outstanding examples of their kind, valued for their historical, artistic, and academic importance.

CuratorialText: The largest Art Institute exhibition devoted to Korean art in four decades, Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art charts the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country’s creative production, from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s, offering insights into these objects’ rich meanings across centuries.
persona3_adapted: This major exhibition is the first in 40 years at the Art Institute to focus on Korean art. It explores the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped Korea's art over 2,000 years—from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) all the way to the 1900s. The show helps visitors understand the deep meanings these objects have held across many centuries.

CuratorialText: Epic and intricate, monumental and meticulous—the paintings of Kashmir-raised, London-based artist Raqib Shaw offer fantastical meditations on identity, transformation, and the redemptive power of beauty.
persona3_adapted: Raqib Shaw grew up in Kashmir and now lives and works in London. His paintings are both grand in scale and incredibly detailed. Through them, he explores big ideas about who we are, how we change, and how beauty can help heal or save us.

CuratorialText: Born in Calcutta and raised in the verdant Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life, many of which were fractured by political upheaval. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he relocated first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a broad range of sources—Mughal and Persian miniatures, Renaissance altarpieces, Old Masters paintings, Japanese arts of the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology—and yet his visual vocabulary and technique are all his own. His signature method involves applying an acrylic liner on gesso to create a golden line almost like the leading of a stained glass window. He then applies automobile enamel paints with needle-fine syringes and manipulates those with a porcupine quill, often adding glittering inset stones and baubles that further enhance the magical and dreamlike quality of the depicted scenes.
persona3_adapted:
Born in Calcutta and raised in the lush Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws heavily on the landscapes and memories of his early life—many of which were disrupted by political conflict. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he moved first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design.

His work is influenced by a wide range of sources, including Mughal and Persian miniature paintings, Renaissance religious artworks, Old Masters paintings, Japanese art from the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology. Yet his visual style and technique are entirely his own.

His signature method involves using an acrylic liner on a surface called gesso to create a golden line, similar to the lead in a stained glass window. He then applies car enamel paints using needle-thin syringes and shapes them with a porcupine quill. He often adds sparkling inset stones and small ornaments, which enhance the magical, dreamlike quality of the scenes he creates.

CuratorialText: At the Art Institute, Shaw debuts his more-than 100-feet-wide, 21-panel Paradise Lost (2009–25), the artist’s most ambitious and personal project to date. This magnificent allegorical painting takes viewers on a spellbinding journey, from the nocturnal solitude of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir to the frenzied daylight of the art world and the West to finally a fragile, renewed dawn. Each panel is dense with symbolism: mythical beasts, anthropomorphic hybrids, collapsing kingdoms, and natural beauty in various states of transformation. Throughout, the work is dotted with images of the artist, sometimes as a humanoid creature with different animal heads, at another time as a monkey looking with awe at the gleaming edifices and the wealth of the West, and sometimes unambiguously in full human form seated on a bed of saffron under a blossoming cherry tree, lost deep in his thoughts
persona3_adapted:
At the Art Institute, Shaw is showing his new work, *Paradise Lost* (2009–25). It is over 100 feet wide and made up of 21 panels. This is his biggest and most personal project yet. The painting tells a story through symbols and images, taking viewers on a journey. It starts with the quiet, lonely nights of Shaw’s childhood in Kashmir, moves into the busy, bright world of Western art, and ends with a fragile, hopeful new dawn.

Each panel is packed with meaning. You’ll see mythical animals, creatures that are part human and part animal, falling kingdoms, and beautiful natural scenes that are changing. Throughout the work, Shaw includes pictures of himself. Sometimes he appears as a human-like creature with different animal heads. Other times, he is a monkey staring in wonder at the shiny buildings and wealth of the West. And in one clear image, he sits in full human form on a bed of saffron under a blooming cherry tree, lost in thought.

CuratorialText: The work is not a direct retelling of Milton’s 17th-century poem Paradise Lost, but rather a reflection on the many paradises lost across a lifetime: childhood innocence, creative freedom, mental tranquility, cultural belonging. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”
persona3_adapted: This artwork isn’t a direct retelling of John Milton’s 17th-century poem *Paradise Lost*. Instead, it reflects on the many kinds of “paradises” we lose over a lifetime—like childhood innocence, creative freedom, peace of mind, or a sense of belonging to a culture. As the artist Shaw put it, “This is not just my story. It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”

CuratorialText: While Shaw has previously shown the first chapter of Paradise Lost, this exhibition marks the first time all four chapters will be displayed, showcasing the fullest extent yet of this epic masterpiece. “This painting was made not only to document a life,” Shaw shared, “but to offer something to those who come after, to offer a space of reflection for emotional recognition and perhaps for quiet rebellion against forgetfulness. I do hope that this work invites the viewer to slow down and to look carefully and to feel without haste.”
persona3_adapted: While Shaw has shown the first part of *Paradise Lost* before, this is the first time all four parts are on display together. It gives us the most complete view yet of this large, ambitious work. Shaw said, “This painting wasn’t just made to record a life. I wanted to give something to people who come later—a place to pause, to recognize feelings, and maybe to quietly push back against being forgotten. I hope it invites viewers to slow down, look closely, and feel without rushing.”

CuratorialText: Exquisitely crafted and shrouded in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.
persona3_adapted: These incredibly detailed ceramics, known as Saint-Porchaire ware, are some of the rarest objects from the French Renaissance. They are beautifully made, but we know very little about how or why they were created.

CuratorialText: Only around 70 examples are known to exist worldwide, and each one dazzles and intrigues with its refined and delicate modeling and astonishingly intricate decoration. Created between about 1520 and 1570 and closely associated with the royal courts of François I and Henri II, these extraordinary works bear the emblems of power, prestige, and desire—including the interlaced cipher of Henri II and his famed mistress, Diane de Poitiers.
persona3_adapted: Only about 70 of these objects are known to exist in the entire world. Each one is stunning and fascinating, with its elegant, detailed shapes and incredibly complex decoration. They were made between about 1520 and 1570 and are closely linked to the royal courts of King François I and King Henri II. These remarkable pieces display symbols of power, status, and desire—including the linked initials of Henri II and his famous mistress, Diane de Poitiers.

CuratorialText: Saint-Porchaire ceramics embody the sophistication and inventive spirit of Renaissance court culture. Fashioned from lead-glazed earthenware, their sculptural forms echo precious metalwork, while their ornamental patterns draw inspiration from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury arts. Together, they express a unique and daring artistry, playful elegance, and refined taste at the height of French royal patronage.
persona3_adapted: Saint-Porchaire ceramics show just how creative and refined the French royal court was during the Renaissance. These objects were made from a type of pottery coated with a shiny, glass-like finish. Their shapes often look like fancy metalwork, and the decorative patterns were inspired by things like fabric, book covers, and other luxury items. Together, they reveal a bold and playful artistic style—one that celebrates elegance and high-class taste at the peak of French royal support for the arts.

CuratorialText: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will present an exceptional group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection—most on public view for the first time since the late 1800s—in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. These rare works will be shown alongside other notable examples of French Renaissance ceramics drawn from the Art Institute’s collection, including several recent acquisitions.
persona3_adapted: Starting in March, the Art Institute will showcase a remarkable set of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection. Most of these pieces haven't been seen by the public since the late 1800s. They will be displayed in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. Alongside these rare works, visitors can also see other important examples of French Renaissance ceramics from the museum's own collection, including several pieces that were recently acquired.

CuratorialText: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused presentation invites close encounters with these remarkable jewel-like objects, while its placement within the broader context of the Deering Family Galleries highlights the rich dialogue between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that flourished during the Renaissance.
persona3_adapted: This small, focused exhibit is set up in the cozy James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery. It lets you get up close to these beautiful, jewel-like objects. Because the gallery is part of the larger Deering Family Galleries, you can also see how pottery and ceramics were connected to the metalwork, fabrics, and decorative arts that were so popular during the Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Over a long and distinguished career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has focused on making mezzotint prints, perhaps the most demanding of all print techniques.
persona3_adapted: For more than forty years, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has dedicated himself to a single, painstaking art form: mezzotint printmaking. This technique is widely considered the most challenging way to make a print.

CuratorialText: Mezzotints are known for their dark and atmospheric appearance. This is because the starting point for any mezzotint is the creation of a roughened surface, which produces a solid black background when printed. To draw the image that emerges from this dark background, the artist uses a series of burnishers and scrapers; the deepest gouges print as white areas on the finished print.
persona3_adapted: Mezzotints are a type of print that looks dark and moody. That's because the artist starts by making the surface of the printing plate rough and bumpy. When ink is applied and the plate is pressed onto paper, this rough surface creates a solid black background. To create the image that comes out of that darkness, the artist uses special tools to smooth or scrape away parts of the rough surface. The deeper the scratches, the lighter those areas will appear in the final print—sometimes even white.

CuratorialText: This presentation includes earlier, smaller-format works done without color, as well as more recent larger work that boast many hues. Among the earlier monochromatic prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 Viva Chicago series, which focuses on the city’s beloved public monuments like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”
persona3_adapted: This exhibit shows two types of Hamanishi's work: earlier, smaller black-and-white prints, and newer, larger pieces full of color. One example of the earlier black-and-white work is his 1997 series *Viva Chicago*. In this series, he focuses on the city's famous public monuments, such as the sculptures by Picasso and Miró. The artist said, "When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago."

CuratorialText: The exhibition also celebrates several important gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—shown here for the first time—is a recent gift from the artist. Each work in the series is reminiscent of the panels of a folding screen and features seasonal floral imagery. Many works in the show come from the 2013 gift to the museum from the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints in the world.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition also highlights several meaningful gifts to the museum. One of them is Hamanishi’s *Four Seasons* series from 2022, which is on display here for the first time. The artist himself donated these works. Each piece in the series looks like a panel from a folding screen and shows flowers and plants associated with a different season. Many other works in the show come from a 2013 gift by the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection. That donation gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints anywhere in the world.

CuratorialText:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over the course of five decades, Dunham has engaged in wide-ranging formal and thematic experimentation across various media, yet his drawings represent a distinct, interconnected body of work. Dunham’s mature artistic career began in 1970s New York amid a scene dominated by Minimalist aesthetics. Using simple elements such as line, shape, and color, he made a tremendous impact with work that nevertheless signaled a return to subject matter. In the intervening decades, Dunham has continued to produce drawings that test, and ultimately collapse, the porous boundaries between abstraction and figuration: bodies shapeshift into buildings, trees pose like models, the cellular resembles the cosmic, and internal organs or private parts are made very public.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Carroll Dunham once said, "There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation." Over the past fifty years, Dunham has tried out many different styles, themes, and materials in his art. But his drawings stand out as a special, connected group of works.

Dunham's serious art career started in New York City in the 1970s. At that time, the art world was mostly focused on a style called Minimalism, which used very simple, clean forms. Dunham also used simple elements like lines, shapes, and colors, but he made a big impact by bringing back a focus on real subjects and stories.

In the years since, Dunham has kept making drawings that blur the line between two types of art: abstract (where shapes and colors don't look like real things) and figurative (where art shows recognizable objects or people). In his work, bodies turn into buildings, trees strike poses like models, tiny cells look like vast galaxies, and private body parts are shown very openly.

CuratorialText: Working in series, Dunham methodically tests out every possible outcome of a drawing’s composition or content. This approach seems to spontaneously yield new imagery and ideas that link one body of work to the next. His influences are likewise wide-ranging—art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science as well as science fiction and comic books—all of which inform his explorations of the tension between male and female, nature and culture, self and other.
persona3_adapted: Dunham works in series, meaning he carefully explores every possible version of a drawing’s layout or subject matter. This method naturally leads to new images and ideas, connecting one group of works to the next. His inspirations come from many different places—art history, philosophy, psychology, and evolutionary science, as well as science fiction and comic books. All of these influences shape his work as he examines the tension between male and female, nature and culture, and self and other.

CuratorialText: Dunham foregrounds the development of his work by precisely dating each drawing. This practice is evidenced in the thousands of drawings he has organized into an archive, from which this show is derived. Developed in conversation with the artist, this comprehensive survey is the first museum presentation focused exclusively on Dunham’s drawings. Encompassing a 50-year period and featuring many drawings making their public debut, it celebrates the medium central to Dunham’s expansive practice.
persona3_adapted: Dunham shows how his work has evolved by carefully dating each drawing. He has done this with thousands of drawings, which he has organized into a personal collection. This exhibition is based on that collection. Created in collaboration with the artist, this wide-ranging show is the first museum display to focus only on Dunham’s drawings. It covers 50 years of his work and includes many drawings that have never been shown to the public before. The exhibition celebrates drawing, which is the core of Dunham’s broad creative practice.

CuratorialText: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute unveiled a newly imagined and installed gallery for the arts of Korea—our first space fully dedicated to this cultural region.
persona3_adapted: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute opened a newly designed gallery for Korean art. This is the first space in the museum that is entirely focused on this cultural region.

CuratorialText: The new installation presents a wider range of objects than previous displays—extending from ceramics to textiles and painting and spanning 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) until today. Intentionally located between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, the thoughtful display offers insight into how these artworks both reflect the religious and material culture of Korea and how they relate to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.
persona3_adapted:
This new exhibit shows a much bigger variety of Korean art than before. You'll see everything from pottery and fabric art to paintings, covering 2,000 years—from the Three Kingdoms period (around 57 BCE to 676 CE) all the way to the present day.

The gallery is placed right between the Chinese and Japanese art rooms on purpose. This thoughtful setup helps you understand how these artworks not only show Korea's religious and everyday life, but also how they connect to the art of Korea's powerful neighbors, China and Japan.

CuratorialText: Six themes introduce Korea’s long and rich history as well as its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section introduces objects that were made to help understand and spread the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece, an 18th-century Buddha statue, is on view for the first time since its acquisition after extensive conservation treatment. A section devoted to celadon and tea culture provides a glimpse into the practice of tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392), and objects grouped around the idea of symbolism illustrate how beautiful embellishments represent desired outcomes, such as a blissful marriage, career advancement, and long life.
persona3_adapted: Six sections tell the story of Korea’s long and fascinating history, including its religion, politics, and everyday life. The first section features objects made to explain and share the teachings of the Buddha. The main highlight is an 18th-century statue of the Buddha, which is on display for the first time since the museum acquired it—after careful restoration work. Another section focuses on celadon pottery and tea culture, giving a look into how tea ceremonies were practiced during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Finally, objects grouped by their symbolic meanings show how beautiful decorations represent things people hope for, like a happy marriage, success in work, and a long life.

CuratorialText: The remaining three sections focus on how material culture responded to a major ideological, political, and cultural shift during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897) as evidenced in the creation of the earthy grey stoneware known as buncheong, elegant white porcelain, and scholars’ objects. The new installation also includes select contemporary works that meaningfully engage with traditional forms and materials.
persona3_adapted: The last three sections of the gallery look at how everyday objects changed during a major shift in ideas, politics, and culture in the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897). This change is shown in the creation of three types of items: a rough, gray stoneware called *buncheong*, a refined white porcelain, and objects used by scholars. The new display also includes a few modern works that thoughtfully connect with these traditional shapes and materials.

CuratorialText: The transformed space explores aspects of life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley with the first new installation of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in a quarter-century. Striking artifacts—displayed along one wall of the gallery in a series of innovative cases that promote viewing from multiple vantage points—provide insight into the beliefs and practices of this illustrious North African culture.
persona3_adapted: This new gallery brings together objects from the museum’s ancient Egyptian art collection for the first time in 25 years. The space explores life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley. Along one wall, striking artifacts are displayed in innovative cases that let you see them from different angles. These objects offer a window into the beliefs and daily practices of this remarkable North African culture.

CuratorialText: Recurring themes throughout this fresh presentation of the collection consider the impact of Egypt’s natural environment, including the Nile River, on its visual culture; reveal the processes of ancient Egyptian artists; and explore the centrality of gods and goddesses to life (and death) along the Nile. Arresting sculptures, such as the statue of Shebenhor, reveal how ancient Egyptians chose to present themselves so that they would be remembered for eternity, while funerary works—such as a gilded funerary mask—unveil practices for preparing for and protecting oneself in the afterlife.
persona3_adapted:
This fresh look at the collection brings together several big ideas that keep coming up. One is how Egypt’s natural surroundings—especially the Nile River—shaped the art and objects people made. Another is how ancient Egyptian artists actually created their work. And a third is just how central gods and goddesses were to everyday life (and death) along the Nile.

Some pieces, like the striking statue of a man named Shebenhor, show how Egyptians wanted to be remembered forever. Other objects, such as a shiny gold funeral mask, reveal the rituals people followed to prepare for and protect themselves in the afterlife.

CuratorialText: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago unveils an elegant and thought-provoking new display of architectural fragments from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 19th century, Chicago’s urban landscape has experienced continuous change. Over time, many  transformations of the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, preserved only in collective memory and archival photographs. Architectural fragments provide another avenue to understanding our urban past, including historical building practices and technologies, our dynamic communities, and evolving ways of life.
persona3_adapted: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago is showing a beautiful and thought-provoking new collection of pieces from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 1800s, Chicago’s cityscape has been constantly changing. Over time, many of these changes to the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, surviving only in people’s shared memories and old photographs. These building pieces offer another way to understand our city’s past—including how buildings were constructed, the technologies used, the lively communities that existed, and how people’s ways of life evolved.

CuratorialText: In this new installation, sculptural elements from building facades and interiors are displayed in groups that allow visitors to learn about many architecturally or culturally significant structures and moments in Chicago. These range from celebrated buildings by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to important local histories related to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, labor, and preservation.
persona3_adapted: In this new exhibit, pieces taken from the outsides and insides of buildings are grouped together so visitors can explore some of Chicago's most important architecture and history. You'll see works from famous buildings designed by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan, as well as objects that tell stories about local communities—such as the Bronzeville neighborhood on the South Side—and topics like labor and historic preservation.

CuratorialText: Featuring well-loved works from the permanent collection alongside important new acquisitions—such as a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this installation, designed by the Chicago-based practice Norman Kelley, offers dynamic encounters with the museum’s rich holdings of architectural fragments, an integral part of our shared material history.
persona3_adapted: This installation brings together favorite pieces from the museum’s permanent collection and exciting new additions—like a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey. Designed by the Chicago-based firm Norman Kelley, the display creates fresh, engaging ways to experience the museum’s large collection of architectural fragments. These pieces are an important part of the physical history we all share.

CuratorialText: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is more than surface decoration. In this region, known as SWANA, artists have used, and continue to use, centuries-old embellishment techniques to transmit cultural expressions of identity, status, and long-held belief systems. Cultural groups throughout the region wear layers of dress and adornment to protect their bodies physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are ornamented with valuable materials that function as wearable wealth, including symbolic motifs and designs constructed with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.
persona3_adapted: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa—a region often called SWANA—embroidery is much more than just decoration. For centuries, artists have used traditional stitching and embellishment methods to share ideas about who they are, their social standing, and their long-held beliefs. Across the region, different cultural groups wear layered clothing and accessories to protect themselves, both physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are decorated with valuable materials, like coins, thread, beads, charms, and jewelry. These items often include symbolic patterns and designs that act as a kind of "wearable wealth," showing the owner's status and heritage.

CuratorialText: The vast area that spans from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east is connected by millennia-old trade networks. The works presented reveal pathways of deep cultural exchange shaped by diverse landscapes—from mountains and deserts to seas—along with the movement of nomadic peoples and the spread and interweaving of multiple religious traditions and urban fashions.
persona3_adapted: This huge region stretches from Morocco and Algeria in the west all the way to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east. For thousands of years, trade routes have connected these lands. The artworks on display show how cultures mixed and influenced each other as people traveled across very different landscapes—mountains, deserts, and seas. Nomadic groups moved through these areas, and different religions spread and blended together. City styles and fashions also traveled along these routes.

CuratorialText: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago devoted exclusively to the artistic and cultural traditions of SWANA and spectacularly showcases the region’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual expressions. Drawn primarily from the Art Institute’s rich collection of textiles, jewelry, and adornment, this display presents over 70 works from the 18th through 20th century—most of which have never been exhibited publicly—including four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition, *Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan*, is the first at the Art Institute of Chicago to focus entirely on the art and culture of the SWANA region (Southwest Asia and North Africa). It highlights the area’s unique beauty and well-known visual styles. Most of the items come from the museum’s own collection of textiles, jewelry, and body ornaments. The show includes over 70 pieces made between the 1700s and 1900s—many of which have never been shown to the public before. Among them are four rare face veils borrowed from a private collector in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

CuratorialText: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.
persona3_adapted: Among the other standout pieces are beautifully decorated clothing from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral "treasure necklaces" from Morocco's Anti-Atlas Mountains; finely embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute's outstanding collection of Ottoman Turkish fabrics; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) people of Iraq.

CuratorialText: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film Sanctus depicts human bodies at risk. It features archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982) of people making simple gestures and engaging in everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking liquids, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer rephotographed the original film and spliced the images together in a visceral montage set to overwhelming requiems by Neil B. Rolnick. She further transformed the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects and by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. In presenting images captured by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer underscored her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging, and even treatment, can cause harm.
persona3_adapted: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film *Sanctus* shows human bodies in vulnerable situations. The film uses old X-ray footage taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982). In these X-rays, people are doing simple, everyday things like putting on lipstick, drinking, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer took this original film and re-photographed it, then cut and rearranged the images into a powerful sequence set to intense music by Neil B. Rolnick. She also changed the X-rays by adding flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects, and by speeding up, repeating, or reversing the movements. By showing images created with medical technology that uses high levels of radiation, Hammer made the point that “science advances through the death of animal and human subjects in experiments,” and that taking images—or even giving treatment—can itself cause harm.

CuratorialText: Sanctus emerged from Hammer’s initial engagement with medical themes, an interest incited in the early 1980s by the AIDS epidemic. Over the course of her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video with an unflinching focus on queer communities and on her encounters within the American medical system.
persona3_adapted: Sanctus grew out of Barbara Hammer’s first exploration of medical topics, a subject that caught her attention in the early 1980s because of the AIDS crisis. Over her 50-year career, Hammer used photography, 16mm film, and video to look directly and honestly at queer communities and at her own experiences with the American healthcare system.

CuratorialText: Step back in time and immerse yourself in the spiritual, domestic, and chivalric worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor present nearly 700 objects from the museum’s rich holdings of art from 1200 to 1600: monumental altarpieces, exquisite jewelry, and the beloved arms and armor collection.
persona3_adapted: Step back in time and explore the everyday life, faith, and ideals of honor from the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor display nearly 700 items from the museum’s collection, dating from 1200 to 1600. You’ll see large religious paintings, fine jewelry, and the popular collection of weapons and armor.

CuratorialText: The sumptuous display begins with a large room featuring architectural sculpture, treasury objects, and early painting that convey the austere sanctity of earlier medieval art. The centerpiece of the gallery is the meticulously conserved Ayala Altarpiece, commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s funerary chapel in northern Spain. Spanning nearly 24 feet across and eight feet high, the painted wood altarpiece has undergone extensive treatment over the last three years and makes its restored debut in brilliant form. Two linked vaulted galleries showcase altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute favorite Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell. Each is displayed to suggest its original religious space, highlighting the church as a center of public life.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition starts with a grand room filled with stone carvings, religious objects, and early paintings that show the simple, sacred feeling of medieval art. The main attraction is the carefully restored Ayala Altarpiece, a large painted wooden artwork commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family's burial chapel in northern Spain. Measuring almost 24 feet wide and eight feet tall, this piece has undergone three years of restoration and is now on display in brilliant condition for the first time. Two connected, arched rooms feature altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the museum's popular piece *Saint George and the Dragon* by Bernat Martorell. Each artwork is arranged to suggest its original church setting, highlighting how churches served as centers of community life.

CuratorialText: The spaces that follow are more intimate, focusing on the texture of late Gothic and Renaissance life. One gallery presents luxury goods and the accessories of feasting, while another displays works of art for the bedchambers of Tuscany’s merchant elite. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and items of personal display, complete the domestic picture of the period.
persona3_adapted: The next rooms feel smaller and more personal. They show what daily life was like in the late Gothic and Renaissance periods. One room has fancy goods and items used for feasts. Another room shows artwork made for the bedrooms of wealthy merchants in Tuscany. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and things people wore to show off, help complete the picture of home life during that time.

CuratorialText: An elegant rotunda introduces the collection of arms and armor with equipment for the bodyguards of four rulers. From here, the space opens to a gallery filled with weaponry and armor dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed and costumed figures engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinetry features the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and equipment for the hunt extending through the Baroque era.
persona3_adapted: A graceful, circular room welcomes you to the collection of arms and armor, featuring equipment used by the bodyguards of four rulers. From there, the space opens into a gallery filled with weapons and armor. The room is dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed, costumed figures fighting on foot. At the end, a gallery lined with cabinets showcases the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and hunting equipment, all from the Baroque period and beyond.

CuratorialText: The new installation expands the display of art of this fascinating period sixfold, premiering several recently conserved works and many significant new acquisitions—an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the architecture of the galleries propel visitors through time and from religious to secular works, while digital labels provide an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations serve as the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering, Sienese and Florentine gold-ground paintings and early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson, a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine Benedetto Buglioni and a South German carved and painted Saint Catherine from Kate Buckingham, and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to enchant.
persona3_adapted:
Step into a world of knights, saints, and masterpieces. This new gallery space shows six times more art from this fascinating era than before. You'll see several recently restored works and many important new additions, including an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate (armor for the chest and back), a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding collection of swords from the 1600s and 1700s.

As you walk through the galleries, subtle changes in the room design guide you through time—moving from religious art to everyday and royal objects. Digital labels let you explore the art through interactive multimedia.

At the heart of this display are gifts from major collectors whose donations form the foundation of the collection. You'll find the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon, donated by Charles Deering; gold-backed paintings from Siena and Florence, plus early Northern European works, given by Martin Ryerson; a terracotta (baked clay) altarpiece by Florentine artist Benedetto Buglioni; a carved and painted statue of Saint Catherine from South Germany, donated by Kate Buckingham; and George F. Harding's impressive collection of arms and armor.

All of these treasures come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that still captures our imagination.

CuratorialText: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into unseen histories. Drawing from art-historical, cinematic, and literary sources, her memorable images can haunt the viewer long after encountering them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together approximately 20 paintings by the artist.
persona3_adapted: Jill Mulleady creates paintings that feel fresh and alive, even though they draw inspiration from overlooked stories of the past. She takes ideas from art history, movies, and books, and turns them into images that stick with you—sometimes haunting your thoughts long after you've seen them. This exhibition is Mulleady's first solo show at a major museum, and it brings together about 20 of her paintings.

CuratorialText: Mulleady uses various methods including staining, and at times incorporating materials such as copper, to achieve fragile, luminous surfaces that feel impossibly thin yet visually deep, holding tension between flatness and depth. Not limited to canvas or linen, she employs a range of supports, including glass and velvet. The resultant works balance different degrees of transparency and opacity and can be looked at and, sometimes, seen through.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady uses different techniques, like staining fabric or adding materials such as copper, to create surfaces that look delicate and glowing. These surfaces feel incredibly thin but also seem to have a lot of depth, creating a sense of tension between being flat and having dimension. She doesn't just work on canvas or linen—she also uses materials like glass and velvet. The finished pieces mix varying levels of see-through and solidness. You can look at them, and sometimes, you can even see through them.

CuratorialText: Mulleady’s paintings often depict scenes that are enigmatic and dangerous, showing figures at risk or under threat—from appetite, predation, or compulsion. While these ambiguous scenes may imply narratives, they likewise refuse tidy resolutions. Her imagery draws actively on painting lineages—allegory and the Baroque, Symbolist unease, the grotesque—recasting genres and canonical images for the present. In contrast to the purity and rationality of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady prioritizes a tradition in which chaos, instinct, and history drive art making.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady’s paintings often show scenes that feel mysterious and unsettling. The people in them seem to be in danger—whether from hunger, being hunted, or some inner urge they can’t control. These images hint at a story, but they don’t give you a clear ending. Her work draws on older styles of painting—like dramatic, emotional scenes from the 1600s, dreamlike and uneasy images from the late 1800s, and strange, exaggerated figures. She takes these classic styles and reworks them for today. Unlike the clean, logical art of the 20th century, Mulleady focuses on a tradition where chaos, raw instinct, and history are the forces that shape art.

CuratorialText: As Mulleady has said, she works with existing languages in order to invent a new one, and her staged tableaux are particularly resonant within the Art Institute galleries, as they rhyme with many works in the collection that have informed the artist.
persona3_adapted: As Mulleady has explained, she takes familiar visual languages—like the styles and symbols we already know from art and culture—and combines them to create something entirely new. Her carefully arranged scenes feel especially meaningful in the Art Institute galleries because they echo many of the artworks in the museum's collection that have inspired her.

CuratorialText: After decades as a representational painter, in her seventies Alma Thomas turned to abstraction, creating shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color with rhythmic dabs of paint that were often inspired by forms from nature. The artist had been fascinated with space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings often referenced America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Although she had never flown, Thomas began to paint as if she were in an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”
persona3_adapted:
Alma Thomas spent most of her life painting realistic scenes—people, places, and objects you could recognize. But in her seventies, she made a big change. She started painting in a completely different way, using small, rhythmic dabs of color to create bright, mosaic-like patterns that shimmered on the canvas. These patterns were often inspired by shapes she saw in nature.

Thomas was also fascinated by space travel. Since the late 1960s, she had been following America’s Apollo missions to the moon. Even though she had never flown herself, she began to paint as if she were looking down from an airplane. She tried to capture what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… You see only streaks of color.”

CuratorialText: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the open expanse and celestial patterns of a night sky, but despite its narrative title, the work could also be read as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of immersion within an otherwise flat picture plane. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse that this painting achieves, however, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly constructed stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the applied paint itself—almost as if the composition were backlit. Thomas relied on the enlivening properties of color throughout her late-blooming career. “Color is life,” she once proclaimed, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty. In the same year, she became the first African American woman to receive a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*Starry Night and the Astronauts* makes you think of the wide-open night sky and the patterns of stars. But even though the title suggests a story about the sky, you could also see this painting as a view of water from above. The artist plays with how we experience depth, making us feel like we're inside the scene, even though the painting itself is flat.

You don't just feel surrounded by the organic, flowing shapes in this work—you also get a sense of how the artist, Alma Thomas, created it. You can see that she built the surface one brushstroke at a time. The tiny gaps of bare canvas between each bright, primary-colored mark stand out almost as much as the paint itself. It's as if the painting is lit from behind.

Throughout her career, which took off later in life, Thomas relied on the lively power of color. She once said, "Color is life, and light is the mother of color."

This painting was made in 1972, when Thomas was eighty years old. That same year, she made history by becoming the first African American woman to have a solo exhibition at a major art museum—the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.

CuratorialText: Perhaps the most famous depiction of a bedroom in Western art history, this vibrant painting documents Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh conceived in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and broken brushwork, and sharply receding lines, the picture might suggest a nervous energy. But the artist understood it as a calming and restful image.
persona3_adapted:
This painting is one of the most famous bedrooms in Western art. It shows Vincent van Gogh’s own sleeping area in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. Van Gogh actually made three versions of this scene. He painted the first one in October 1888, just a month after moving into the house.

In a letter to his brother Theo, Van Gogh described the colors he used: pale lilac walls, a faded red floor, chrome yellow chairs and bed, very pale lemon green pillows and sheets, a blood-red bedspread, an orange dressing table, a blue washbasin, and a green window.

The painting uses bold colors, thick and uneven brushstrokes, and lines that seem to tilt sharply. This might make the room feel restless or full of energy. But Van Gogh himself saw it as a calm and restful scene.

CuratorialText: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of the scene, made nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. He produced a third, smaller version at the same time as a gift for his mother and sister.
persona3_adapted: Van Gogh painted this scene twice. This version, which is in the Art Institute’s collection, was made in September 1889—almost a year after the first one. Around the same time, he also created a third, smaller version as a gift for his mother and sister.

CuratorialText: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India, which was, as a result of settlers from Srivijaya (Indonesia), one of the few places where Buddhism was still flourishing in the twelfth century. The Buddha—with his elongated earlobes, the wheel marks on his palms, the urna between his brows, and the cranial protuberance covered with snail-shell curls—is seated in the posture of meditation, with his hands resting on his lap (dhyanamudra), wearing a seemingly diaphanous monastic garment. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame emerges out of the Buddha’s cranial protuberance, probably signifying wisdom. This monumental granite sculpture originally would have graced a monastic site at Nagapattinam, which is also well known for its Buddhist bronzes. The Tamil inscription covering its back is no longer legible.
persona3_adapted:
This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India. Because of settlers from Srivijaya (in modern-day Indonesia), this was one of the few places where Buddhism was still thriving in the 1100s.

The Buddha has several distinctive features: long earlobes, wheel-shaped marks on his palms, a small dot between his eyebrows (called an *urna*), and a bump on his head covered with curls that look like snail shells. He sits in a meditation pose, with his hands resting in his lap. He wears a robe that appears almost see-through, like a thin cloth.

Like other statues from Nagapattinam, a flame rises from the bump on his head, probably representing wisdom. This large granite sculpture originally stood at a monastery in Nagapattinam, a place also famous for its Buddhist bronze statues. A Tamil inscription on its back is now too worn to read.

CuratorialText: Many Mansions is the first in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale paintings depicting public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles such as Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in Many Mansions, Stateway Gardens. Struck by the absurdity of the term “garden” to describe these failed solutions to low-income housing, Marshall was inspired to represent the profound contradictions of living in such an environment. Many Mansions is filled with ironic and startling juxtapositions of the real and artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting, ebony-skinned figures dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title of the painting, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on Christ’s oft-quoted remark found in John [14:2]: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”
persona3_adapted: Many Mansions is the first of five large paintings by Kerry James Marshall that show public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles—places like Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, and, in this painting, Stateway Gardens. Marshall thought it was strange and even ridiculous to call these failed attempts at low-income housing “gardens.” That idea pushed him to capture the deep contradictions of living in such a place. The painting is full of surprising and ironic contrasts between what’s real and what’s fake—from the oddly cheerful landscape to the three dark-skinned figures dressed in old-fashioned Sunday clothes, who seem haunting and out of place. The title, written on the red ribbon above, comes from a famous line in the Bible (John 14:2): “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”

CuratorialText: In Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye intensely studies this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. Featuring people of every age and social class on the banks of the River Seine, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884 has captivated visitors ever since its arrival at the museum in 1924.
persona3_adapted: In *Ferris Bueller’s Day Off*, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye stares closely at this huge painting—almost ten feet wide—during a scene at the Art Institute. The artwork shows people of all ages and social classes relaxing on the banks of the River Seine. Ever since it arrived at the museum in 1924, *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884* has fascinated visitors.

CuratorialText: If we, like Cameron, come closer to the painting, figures and forms dissolve into dots and dashes of complementary colors laid side by side, characteristic of Seurat’s pointillist technique. Many smaller painted and drawn sketches and several larger canvases, in which Seurat laid out the parameters for the landscape and figures, led up to this majestically composed scene. Seurat returned to the work two years after its start date, amplifying the silhouettes of some figures and adding others. Some of these, like the monkey on the leash, seem so integral to the final composition that it is hard to imagine them as add-ons, but others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the furthest distance, are barely noticeable. To the artist, however, every decision was essential to his aim of making a painting of modern life equivalent to a classical Greek frieze.
persona3_adapted: When you get close to this painting—just like the artist Cameron did—the people and shapes break apart into tiny dots and dashes of color placed next to each other. This is a signature technique of Seurat’s called pointillism. Before creating this grand, carefully arranged scene, Seurat made many smaller sketches and paintings to plan out the landscape and the figures. He came back to the work two years after starting it, making some figures darker and adding new ones. Some of these additions, like the monkey on a leash, feel so essential to the final picture that it’s hard to believe they weren’t there from the start. Others, like the man carrying a rolled-up newspaper far in the background, are so small you might not even notice them. But for Seurat, every detail mattered. His goal was to create a painting of modern life that felt as timeless and important as a classical Greek frieze.

CuratorialText: Laboring at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and Nahua, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With expertise and dexterity, Jiménez threads weft through warp, slowly building her intricately patterned textile, the completed portion resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who came to prominence in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures, distinct from the legacies of Spanish colonialism. By centering Jiménez in Weaving, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.
persona3_adapted:
Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and a member of the Nahua people—one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica—is shown here working at a backstrap loom. With skill and steady hands, she guides the thread through the loom, slowly building a detailed pattern. The finished part of the fabric rests on her lap.

Diego Rivera was a leading modern painter who became famous in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20). He promoted a vision of Mexican identity that was rooted in Indigenous and folk traditions, separate from the influence of Spanish colonialism. By placing Jiménez at the center of this painting, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.

CuratorialText: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest, most ambitious work and has been a visitor favorite since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964, well before the artist’s contributions to the Impressionist movement were widely recognized. Caillebotte captured a busy intersection of Paris that was only a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely depict areas beyond his familiar neighborhoods.
persona3_adapted: This huge painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s biggest and most important work. It has been a favorite with visitors ever since the Art Institute bought it in 1964, long before people widely recognized Caillebotte’s role in the Impressionist movement. The scene shows a busy Paris intersection just a short walk from where he lived. In fact, most of his paintings of Paris focus on the neighborhoods he knew well.

CuratorialText: Caillebotte demonstrated his affinity with the Impressionists through his subject of modern urban life but approached these settings with a more Realist technique. The dramatic cropping of the scene may have been inspired by the newly popularized medium of photography; the two central figures seem to be walking straight into the viewer as something captures their attention outside of the frame. At the time, artists rarely attempted to depict people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each of the background figures to evoke the feeling of bustling city streets. When he started exhibiting with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on portraying the atmosphere of his scenes; here, instead of explicitly painting raindrops to indicate the weather, he suggests it through the incorporation of newly available retractable umbrellas and the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.
persona3_adapted: Caillebotte shared the Impressionists' interest in painting modern city life, but he painted it in a more realistic, detailed way. The scene is cut off dramatically at the edges, which may have been inspired by the new invention of photography. The two people in the middle seem to be walking right toward us, as if something just outside the picture has caught their eye. At the time, artists rarely tried to show people in motion. Caillebotte carefully placed each person in the background to create the feeling of a busy, lively street. When he began showing his work with the Impressionists, he also focused on capturing the mood of a scene. Here, instead of painting raindrops directly, he suggests it's raining by including the newly popular retractable umbrellas and the soft reflections on the wet cobblestones.

CuratorialText: Richard Hunt created Hero Construction in 1958, one year after graduating from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is composed of found objects—old pipes, bits of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist discovered in junkyards and on the street. Using a torch like a paintbrush, Hunt welded these elements into a form that is abstract yet recognizable.
persona3_adapted: Richard Hunt made *Hero Construction* in 1958, just one year after finishing school at the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is built from things he found in junkyards and on the street—old pipes, scraps of metal, and car parts. Using a welding torch the way a painter uses a brush, Hunt joined these pieces together into a shape that is not realistic, but still feels familiar.

CuratorialText: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and Hero Construction can be related to other sculptures past and present, from ancient statues of Greek heroes to modern monuments of statesmen. The modest but monumental stance of the figure suggests a contemporary hero who maintains strength in the face of the uncertainties and dangers of the present age.
persona3_adapted: Hunt was deeply interested in mythology, and *Hero Construction* connects to sculptures from many time periods—both old and new. You can see links to ancient Greek statues of heroes, as well as modern monuments of political leaders. The figure stands in a way that is both humble and powerful, suggesting a modern-day hero who stays strong even when facing the unknowns and dangers of today's world.

CuratorialText: The Mexicas, or Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses glyphs to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, 1 Rabbit represents the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era 4 Movement appears in the center. 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) indicate the date July 15, 1503, when the ninth emperor Moctezuma Xocoyotzin was coronated. Just as these glyphs recurred throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.
persona3_adapted:
This monument was carved by the Mexica people—also known as the Aztec Empire—who ruled central Mexico from 1428 to 1521. It uses picture symbols, called glyphs, to tell the story of the Mexica universe up to the year 1503.

On the back of the monument, the symbol "1 Rabbit" marks the beginning of time. On the front, the four corners show the four previous eras of the world, while the center shows the current era, "4 Movement." The symbols "1 Crocodile" (at the top) and "11 Reed" (inside the square frame) together represent the date July 15, 1503. That was the day the ninth emperor, Moctezuma Xocoyotzin, was crowned.

These same symbols were once common throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan (modern-day Mexico City). Today, you can still find them in Chicago. For example, "4 Movement" appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” train stop and on manhole covers in the Pilsen neighborhood—an area that has long been home to Chicago’s large Mexican community.

CuratorialText: A palpable energy and sense of movement enliven Nightlife, Archibald Motley’s portrayal of a crowded cabaret in the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville in Chicago. With stylized figures, an array of diagonal lines, and heightened colors keyed to shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the exuberance of city dwellers out on the town. Motley created a network of gestures and glances among the people, drawing attention to the various social interactions that animate the scene. The composition is an exploration of artificial lighting. Motley was inspired, in part, to paint Nightlife after having seen Edward Hopper’s Nighthawks (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the prior year.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*Nightlife* is a lively painting by Archibald Motley that shows a crowded nightclub in Chicago’s Bronzeville neighborhood. The scene feels full of energy and motion. Motley used stylized figures, lots of diagonal lines, and bright colors—especially shades of magenta and violet—to capture the excitement of city people enjoying a night out.

The artist created a web of gestures and looks between the people in the painting, which highlights the different social interactions happening in the scene. The painting also explores how artificial light affects what we see. Motley was partly inspired to paint *Nightlife* after seeing Edward Hopper’s famous painting *Nighthawks*, which had been added to the Art Institute’s collection the year before.

CuratorialText: Although influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s, Joan Mitchell did not prioritize self-expression: her often exuberant abstractions were “about landscape, not about me,” she once explained. Mitchell painted large, light-filled canvases animated by loosely applied skeins of bright color—here infused with the energy of a large metropolis. The title suggests a relationship between the painting’s network of pigments and the nerves or arteries of an urban space. The sense of spontaneity conveyed in City Landscape, however, belies Mitchell’s methods. Unlike many of her contemporaries, who were dubbed “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and deliberately. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”
persona3_adapted:
Joan Mitchell was inspired by the Abstract Expressionist artists working in New York in the early 1950s. But unlike many of them, she wasn't focused on expressing her own feelings. Instead, she said her lively, colorful paintings were "about landscape, not about me."

Mitchell created large, bright canvases filled with loosely painted swirls of vivid color. In this painting, the energy of a big city seems to pulse through the work. The title, *City Landscape*, hints that the web of colors might represent the nerves or arteries of an urban space.

Even though the painting looks spontaneous—like it was made in a burst of energy—Mitchell actually worked very slowly and carefully. While other artists of her time were called "action painters" for their fast, physical style, Mitchell took a different approach. She once explained, "I paint a little. Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do."

CuratorialText: Around 1920 Georgia O’Keeffe painted a number of oils exploring, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In Blue and Green Music, O’Keeffe’s colors and forms simultaneously suggest the natural world and evoke the experience of sound. She was drawn to the theories of the Russian Expressionist painter Vasily Kandinsky, who, in his 1912 text Concerning the Spiritual in Art, argued that visual artists should emulate music in order to achieve pure expression free of literary references.
persona3_adapted: Around 1920, the American artist Georgia O’Keeffe created several paintings that explored a simple but powerful idea: that music could be turned into something you could see. In her painting *Blue and Green Music*, she uses colors and shapes that remind us of nature—like rolling hills or flowing water—while also making us feel the energy and rhythm of sound. O’Keeffe was inspired by the ideas of the Russian painter Vasily Kandinsky. In a book he wrote in 1912, Kandinsky said that visual artists should try to be more like musicians. He believed that music could express feelings directly, without needing to tell a story or describe something real, and that painters should aim for that same kind of pure, emotional expression.

CuratorialText: Henri Matisse considered Bathers by a River to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it facilitated the evolution of the artist’s style over the course of nearly a decade. Originally, the work was related to a 1909 commission by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral images, though Shchukin decided to purchase only two works, Dance II and Music (both State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).
persona3_adapted: Henri Matisse once said that *Bathers by a River* was one of the five most important works of his career. And he had a good reason for saying that: this painting helped his style change and grow over nearly ten years. At first, the work was part of a project from 1909. A Russian collector named Sergei Shchukin asked Matisse to create two large paintings for the staircase of his home in Moscow. Matisse suggested three peaceful, countryside scenes, but Shchukin chose to buy only two of them: *Dance II* and *Music* (both now in the State Hermitage Museum in Saint Petersburg).

CuratorialText: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas, the rejected third image, altering the idyllic scene and changing the pastel palette to reflect his new interest in Cubism. He reordered the composition, making the figures more columnar, with faceless, ovoid heads. Over the next years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical bands and turned the formerly blue river into a thick black vertical band. With its restricted palette and severely abstracted forms, Bathers by a River is far removed from Dance II and Music, which convey a graceful lyricism. The sobriety and hint of danger in Bathers by a River may in part reflect the artist’s concerns during the terrible, war-torn period during which he completed it.
persona3_adapted: Four years later, Matisse came back to this painting—the one that had been rejected as the third image in a series. He changed the peaceful scene and swapped the soft, pastel colors for a style that shows his growing interest in Cubism. He rearranged the composition, making the figures look more like columns, with blank, egg-shaped heads. Over the next few years, he turned the background into four vertical strips and changed the river from blue to a thick black vertical band. With its limited colors and heavily simplified shapes, *Bathers by a River* feels very different from *Dance II* and *Music*, which have a graceful, flowing beauty. The serious mood and slight sense of danger in *Bathers by a River* may partly come from the artist’s worries during the terrible war years when he finished it.

CuratorialText: One of four posts sculpted for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this evocative image of the Yoruba concept of a divine ruler is a masterwork of Olowe of Ise. The renowned artist’s dynamic style can be distinguished by the interrelationships between figures, their exaggerated proportions, and the use of open space. The sculptor’s diminutive portrayal of the king suggests his dependence on others, and his crown has been emphasized to evoke the spiritual power residing within it. The towering image of the king’s senior wife standing behind him underscores her superior status as a guardian of her husband’s interests, endowed with keen political understanding and supernatural knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his enthronement ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.
persona3_adapted:
This carved wooden post is one of four that once stood in the palace of the king (called the *ogoga*) of Ikere, a city in what is now Nigeria. It shows how the Yoruba people imagined a divine ruler. The artist, Olowe of Ise, was famous for his lively, energetic style. You can see it in the way the figures relate to each other, their oversized features, and the empty spaces carved around them.

The king himself is carved smaller than the others. This isn’t meant to make him look weak—instead, it suggests that he depends on the people around him. His crown is made larger and more detailed to show the spiritual power it holds.

The tall woman standing behind the king is his senior wife. She is shown as a powerful guardian who looks out for his interests. In Yoruba culture, a senior wife often had deep political understanding and spiritual knowledge. In fact, she would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his crowning ceremony.
