At the king’s feet are two smaller figures. One is one of his junior wives. The other is Eshu, the trickster god, shown playing a flute.

CuratorialText: About Nighthawks Edward Hopper recollected, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter opposite a server, each appear to be lost in thought and disengaged from one another. The composition is tightly organized and spare in details: there is no entrance to the establishment, no debris on the streets. Through harmonious geometric forms and the glow of the diner’s electric lighting, Hopper created a serene, beautiful, yet enigmatic scene. Although inspired by a restaurant Hopper had seen on Greenwich Avenue in New York, the painting is not a realistic transcription of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the figures, their relationships, and this imagined world.
persona3_adapted:
**About *Nighthawks***  
Edward Hopper once said, “Unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In this scene, we see an all-night diner. Three customers sit at the counter, facing a server. Each person seems lost in their own thoughts, disconnected from the others.  

The painting is simple and carefully arranged. There is no entrance to the diner, and the streets are empty. Through clean shapes and the warm glow of the diner’s lights, Hopper created a calm, beautiful, and slightly mysterious scene.  

Although Hopper was inspired by a real restaurant he saw on Greenwich Avenue in New York, this painting is not a realistic copy of that place. Instead, it invites us to wonder about the people, their stories, and the imaginary world he created.

CuratorialText: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central image of El Greco’s first major commission, an altarpiece for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting’s subject, the raising of the Virgin Mary’s body into heaven, relates to the function of the church as the funerary monument of a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva; the iconography of the entire commission focuses on death and salvation.
persona3_adapted: The Assumption of the Virgin was the main painting in El Greco’s first big job—an altarpiece for a church in Toledo, Spain. The painting shows the Virgin Mary being lifted up to heaven. This theme connects to the church’s purpose as a memorial for a rich noblewoman named Doña Maria de Silva. All the artwork in the church focuses on ideas of death and being saved.

CuratorialText: El Greco used broad, free brushwork, flickering hues, rich color harmonies, and bold figural arrangements to arouse devotional fervor in the viewer and impart a deep sense of faith. The composition is divided into two zones: the earthly sphere of the apostles and the celestial realm of the angels. El Greco masterfully compressed many figures into a tall and narrow format: In the earthly zone, the apostles’ faces and gestures express a range of emotions through their dynamic, agitated gestures and poses. They are so individually considered that it seems El Greco studied actual studio models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and direct in their focus toward the looming figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.
persona3_adapted: El Greco used bold, loose brushstrokes, shimmering colors, and dramatic arrangements of figures to stir strong religious feelings in the viewer and create a powerful sense of faith. The painting is split into two parts: the earthly world of the apostles below and the heavenly world of the angels above. El Greco skillfully packed many figures into a tall, narrow space. In the earthly section, the apostles’ faces and gestures show a wide range of emotions through their lively, restless poses. Each figure looks so carefully observed that it seems El Greco worked from real models in his studio. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm and graceful, with their attention focused directly on the towering figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.

CuratorialText: Katsushika Hokusai’s much celebrated series, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjûrokkei), was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This tour-de-force series established the popularity of landscape prints, which continues to this day. Perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s copious use of the newly affordable Berlin blue pigment, featured in many of the compositions in the color for the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the protagonist in each scene, viewed from afar or up close, during various weather conditions and seasons, and from all directions.
persona3_adapted: Katsushika Hokusai’s famous series, *Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji*, was started in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This impressive series helped make landscape prints popular, and they are still loved today. One of the most eye-catching things about the series is Hokusai’s frequent use of a bright blue color called Berlin blue, which had just become affordable. You can see it in many of the prints, especially in the sky and water. In every scene, Mount Fuji is the main subject—sometimes shown from far away, sometimes up close, in different weather and seasons, and from every direction.

CuratorialText: The most famous image from the set is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura), in which a diminutive Mount Fuji can be seen in the distance under the crest of a giant wave. The three impressions of Hokusai’s Great Wave in the Art Institute are all later impressions than the first state of the design.
persona3_adapted: The most famous picture from this series is the "Great Wave" (Kanagawa oki nami ura). In it, you can see a tiny Mount Fuji far in the distance, beneath the curl of a huge wave. The Art Institute has three copies of Hokusai's Great Wave, but all of them were printed later than the very first version of the design.

CuratorialText: Pablo Picasso made The Old Guitarist while working in Barcelona. In the paintings of his Blue Period (1901-04), the artist restricted himself to a cold, monochromatic blue palette, flattened forms, and emotional, psychological themes of human misery and alienation related to the work of such artists as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The elongated, angular figure of the blind musician also relates to Picasso’s interest in Spanish art and, in particular, the great 16th-century artist El Greco. The image reﬂects the twenty-two-year-old Picasso’s personal struggle and sympathy for the plight of the downtrodden; he knew what it was like to be poor, having been nearly penniless during all of 1902.
persona3_adapted:
Pablo Picasso painted *The Old Guitarist* while he was living in Barcelona. During what is known as his Blue Period (1901–04), he used mostly cold shades of blue in his work. He also simplified the shapes of his figures and focused on sad, lonely, and difficult emotions—themes of human suffering and feeling cut off from others. These ideas were similar to those in the work of artists like Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin.

The thin, stretched-out figure of the blind musician also shows Picasso’s interest in Spanish art, especially the work of a famous 16th-century painter named El Greco. The painting reflects what Picasso himself was going through at age twenty-two, as well as his sympathy for people who were struggling. He knew what it was like to be poor—during most of 1902, he had almost no money.

CuratorialText: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 20th century, beloved in particular for the hundreds of cover images he created for the Saturday Evening Post. The Dugout is the largest of several studies for one such cover, published on September 4, 1948. It was inspired by a springtime doubleheader that he attended during which the hometown Boston Braves swept the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created an image contrasting dejected Cubs players and elated Braves fans.
persona3_adapted:
Norman Rockwell was one of the most famous American artists of the 1900s. He is best known for the many cover pictures he made for a popular magazine called the *Saturday Evening Post*. 

*The Dugout* is the biggest version of several practice drawings he made for one of those covers. The cover was published on September 4, 1948. Rockwell got the idea for this picture after going to a baseball doubleheader (two games in one day) in the spring. That day, the home team, the Boston Braves, won both games against the visiting Chicago Cubs. In the painting, you can see the sad Cubs players on one side and the happy Braves fans on the other.

CuratorialText: Rockwell staged the entire scene, which enabled him to take photographs for later reference. He recruited several family members of Braves players to act as fans. In the dugout, he positioned (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The figure of the forlorn batboy, however, was not a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to don the Cubs uniform and pose.
persona3_adapted: Rockwell set up the whole scene so he could take photos to work from later. He asked family members of the Braves players to pretend to be fans in the stands. In the dugout, he placed (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. But the sad-looking batboy wasn’t actually a Cubs employee. Rockwell paid the Braves’ batboy, Frank McNulty, $5 to put on a Cubs uniform and pose for the picture.

CuratorialText: By the time the issue was published, the Cubs had fallen to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped bolster their growing reputation as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most recognized sports images of the 20th century.
persona3_adapted: By the time the magazine came out, the Cubs had dropped to the bottom of their league. Rockwell's cover helped build their image as the "loveable losers," and it went on to become one of the most famous sports pictures of the 1900s.

CuratorialText: The monumental stacks that Claude Monet depicted in his series Stacks of Wheat rose fifteen to twenty feet and stood just outside the artist’s farmhouse at Giverny. Through 1890 and 1891, he worked on this series both in the field, painting simultaneously at several easels, and in the studio, refining pictorial harmonies. In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these canvases next to each other in one small room in the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. An unprecedented critical and financial success, the exhibition marked a breakthrough in Monet’s career, as well as in the history of French art. In this view, and in nearly all of the autumn views in the series, the conical tops of the stacks break the horizon and push into the sky. But in most of the winter views, which constitute the core of the series, the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field, as if bedded down for the season. For Monet, the stack was a resonant symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this group with further series depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute has the largest group of Monet’s Stacks of Wheat in the world.
persona3_adapted:
The towering piles of wheat that Claude Monet painted in his series *Stacks of Wheat* were about fifteen to twenty feet tall and stood just outside his farmhouse in Giverny, France. Between 1890 and 1891, Monet worked on this series both outdoors, painting at several easels at once, and inside his studio, where he fine-tuned the colors and composition. In May 1891, he hung fifteen of these paintings side by side in a small room at the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. The exhibition was a huge success, both with critics and financially, and it marked a major turning point in Monet’s career and in French art history.

In this painting, and in nearly all the autumn scenes from the series, the cone-shaped tops of the wheat stacks break the horizon line and reach into the sky. But in most of the winter scenes—which form the heart of the series—the stacks appear wrapped by the curves of hills and fields, as if they are settling in for the season. For Monet, the wheat stack was a powerful symbol of food and survival. He followed this series with others featuring poplars, the front of Rouen Cathedral, and later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute of Chicago has the largest collection of Monet’s *Stacks of Wheat* in the world.

CuratorialText: In 1886 Vincent van Gogh left his native Holland and settled in Paris, where his beloved brother Theo was a dealer in paintings. Van Gogh was apparently inspired by the energy of the city and by his introduction to Impressionism, and he created at least twenty-four self-portraits during his two-year stay in the French capital.
persona3_adapted: In 1886, Vincent van Gogh left his home country of the Netherlands and moved to Paris. His brother Theo, whom he was very close to, worked there as an art dealer. Van Gogh was clearly energized by the city's lively atmosphere and by his first real exposure to Impressionism, a style of painting that focuses on capturing light and everyday scenes. During his two years in Paris, he painted at least twenty-four portraits of himself.

CuratorialText: This early example is modest in size and was painted on prepared artist’s board rather than canvas. Its densely dabbed brushwork, which became a hallmark of Van Gogh’s style, reﬂects the artist’s response to Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique in A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884. What was for Seurat a method based on the cool objectivity of science became in Van Gogh’s hands an intense emotional language. The surface of the painting dances with particles of color—intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this dazzling array of staccato dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.
persona3_adapted: This early painting is small and was done on a prepared artist’s board instead of canvas. The thick, dotted brushstrokes—a style that later became famous in Van Gogh’s work—show how he was inspired by Georges Seurat’s groundbreaking painting *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884*. Seurat used tiny dots of color based on scientific ideas about how the eye mixes colors. But Van Gogh turned that technique into a way to express strong feelings. The surface of the painting seems to dance with specks of bright green, blue, red, and orange. Standing out among all these lively dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of his stare. Van Gogh once wrote to his brother Theo, “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals. No matter how grand and impressive cathedrals are, a human soul—even that of a poor streetwalker—is more interesting to me.” After his time in Paris, Van Gogh moved to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. By the time he died in 1890, he had been actively making art for only five years.

CuratorialText: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very brief. From Paris, he traveled to the southern town of Arles for ﬁ ft een months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for just ﬁve years.
persona3_adapted: Van Gogh’s time as a painter was actually very short. After leaving Paris, he spent fifteen months in the small town of Arles in the south of France. By the time he died in 1890, he had been actively making art for only five years.

CuratorialText: In American Gothic, Grant Wood directly evoked images of an earlier generation by featuring a farmer and his daughter posed stiffly and dressed as if they were, as the artist put it, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside of their home, built in an 1880s style known as Carpenter Gothic. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When it was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, the painting became an instant sensation, its ambiguity prompting viewers to speculate about the figures and their story. Many understood the work to be a satirical comment on midwesterners out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the beginning of the Great Depression.
persona3_adapted:
In *American Gothic*, Grant Wood created a picture that reminds us of an earlier time. The painting shows a farmer and his daughter standing very straight and still, dressed in old-fashioned clothes. Wood himself said they looked like "tintypes from my old family album"—those stiff, old-fashioned photographs people used to take. The two stand outside their home, which is built in a style from the 1880s called Carpenter Gothic. Wood saw a similar farmhouse when he visited Eldon, Iowa.

When the painting was first shown at the Art Institute in 1930, it became an instant hit. People couldn't agree on what it meant, and they started guessing about who the figures were and what their story might be. Many thought the painting was making fun of midwesterners who seemed stuck in the past, not keeping up with a changing world. But Wood himself said he meant it as a positive image of traditional American country life. He wanted to offer a comforting, reassuring picture at the start of the Great Depression, when many people were struggling and needed hope.

CuratorialText: Little did Claude Monet know that the water garden he created three years after buying his Giverny, France, property in 1890 would become his primary inspiration over the next two and a half decades. These paintings, numbering around 250, mark Monet’s artistic journey from more straightforward depictions of the pond spanned by the wooden Japanese bridge to the monumental and near-abstract series on the water lily theme he made in preparation for his murals at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris between 1914 and 1926.
persona3_adapted: When Claude Monet bought his home in Giverny, France, in 1890, he had no idea that the water garden he built three years later would become his main source of inspiration for the next 25 years. Over that time, he painted about 250 pictures of it. These works show how Monet’s style changed—starting with clear, realistic views of the pond and its wooden Japanese bridge, and eventually moving toward the huge, almost abstract paintings of water lilies that he created between 1914 and 1926. Those later pieces were made as studies for the large murals now on display at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris.

CuratorialText: Water Lilies, one of a group of paintings on the subject made between 1903 and 1908, comes at the midpoint of Monet’s developing style and spatial experimentations. The nearly square format underscores his move away from painting the conventional zones of land, sky, and water to focus solely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies at bottom left and top right frame a watery path, while the water’s surface reflects trees and clouds. Although the dreamlike quality of floating forms might seem to be a natural development for the artist, he considered these works “an obsession,” a sentiment borne out by technical examinations on the Art Institute’s canvas, revealing many significant changes made to the painting in progress.
persona3_adapted: Monet painted *Water Lilies* as part of a series he worked on between 1903 and 1908. This painting marks a middle point in his changing style and his experiments with space. The canvas is almost square, which shows Monet moving away from traditional scenes with land, sky, and water. Instead, he focuses only on the water's surface. Groups of water lilies in the bottom left and top right create a kind of watery path, while the water reflects trees and clouds. Even though the floating, dreamlike shapes might seem like a natural step for the artist, Monet himself called these works "an obsession." Scientists who studied this painting at the Art Institute found that Monet made many big changes to it as he worked.

CuratorialText: Pierre-Auguste Renoir considered the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou (a village along the Seine River) the perfect spot to enjoy nature and good company away from the urban distractions of Paris. His painting Two Sisters (On the Terrace), which he exhibited at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is among the last of his paintings set there.
persona3_adapted: Pierre-Auguste Renoir thought the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou—a small village along the Seine River—was the ideal place to relax, enjoy nature, and spend time with friends, away from the busy city life of Paris. His painting *Two Sisters (On the Terrace)*, which he showed at the Impressionist exhibition in 1882, is one of the last works he painted at that location.

CuratorialText: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, Luncheon of the Boating Party (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For Two Sisters, however, despite the title of the painting, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. Her oversize headdress composed of a floral bouquet is echoed in the brightly colored skeins of yarn in the basket that she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork creating a porcelain-like surface that contrasts brilliantly with the diaphanous, impressionist backdrop of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the railing of the terrace.
persona3_adapted:
Here's the adapted version of the text, written for a native English speaker without specialized art knowledge:

---

Sometimes, as in his most famous painting on this subject, *Luncheon of the Boating Party* (1882), Renoir used his close friends as models. But for *Two Sisters*, even though the title suggests otherwise, he paid strangers to pose. The result is a calm and perfectly balanced scene of youth and nature.

The older girl wears a blue flannel boater’s outfit and a striking red hat. But it’s her younger companion who really draws your eye. The little girl’s oversized headdress is made of flowers, and that floral theme is repeated in the bright yarns in the basket she gently touches.

As was typical for Renoir, he painted the faces of his models with careful, controlled brushstrokes. This gives their skin a smooth, porcelain-like finish. That smoothness stands out beautifully against the soft, blurry background of the river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the terrace railing.

CuratorialText: Willem de Kooning was a central figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that espoused the painterly actions of the artist as a sign of his or her emotions. De Kooning completed Excavation in June 1950, just in time for it to be exhibited in the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale. His largest painting up to that date, the work exemplifies the Dutch-born innovator’s style, with its expressive brushwork and distinctive organization of space into sliding planes with open contours. According to the artist, the point of departure for the painting was an image of women working in a rice field in Bitter Rice, a 1949 Neorealist film by Italian director Giuseppe de Santis. The mobile structure of hooked calligraphic lines defines anatomical parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to dance across the painted surface, revealing the particular tension between abstraction and figuration that is inherent in de Kooning’s work. The original white pigment has yellowed over the years, diluting somewhat the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. Aptly titled, the painting reflects de Kooning’s technically masterful painting process: an intensive building up of the surface and scraping down of its paint layers, often for months, until the desired effect was achieved.
persona3_adapted:
Willem de Kooning was a key figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that focused on the artist's physical act of painting as a way to express their emotions. He finished *Excavation* in June 1950, just in time for it to be shown at the 25th Venice Biennale, a major international art exhibition. At that time, it was the largest painting he had ever made. The work shows his unique style, with bold, energetic brushwork and a way of organizing space into sliding, overlapping shapes with open edges.

According to de Kooning, the starting point for this painting was a scene from a 1949 Italian film called *Bitter Rice*, which showed women working in a rice field. The painting is filled with hooked, flowing lines that suggest body parts—like birds, fish, noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to move and dance across the canvas. This creates a special tension between abstract shapes and recognizable figures, a key feature of de Kooning's work.

Over time, the original white paint has yellowed, softening the bright flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that stand out in the composition. The title *Excavation* is fitting: it reflects de Kooning's intense, careful process of building up layers of paint and then scraping them away, often over many months, until he achieved the effect he wanted.

CuratorialText: White Crucifixion is the first in Marc Chagall’s series of compositions that feature Jesus as a Jewish martyr and dramatically call attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany at the hands of the National Socialist Party. Chagall stressed Jesus’s religious identity by depicting him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish garments. The surrounding images show the devastation of pogroms, violent attacks against Jewish communities often organized or sanctioned by local governments. Combining the Crucifixion with contemporary events, Chagall’s painting links the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe and implicitly compares the Nazis with Jesus’s tormentors.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*White Crucifixion* is the first painting in a series by Marc Chagall that shows Jesus as a Jewish victim. The artwork draws strong attention to the persecution and suffering of Jewish people in 1930s Germany under the Nazi regime. Chagall highlights Jesus’s Jewish identity by dressing him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish clothing. The scenes around the main image show the destruction caused by pogroms—violent attacks on Jewish communities that were often organized or approved by local governments. By combining the Crucifixion with current events, Chagall connects the suffering of Jesus with the suffering of Jewish people across Europe. He also subtly compares the Nazis to those who tormented Jesus.

CuratorialText: The Child’s Bath is a tender portrayal of familial closeness, a subject that Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her encircling embrace, and the child’s pudgy hand on her knee suggest an emotional bond between the two. Captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt set out to produce a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She then continued her investigation across media, culminating in this bold composition, with its dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and bright palette.
persona3_adapted:
The Child’s Bath shows a quiet, tender moment between a caregiver and a child—a theme Mary Cassatt returned to often in her work. The caregiver’s cheek gently touches the child’s shoulder, her arms wrap around the child, and the child’s chubby hand rests on her knee. These small details help us feel the close, loving bond between them.  

In 1890, Cassatt saw a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris and was fascinated by them. Inspired by their style, she created a series of color prints. She then explored this new approach in other types of art, leading to this bold painting. The image feels flat and decorative, with bright colors and strong patterns—a clear break from traditional Western painting.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona3_adapted: René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a time when he was trying to uncover “poetic secrets” through his art. Thinking about both philosophy and psychology, he set out to place two images side by side in a way that would make viewers imagine a third thing that wasn’t actually shown. In this painting, he brought together two very different objects—a train and a fireplace—in a scene that became one of his most famous works. Even though the fireplace belongs to a home and the train belongs to the industrial world, they are connected by smoke. Magritte knew that viewers would link the train’s billowing steam with the smoke that rises from a chimney, revealing the painting’s hidden, poetic meaning.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona3_adapted:
One of the most daring choices the Impressionists made was what they decided to paint. Instead of traditional subjects, they often showed scenes from modern, industrial Paris—things like iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train sheds.

For Claude Monet in the 1870s, the Saint-Lazare train station would have been a familiar and meaningful place. This station connected Paris to Normandy, where Monet had developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the starting point for many towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists often visited.

Monet finished eight of his twelve known paintings of the Saint-Lazare station in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877. He probably displayed them all together in the same room.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona3_adapted: Monet was drawn to the train station’s glass-and-iron roof, where he saw a mix of human-made and natural beauty. For example, he liked how the steam from the trains rose up inside the building, while sunlight streamed in through the large glass panels overhead. His paintings of this station started a habit that would become very important to him: painting the same subject over and over again to show how light and weather changed it over time. He did the same thing later with his famous paintings of haystacks. However, these train station scenes were also his last major attempt to paint city life. After this, Monet mostly focused on painting landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona3_adapted: Salvador Dalí is one of the most famous artists associated with Surrealism, a movement known for dreamlike and strange imagery. He created bizarre, unsettling scenes that feel like the world has been twisted upside down. What makes his work even more powerful is his incredible skill as a painter. In 1943, when the Art Institute bought his painting *Inventions of the Monsters*, Dalí wrote to congratulate the museum and explained the meaning behind the work.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona3_adapted:
This painting was made in the mountains near Vienna, just a few months before Austria was taken over by Nazi Germany in 1938. The artist believed that strange, monstrous creatures in art could predict the coming of war.

In the painting, different figures represent different kinds of monsters:
- The horse-women are like river monsters connected to motherhood.
- The flaming giraffe is a male monster that brings destruction.
- The cat-angel is a holy monster that represents a kind of perfect, unnatural love.
- The hourglass is a monster of time and death.
- The artist and his wife, Gala, together form a monster of human emotion.
- The small blue dog is the only one that is not a real monster.

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona3_adapted: Inventions of the Monsters feels dark and unsettling. The painting is full of hints of danger—from the threatening fire in the background to the mysterious figure in the front holding an hourglass and a butterfly. Both of these objects are symbols that remind us death is unavoidable. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. Since Dalí’s home region of Catalonia was caught up in the Spanish Civil War, the artist was likely deeply worried about a world without a safe place—a world that, in fact, had allowed such frightening things to be created.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.
persona3_adapted:
Shiva is one of the most important gods in Hinduism. Here, he is shown as the Lord of the Dance, a famous image in Indian art. His dance represents the cycle of life and death—everything in the universe moves to its rhythm. This idea is shown by the ring of fire around him, which is filled with the wild, snake-like strands of his hair.

One pair of his arms holds a flame (symbolizing destruction) and a small hand drum that beats the rhythm of life. His other arms make symbolic gestures: his raised right hand says “do not be afraid,” and his left hand points down toward his raised left foot, which means freedom from the ignorance that keeps us from understanding the deepest truths.

Shiva stands perfectly balanced, with his right foot pressing down on a small figure—the demon of darkness—crushing ignorance. In his tangled, flowing hair, you can also see a tiny figure of the river goddess Ganga. According to belief, when the great Ganges River fell from the Himalayas, Shiva stood beneath it so the water would break over his hair, splitting into the seven holy rivers of India.

This bronze statue is a classic example from the Chola period in southern India. Statues like this were carried in religious processions during ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona3_adapted: Albert Bierstadt is famous for his paintings of the American West, but early in his career, the ambitious artist also painted landscapes of New England—especially the White Mountains, like the one you see here. When this painting was first shown in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised his skillful use of “light and shade,” which made the image feel more real. Bierstadt’s love for the White Mountains matched a growing interest in the area as one of America’s top tourist destinations. Photos from the 1800s suggest that Bierstadt borrowed landscape features from the Flume, a popular spot in New Hampshire, to create this imaginary scene.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona3_adapted: In this large painting, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of different ages seem to be going about their daily lives in a bright, colorful outdoor setting. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting mixes real-life details with imagination to create a dreamlike scene whose story is not easy to explain. The painting’s intentionally shocking title adds to this effect, making us wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to us, the viewers.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona3_adapted: People have traveled for many different reasons throughout history—and in just as many different ways. Whether they walked, rode animals, joined caravans, sailed on ships, or used modern options like planes, trains, and cars, art helps show us both how and why we move around the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona3_adapted: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a merchant from Venice, Italy. He spent about seventeen years in China, from around 1274 to 1292. This was during the rule of Khubilai Khan, the Mongol emperor who founded the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies took over the Chinese capital city of Hangzhou without causing much damage. Sometime after that, Marco Polo visited Hangzhou and its famous West Lake.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona3_adapted: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Since ancient times, China's emperors built a system of roads and waterways that connected their vast empire and made it easier for people and goods to move around. Because of this connection to the emperor, pictures of everyday travelers on the empire's roads and rivers came to suggest a wise and good ruler, and to bring to mind a time of peace and prosperity. Instead of showing the difficulties or real details of any specific trip, these images of unnamed people traveling or working together capture some of the interesting sights and scenes a traveler might see along the way. At the same time, they promote the basic ideas of a harmonious society and a well-organized state.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona3_adapted: One of the oldest reasons people have traveled is religious faith. Almost every major religion—including Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism—has a tradition of pilgrimage: a journey made on purpose to a place that is spiritually important. For example, Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to follow the path of Jesus, or visited local sites connected to the lives or remains of saints. In Islam, making a pilgrimage (called the hajj) to Mecca is one of the main duties for all believers. Other holy places were also visited regularly. Over many centuries, hundreds of pilgrims from China, Japan, and Korea faced great dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Sometimes, pilgrims would bring gifts, known as votive offerings, as donations to the place they were visiting.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona3_adapted: Trade was another big reason people traveled, and it happened in many different ways. But the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road. This was a network of land routes that connected the powerful Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. Along the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled alongside Buddhist missionaries, government messengers, translators, craftspeople, entertainers, and other skilled newcomers. They all moved through a wealthy, cosmopolitan world where there was a strong demand for luxury goods from faraway places—things like silver and gold items, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona3_adapted: In the 1900s, traveling was an important way for countries to build relationships and share their cultures. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, joined a diplomatic trip to Asia in 1905. This trip was part of a goodwill mission led by William Howard Taft. The journey was captured in many photographs, and it also led to special gifts from foreign leaders. For example, the emperor of Japan gave Roosevelt presents as a sign of friendship.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona3_adapted: Explorers, archaeologists, and researchers have traveled the world for many years to study and discover new places. In the early 1900s, people from Europe and North America also started traveling more for fun. Curious about the history and culture of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to broaden their experiences by visiting those regions.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona3_adapted: Artists who traveled to faraway places played a key role in recording and sharing what they saw. By meeting other artists and experiencing new things on their trips, they helped spread different styles and ideas in art.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona3_adapted: We usually think of travel as a good thing—vacations, adventures, new experiences. But it’s also important to remember that travel has a darker side. Not everyone travels by choice. People are sometimes forced to leave their homes because of war, famine, disease, poverty, environmental disasters, or colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be risky. This was especially true in the past. Simply being on the road could mean getting hurt or sick. The weather was unpredictable and could damage ships, carts, or other ways of getting around. And there was always the unknown—you never knew what might happen when you were far from home. These dangers influenced the stories people told and the beliefs they held.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona3_adapted:
Objects have rich stories to tell, and they often travel far from where they were first made. They might be traded, given as gifts, or collected by different people over time. For conservators—the people who take care of and repair objects—an item’s journey is written into its physical condition. Scratches, dents, repairs, and other changes show how others have handled and shaped it over the years.

The work of conservation is about putting together these physical clues and figuring out where an object fits in history. It’s not just about understanding the past, but also about planning how to keep the object safe for the future. Anthropologists sometimes talk about objects as having “lives” or “biographies,” but for conservators, this idea feels natural. Every time they work on an object, they add another chapter to its story—both in how it looks and in its journey through time.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona3_adapted: Travelers brought along items they needed for the trip itself. The clothes and gear they used for covering long distances often made them stand out from local people. They also carried personal belongings and small accessories—like tiny books, drinking flasks, and good-luck charms—to help make the journey more comfortable.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona3_adapted: Travelers often brought back objects they had collected during their trips. For pilgrims, holy items served as a way to carry the blessings of their journey home with them. Images created while traveling—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—helped capture the experience so it could be remembered later. Souvenirs bought along the way gave travelers a physical object to help them reflect on their trip. As global travel became easier and more common, people started producing a huge number of photographs, drawings, postcards, keepsakes, and other items that recorded both scientific discoveries and personal memories from abroad. Perhaps the most familiar travel item from the last 150 years is the picture postcard. People collected them as souvenirs or mailed them to family and friends, adding extra value with a foreign stamp and postmark.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona3_adapted: Most of us think of travel as actually going from one place to another. But there's another kind of travel—sometimes called "armchair travel"—that happens in your mind. It can take you across time and cultures, and it's made possible by books and works of art. By carefully looking at a travel story, a map, a favorite souvenir, or pictures of distant places, your memory and imagination work together to take you on a mental journey. This can feel almost like the real experience of traveling.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona3_adapted: When you look at a piece of art, you might not immediately think about the environment. But in fact, the environment has shaped nearly everything about it—how it was created, how people have understood and valued it over time, and how it has been kept safe for us to see today. Explore the many ways art and the environment are connected, from pictures of nature to the reuse of ancient jade objects, and discover a fresh way of looking at our collections.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona3_adapted: Nature is something everyone can relate to, even though it looks different depending on where you live. That’s why you’ll find images of plants, animals, water, and the sky in art from all over the world. These natural themes have always inspired artists and given their work deeper meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona3_adapted: Landscape paintings do more than just show what a place looks like. They can also make us feel something. A picture of nature might remind us how beautiful and powerful the world is, bringing up feelings of calm, curiosity, amazement, or quiet thought.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.
persona3_adapted:
For centuries, from Japan's earliest poetry collection (written in the 700s) all the way to today, the beauty of Japan's landscapes has been a favorite subject in both literature and art. At first, landscapes weren't the main focus of paintings. Instead, they appeared as backgrounds in Buddhist art, in scenes showing the four seasons, and in paintings of famous places or sacred sites.

Around the 1200s, things started to change. This may have been because new styles of ink painting arrived from China and Korea. From then on, landscapes became a subject in their own right in Japanese painting.

When Japanese artists painted their own country's landscapes, they usually used bright, full colors. This helped them show the changing seasons, which are deeply connected to nature in Japanese poetry and art. For scenes of China—which most Japanese artists knew only from imported paintings and later from instruction books—they often chose ink painting instead. This style worked well for showing dramatic and varied Chinese landscapes.

Whether they used rich, deep colors or subtle shades of ink, Japanese artists always painted with a deep sense of understanding and appreciation. Their works capture nature's fleeting beauty and its spiritual meaning, showing just how closely connected they felt to the natural world.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona3_adapted: Step into misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches as seen through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the 1500s to today. As you explore, you’ll hear the gentle sounds of the shakuhachi (a traditional bamboo flute). This experience shows how nature has inspired Japanese art and music. Your guides are master musician Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona3_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, looking at landscape paintings was more than just a visual pleasure—it often carried deeper meanings. A scene might hint at the season, or it could express more complex ideas. For example, when a painting showed people in a remote, natural setting, it often referred to a key cultural and political idea called *eremitism*—the practice of withdrawing from society.

The scholar class, known as the *literati*, believed in the Confucian idea that serving the government was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was asked to serve a dynasty founded by foreign rulers who had taken power unfairly, a true gentleman would refuse to serve. Following the tradition of eremitism, he would instead become a mountain recluse, or at least act like a hermit or a simple fisherman, as a way of protesting the breakdown of moral order. Because of this, landscape images carried a powerful meaning as symbols of ethical withdrawal, making them deeply significant.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona3_adapted: Artists didn't just paint real places they had seen. They also used what they knew about nature to create imaginary landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona3_adapted:
To truly appreciate Chinese art, it helps to understand a special category called "scholar's rocks," also known as "spirit stones." These are naturally shaped and textured rocks—often made of limestone—that people collect to place in gardens or bring indoors to display in a study. They can be as large as twelve feet tall or small enough to fit in your hand.

For centuries, Chinese philosophy, religion, and poetry have focused special attention on mountains and rocks. A saying from the time of Confucius (551–479 BCE) goes, "The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains." Meanwhile, Daoists saw mountains as peaceful sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings, and they used minerals in potions meant to grant eternal life.

By placing these striking rocks in gardens and homes, people could turn an ordinary space into a kind of paradise—or at least into a place that encouraged a long, healthy life or the search for immortality.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona3_adapted: In Chinese tradition, educated people loved rocks because they saw them as real, physical examples of nature's creative and changing forces. Sometimes they even wrote or spoke about them with great emotion, calling them forever loyal "friends." Looking at these rocks could inspire an imaginary journey deep into the natural world. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all collected rocks. Painters often studied the rocks they owned, using them both as a source of inspiration and as a model for their paintings of landscapes.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona3_adapted:
From small, private courtyards to grand temples, tombs, and pleasure gardens, Asia has played a central role in shaping how people design and enjoy outdoor spaces. The earliest known garden—the Garden of Eden from the Bible—may have been located in West Asia, where the Tigris and Euphrates rivers meet in what is now Iraq. In fact, the word "paradise" comes from the ancient Persian term *pardis*, which referred to walled orchards and hunting parks. These enclosed gardens became a key feature of Persian architecture for centuries, often including small buildings called pavilions that offered shade and relief from the sun.

Written records tell us that the earliest gardens in China, from the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE), were enclosed hunting grounds reserved for royalty. Chinese gardens often used carefully placed rocks and pools to create small, miniature versions of the natural world. In Japan, gardens followed a more natural style, with rolling hills and calm ponds designed to highlight harmony between people and their environment. In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, one common garden layout relied on a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona3_adapted: Throughout history, art has shown us how people respond to changes in the world around them. Sometimes these changes happen because people intentionally adapt to or reshape their environment. The ways we build on, change, and picture nature—what experts call "cultural landscapes"—help shape how we understand the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona3_adapted: Art can show us how the environment has changed over time. For example, paintings or drawings of animals in places where those animals no longer live can tell us about animal movements or extinctions.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona3_adapted: Humans have always changed the places where they live, but the amount of change grew much bigger with the rise of industry. In the United States and Europe, factories and new ways to travel appeared, making it easier and faster to make goods and move around. Most of these machines ran on coal, and they released pollution into the air. Some artists, like Whistler, tried to show this pollution in their paintings.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona3_adapted: During the Meiji era (1868–1912), Japan began to adopt Western technology at a rapid pace. This led to a boom in visual records—paintings, prints, and photographs—that captured all the new changes. In just twenty years, the Japanese landscape was transformed by railroads, telegraph lines, gas streetlights, steamboats and steamships, and new kinds of bridges and buildings made with modern materials and designs.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona3_adapted: Artists today are looking more and more at how climate change affects nature and people's lives. Some want to make us more aware of the pressing need to tackle this issue. Others focus on the social and political problems tied to environmental damage. And still others imagine different futures, hoping to inspire optimism and action.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona3_adapted: The process of making art affects the environment, and the environment also affects how art is made. For example, stones and precious metals must be taken from the ground. Wood has to be cut down. Clay is dug up from the earth. Plants need to be collected. Many artworks also use materials that come from animals, like parchment (made from animal skin), feathers, horn, and ivory. Different ways of making art require burning fuels to melt, fire, or glaze materials. Getting these raw materials and turning them into art objects always has some effect on the environment. Sometimes, this has happened at the cost of using up natural resources or harming people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona3_adapted:
Recycling might seem like a modern idea, but people have been reusing materials to make art for a very long time. Sometimes this happened because materials were hard to find, and other times because an object was already considered valuable. 

For example, in ancient China, jade was a highly prized material. Instead of throwing away broken or old-fashioned jade items, people would turn them into new objects. This was especially common during the late Shang dynasty (around 1250–1050 BCE). 

Archaeologists working at the Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly because resources were scarce when demand was high, people took objects from the Stone Age (about 5000–1700 BCE) and turned them into decorated jewelry and other items that were more meaningful to Shang culture. 

The three pendants shown here were all made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a *cong*. By Shang times, this tube had lost its original religious importance. It's also interesting to note that the recycled *cong* used here came from different Stone Age cultures across northern and southern China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.
persona3_adapted:
The environment around us can have a big impact on how well artworks last. Things like temperature, humidity (how much moisture is in the air), light, and pollution can change the materials that art is made from. Over time, these factors can cause damage and make objects break down. On a larger scale, bigger environmental changes—like shifts in temperature and more extreme weather—can also put historic sites around the world at risk. 

Conservators are the people who work to protect the objects in a museum’s collection. They help keep these items safe for the future. You can read more about conservation and scientific research at the National Museum of Asian Art. 

The Smithsonian Institution sees climate change as one of the biggest threats of our time. To learn more about what the Smithsonian is doing to help the environment, check out their sustainability initiatives.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona3_adapted: Red is a color full of life, strong feelings, good luck, and celebration. It has a powerful energy that grabs our attention and makes us feel more alive. Whether it comes from shiny gems, reddish soil, insects, or minerals, people have found many ways to create and understand this color. Explore examples from the museum’s collections to see how red has been used and valued across different times and cultures.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona3_adapted: Several metals—like iron, mercury, and lead—are used to make colored pigments that have been popular throughout history.
