These pigments can be found in nature, dug up from the ground, or created by people. For example, iron-based pigments from the earth can come in different colors. Red iron earth pigments are easy to find or make, and they've been used in many things, such as pottery and paints.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona3_adapted: Mercury-based red pigments come from grinding up a mineral called cinnabar. People have also made artificial versions of this pigment for a long time, and they call it vermilion. Even though this deep red color is poisonous, it was used in burial rituals, added to lacquers, and used to decorate paintings and documents.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona3_adapted: While red lead can be found in nature as a mineral (called minimum), it was much more commonly made by people for use as a pigment. Usually, lead compounds were heated in a controlled way to create the desired color while reducing the chance of other colors forming.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona3_adapted: Red copper oxide, also known as cuprite, is a substance that forms on bronze objects over time. It happens when the copper in the bronze reacts with the air.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona3_adapted: Plants and animals can also give us reds for art. For example, the roots of the madder plant and the flowers of the safflower can be processed to extract their color. This color is then purified and used in things like fabric and prints. How the dye is made can change the final shade of red. Certain insects, like lac and cochineal, are another source. People would collect these insects, extract their red color, and turn it into a pigment or dye. When scientists can figure out which colorants were used in a piece of art, it can tell us about old trade routes. That's because these insects only live naturally in certain parts of the world.

CuratorialText:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Red also appeared in nature as gemstones—both the very valuable kind and the more common ones. Examples include rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Because these stones were rare and had such a striking color, they were highly prized. Wealthy and powerful people often wore them as jewelry or used them to decorate expensive objects. They also made meaningful gifts.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.
persona3_adapted:
In many old books and manuscripts, writers used red ink to highlight important parts of the text. This practice, called rubrication, became common in European manuscripts after the 700s. Red was also used in copies of the Qur’an made between the 700s and 1000s CE, where it helped show how to pronounce words. The red stood out clearly next to the black or brown ink used for the main writing.

In Chinese painting and calligraphy, red was often used for the paste that held the ink seals added to finished works. These seals, stamped in red, stood out against black ink writing and images. A seal might show the name of the artist or their studio, the person who owned the work, or other private individuals. Sometimes, a seal even included a favorite short saying.

CuratorialText: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were depicted in red due to its association with royalty and passage through the afterlife. In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.
persona3_adapted: Red has long been linked to positive ideas like love, passion, and importance. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were shown in red because the color was connected to royalty and the journey through the afterlife. In China, red is considered lucky. It is tied to life-giving energy—like the sun, blood, and fire—and is the color of celebrations and wealth.

CuratorialText: Red is still associated with festivities and happiness in Chinese culture today. Learn more about Lunar New Year, when families decorate windows with red paper cuttings and adorn doors with couplets expressing auspicious wishes. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made, from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
persona3_adapted: In Chinese culture today, the color red is still linked to celebrations and joy. Discover more about Lunar New Year, a time when families put up red paper cutouts on their windows and hang couplets—poems with good luck wishes—on their doors. Watch a video from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage to see how these paper cutouts are made.

CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.
persona3_adapted:
Red can also bring to mind more negative ideas, like war, anger, and destruction. Because it is the color of blood, red has a powerful, gut-level effect on us that is unlike any other color. 

Artist Chiharu Shiota is interested in the marks that people leave behind. In her 2015 artwork called “Over the Continents,” she collected nearly 400 used shoes. Each shoe came with a note from the person who donated it, telling a story about someone they had lost or a memory from the past. 

The shoes were wrapped in almost four miles of red yarn. The yarn was all connected to one point in the museum’s Sackler building. For the artist, this web of red threads represents the invisible but powerful connections that link people to each other and to the world around them.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.
persona3_adapted: Green is everywhere—in the plants we grow, the gems we treasure, and the colors we mix into paints. People have always wanted to bring green into the art they make, the places they live, and the buildings they create. This color makes us think of rich, growing fields, new life, success, and even good health. Explore how different cultures and traditions in the museum's collections have created and understood the color green.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
persona3_adapted: In the world of art, finding green dyes from plants is quite rare. However, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to create the look of shiny green emeralds. In especially valuable artworks, real emeralds were sometimes carved into one-of-a-kind objects or turned into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.
persona3_adapted: The green coating you see on copper and bronze objects forms as the metal reacts with its environment. This happens naturally when copper is exposed to salty or acidic conditions. For example, look at the ancient Chinese bronze vessel below. Its surface shows different colors caused by various chemical reactions. Most of it has a smooth, pale green layer from tin oxide mixed with copper compounds. You'll also notice scattered small patches of dull red, which come from a copper mineral called cuprite. And the bright, powdery green spots you see here and there are from a copper compound called copper chloride. You can explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.
persona3_adapted: Copper can rust to form a green substance, and people have learned how to turn that into a green paint. But more often, a naturally green stone called malachite is crushed into a powder to make paint. This type of green paint was commonly used in Chinese paintings.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a close-up image of finely ground malachite, a green mineral. It is provided courtesy of the Conservation and Scientific Research department at the National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution.

In ceramics, copper is a common ingredient used to create green glazes, often in combination with lead. To make green enamels, copper is also mixed with tiny yellow particles, such as lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
persona3_adapted:
Celadon is the name for a large group of ceramics that are fired at very high temperatures. Their shiny, glass-like coating—called a glaze—gets its color from a small amount of iron oxide. When these pieces are fired in a kiln with very little oxygen (known as a "reducing" atmosphere), the glaze turns into different shades of bluish green. This color is traditionally called "céladon," a French word.

The word "celadon" is thought to come from a 17th-century French play called *L’Astrée*. In the play, a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe. Some modern experts prefer to call these pieces "green ware," but the more poetic French name helps remind us that celadon ceramics can actually range in color from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona3_adapted:
Jade was the first truly luxurious material in ancient China. While the word "jade" is often used to describe several types of hard, beautiful stones, most jade from ancient China—from the Neolithic period through the early imperial era—has been scientifically identified as nephrite. This is different from the bright, apple-green jadeite that became popular much later.

Nephrite is made of tightly interlocked, fibrous crystals. It forms as pebbles and boulders and comes in many colors, depending on minerals like iron, chromium, or manganese that are mixed into the stone. It is incredibly hard—so hard that metal tools cannot carve or even scratch it. Instead, it had to be shaped by grinding it with quartz or other tough minerals.

Because nephrite was so rare and difficult to work with, ancient Chinese people valued it very highly. They reserved it for special uses, such as jewelry and objects used in ceremonies and rituals.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.
persona3_adapted: When Charles Lang Freer was sick near the end of his life, his close friend Agnes Meyer said he often held a piece of jade in his hand. He believed, almost like a religious faith, that the stone could comfort him and help him feel better. Freer collected many ancient Chinese jades, even though most other Western collectors at the time weren't interested in them. In the end, his collection had more than 500 pieces, covering a time span of more than 5,000 years.

CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona3_adapted: Green is everywhere in nature—it’s the color of forests, fields, and jungles. When we see green, we often think of life, growth, and renewal. In Southeast Asia, between the 800s and 1200s, building cities meant carving space out of the thick, tropical jungle. People had to clear the land, build canals and reservoirs for water, and carefully manage the landscape. But when cities were abandoned—because of changes in politics, religion, or the environment—the jungle quickly grew back, turning old cities back into wilderness. This constant cycle of building and regrowth inspired local artists. On temple walls and in sculptures, you can see plants and flowers with thick stems, twisting vines, and clusters of leaves that turn the surfaces into lush, living scenes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
persona3_adapted: In a garden scene, a group of angels—called *huris* or *paris* in Persian—are entertaining their queen. They play music, dance, and serve food and wine. The lively mood of the drawing is similar to other Persian images of outdoor parties, known as *bazm*, which were celebrations with wine and music. But unlike typical scenes from poems by Hafiz and Assar, which show princes, this artwork focuses on heavenly beings. That kind of subject was very popular in Iran and Turkey during the 1500s and 1600s.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.
persona3_adapted: This winged celestial creature, known as a *peri*, was a popular subject in 16th-century Ottoman and Persian art. The delicate drawing shows a style typical of Ottoman artists, who combined flowing, calligraphy-like lines with soft, feathery strokes. You can see this in the tiny patterns on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color give the heavenly being a sense of shape and texture.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The inset rim of each of the three compartments fits into the base of the compartment above it or into the lid. The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.
persona3_adapted:
This small container, called an *inro*, is shaped like a flattened rectangle with rounded corners—a form likely inspired by a type of old Japanese coin called a *ryo*. It has three compartments that stack on top of each other. Each compartment fits snugly into the one below it, with the lid sitting on top.

A blue-green silk cord holds all the pieces together. The cord passes through two holes in each part, is tied in a bow underneath, and is secured above by a small bead (called an *ojime*). At the very end of the cord is a carved toggle (called a *netsuke*), which helps keep the whole thing from slipping off the wearer's belt.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.
persona3_adapted: This small, round box has a lid that fits perfectly. It is made of hard, grayish clay. The surface is covered in a pale greenish-gray glaze with a hint of lilac. The decoration is set into the clay beneath the glaze, using white and black materials. The main design shows two cranes in flight inside a circular frame, surrounded by repeating geometric patterns.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.
persona3_adapted:
This cup has straight sides that gently curve inward near the bottom, which is flat. The walls are very thin—so thin that this type of pottery is sometimes called "egg-shell ware." The cup is a pinkish-beige color, and the inside is left unpainted.

On the outside, the decoration is painted in brownish-black and red on the unpainted surface. The same design is repeated seven times around the cup: a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black, tied with ribbons of the same dark color. The decoration runs from the rim of the cup down to about the middle.

Inside the cup, there are traces of a black, powdery substance.

CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
persona3_adapted: This is one of several paintings of storefronts that Whistler made while he was in St. Ives. When he showed it in London in 1884, some supportive critics gave it special praise. One critic said, "This is probably the most perfect small work of its kind ever created by an artist who has learned how to see…. It has no small details, no obvious effort, and no dramatic story; but its colors are beautiful, its shades of light and dark are flawless, and its thoughtful mystery is at least interesting because it makes you wonder."

CuratorialText:
Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: buff-colored. Glaze: transparent over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock amidst flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the exterior.
Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.
persona3_adapted: This plate has a flat rim and a peacock painted in the center. It is made from a light brown clay. The surface is covered with a clear glaze, and the colors—blue, green, red, and black—were painted on top of a white base. In the middle, a peacock stands among flowers. Around the rim, there is a repeating pattern of curved, leaf-like shapes. On the outside of the plate, there are eight flower blossoms. A small label on the piece shows Greek letters and the number 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
persona3_adapted:
This small container was made from molded Yue-ware, a type of early Chinese pottery. Many similar pieces were produced, but we aren't sure exactly how they were used. It might have held a candle, or scholars may have used it as a water pot to wet their writing brushes.

During the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), people used images of mythical creatures for protection. These included lions with large, curly manes and winged monsters called chimeras. Huge stone versions of these creatures were placed outside important tombs to guard them.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.
persona3_adapted: A pair of blue and white porcelain vases, shaped like tall drinking cups. They are decorated with hand-painted feathers, curling leaf patterns, and scattered flowers. On the bottom of each vase, there is a small mark shaped like a leaf or a seashell. These vases were originally part of a five-piece set that also included three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
persona3_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the Tokaido, a major road in Japan that connected the city of Edo (now Tokyo) with Kyoto. Edo was the seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, a military government, while Kyoto was the capital and home of the emperor.

This print is part of a series called *Shokoku taki meguri* (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces). In the image, the Kiyotaki Waterfall flows down a steep, rocky cliff in small, branching streams. In the foreground, pilgrims are walking up from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist figure known for compassion. The scene feels remote and high up, which is emphasized by the bands of clouds in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.
persona3_adapted: This small dish has a pleated, or fluted, rim that gives it a delicate, scalloped edge. It is made from buff-colored clay and covered in a transparent glaze with a light olive-green tint, known as Ofuke glaze. The glaze has developed large, irregular cracks that have been stained brown over time, and it appears a deeper green in thicker areas. The inside of the foot—the small ring at the base—is glazed, while the glaze has been wiped away from the outside of the foot and the surrounding base. The dish’s fluted rim gently echoes the shape of traditional Chinese celadon dishes from the Longquan kilns.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
persona3_adapted:
**The Khamsa: A Persian Masterpiece**

The *Khamsa* (which means "five" in Arabic) is a collection of five long poems by the Persian writer Nizami. It is considered one of the greatest works of Persian literature. One of these poems tells the love story of a Persian king named Khusraw Parviz and an Armenian princess named Shirin. The copy of the *Khamsa* on display here was made in the early 1400s. It is one of the earliest known versions of this work that includes painted illustrations.

**A Tense Meeting at the Castle**

The painting you see here, *Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle*, is one of the most emotionally powerful manuscript paintings from the 1400s. In the story, Shirin is angry with Khosraw because he married another woman. So she has shut herself away in her castle. After his wife dies, Khosraw tries again and again to see Shirin. Finally, she agrees to meet him.

In the painting, the king stands on expensive carpets and cloth, protected by a royal umbrella. He gestures humbly, begging for forgiveness, while Shirin looks down at him from a castle window. 

**A Surprising Setting**

The poem says that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter. But the artist chose to show the scene in a beautiful, green garden, softly lit by a fading moon. This makes the moment feel more romantic and hopeful.

**A Familiar Face**

You might notice that the king in this painting has a thin mustache and a very elegant look. That's because he closely resembles a real historical figure: Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434), a prince and art lover from the Timurid dynasty. It's likely that the artist used the prince's face as a model for the king.

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
persona3_adapted:
In this painting, we’re looking inside the London home of Whistler’s brother-in-law, Seymour Haden. The piece was first called *The Morning Call*.  

On the left, you can see a woman reflected in the mirror—that’s Whistler’s half-sister, Deborah Haden. The young girl sitting down is his niece, Annie Haden. The woman standing is Miss Isabella Boott, who was related to the Haden family through marriage.  

Rather than trying to make the scene look deeply three-dimensional, Whistler used a bold color scheme and a sharp, tilted angle. This approach makes the painting feel flatter and more like a pattern on a surface, instead of a window into a real room.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.
persona3_adapted: In traditional stories and colorful pictures, the Jain community shared their beliefs and values. One such story is the *Shripalarasa*, which follows the exciting adventures of Prince Shripala, a Jain prince who travels far away to find trade and riches. In this unusually large and lively painting, the artist shows the prince’s ship as a British vessel. Words are written all over the painting, repeatedly naming parts of the ship. This shows a strong interest in sailing and the sea. The British flag, the Union Jack, is also shown, which makes the story feel more immediate and real—even though the story takes place long before the British arrived in India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This tall, slender vase was made for a funeral. It sits on a hollow, flared base and comes with a wooden stand. The clay is soft, light, and a gray-white color. The glaze is a thin, see-through green (known as celadon), with a fine brown crackle pattern across the surface. Some areas are stained, and parts of the glaze look wrinkled from being over-baked. The decoration shows formal, stiff figures and a dragon holding up a sun disk. These designs were pressed onto the vase in raised relief and then covered with glaze.

CuratorialText: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility. Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona3_adapted:
The story of Shuten Doji has been a favorite in Japan for a very long time. The earliest known illustrated version of this tale dates back to the 1300s. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021) was a real-life warrior known for his great fighting skills. Over time, popular stories turned him into a legendary hero who saved the land from evil monsters.

Shuten Doji was a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto. He terrorized the local people, eating them and keeping young women as prisoners. With help from the gods and by using clever tricks, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre's head and brought peace back to the region.

The artist Kyosai was clearly interested in telling stories through his art. He also studied old artworks very carefully, and you can see this in both his paintings and his sketchbooks. His own works are full of dark humor and strange, ugly images. These kinds of pictures were exactly what people liked to see in the late Edo period (1615-1868) and the early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, completing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but recalls a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, emphasizing the geometrical patterns created by the play of variously colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light splayed across the near foreground.
persona3_adapted: During a trip to Oregon in the summer of 1908, the artist Childe Hassam painted this scene of Portland's Chinatown—but he only finished it after returning to New York. The subject is unusual for him, but it echoes a series of storefront paintings by James McNeill Whistler from the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam turned the street into a shallow, stage-like space. He focused on the geometric patterns created by the mix of colorful rectangular shapes: bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and a white diagonal of broken light spreading across the front of the scene.

CuratorialText: Tall, blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with long neck, round bulbous body and "garlic"-shaped neck/mouth. Round ring base, slightly uneven. The body decorated with scrolling, flowering vines and animals.
persona3_adapted: This tall vase is made of porcelain and glazed in blue and white. It has a long neck, a round, bulbous body, and a mouth shaped like a garlic bulb. The base is a round ring, slightly uneven. The body is decorated with twisting vines, flowers, and animals.

CuratorialText: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which employ powders and particles of gold and other material to create intricate designs.
persona3_adapted:
This lacquer box holds an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. Its lid is decorated with scenes from Japanese legends and theater.

One figure is the Wisteria Maiden, a character from a type of folk painting called Otsu-e, which was sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. Her silver face has turned a bluish-black over time due to a chemical reaction. In 19th-century kabuki theater, the Wisteria Maiden became the subject of a dance about unrequited love, performed to a type of music called nagauta (which means "long song").

Next to her is a praying demon, another common figure in Otsu-e paintings. Above them, the Thunder God rushes down from the sky, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm.

The detailed decoration on this box was made by an artist who mastered a technique called maki-e. This method uses gold powder and other materials to create intricate designs.

CuratorialText: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
persona3_adapted: This bowl has a cone-like shape that widens toward the top. It sits on a tall, deep base, and you can see four small marks on the underside where it was held during firing. The rim has six small notches cut into it. The clay is dense and makes a ringing sound when tapped. The glaze is a gray-green celadon, with long, fine cracks running across the surface. Under the glaze, the decoration was pressed and carved into the clay: a rosette pattern in the center, with hibiscus flowers and leaves covering the sides. A single incised line runs just below the rim.

CuratorialText: Depicts a hilly landscape, with a scraggly tree, and a Central Asian groom in a red jacket holding the bridle of a dappled horse with trappings and a saddle. Spurious inscription and one seal, no colophons or labels.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a hilly landscape with a thin, uneven tree. In the scene, a groom from Central Asia, wearing a red jacket, holds the reins of a spotted horse. The horse is decorated with a saddle and other gear. There is a fake signature and one seal on the artwork, but no written notes or labels.

CuratorialText: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
persona3_adapted: This tile is rectangular and off-white. It has a white base layer underneath the glaze. On top of that, the artist painted a bird sitting on a branch of a fruit tree. The colors used are yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and a dark purple-black for the outlines. The sides and back of the tile are not glazed.

CuratorialText: Painting is one of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. This view of a racetrack focuses on the action in the foreground, where spectators are enthralled by the competition. Contained in modern wooden box with lid.
persona3_adapted: This painting is part of a set of eight scenes of Tokyo by the artist Ogata Gekko. It shows a racetrack, with the main action happening up close. In the foreground, the crowd is completely caught up in the excitement of the race. The artwork is stored in a modern wooden box with a lid.

CuratorialText: Folio from a dispersed copy of Yusuf u Zulaykha by Jami; Persian in black nasta'liq script on gold-flecked paper; heading in red; recto: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; verso: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; one of a group of 13. Border: The recto is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with birds, floral, and leaf scrolls. The verso is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits,and birds in a landscape.
persona3_adapted:
This page comes from a rare, hand-copied book of the poem *Yusuf u Zulaykha*, written by the Persian poet Jami. The text is written in black ink in a flowing Persian script called *nasta'liq*, on paper decorated with tiny flecks of gold. The heading is in red ink.

Each side of the page has two columns of text, with 14 lines per column. This is one of 13 pages from the same book that are kept together.

The front of the page has a border made of gold, green, and black lines. Outside the border, the margin is decorated with birds, flowers, and curling leaves.

The back of the page also has a border of gold, green, and black lines. Its margin shows a landscape filled with animals: lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds.

CuratorialText: Squared, hollow cylinder of the type ts’ung [cong] 琮; narrow projecting collar at both ends; rich cream and tans; decoration: incised and in low relief, stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on corners. (Collar chipped.)
persona3_adapted: This object is a hollow, square-shaped cylinder with a narrow, raised rim at both the top and bottom. It is made of a material that has a rich cream and tan color. The surface is decorated with carved designs, including stylized masks and double bands of fine lines along the corners. These designs are both incised (cut into the surface) and in low relief (slightly raised). Please note that the rim has a small chip.

CuratorialText: One of a pair of blue and white vases, Kangxi period, each of trumpet shape form with flared foot, after a bronze Ku, each of the three sections with large loose lotus scroll, the central lobed section encircled by a diaper filled band.
persona3_adapted: These are one of a pair of blue and white vases from the Kangxi period. Each vase is shaped like a trumpet, with a wide, flared base. The design is inspired by an ancient bronze vessel called a *gu*. The vase is divided into three sections, each decorated with large, flowing lotus flowers. The middle section has a band filled with a repeating diamond pattern.

CuratorialText: Pitcher, of a full-bodied pear form, on a high spreading foot; plaster restorations including handle and foot. Clay: soft, sandy, white. Glaze: turquoise green, partly disintegrated and having a silver iridescence.
persona3_adapted: This pitcher has a rounded, pear-like shape and sits on a tall, flared base. The handle and base have been repaired with plaster. The clay is soft, sandy, and white. The glaze is a turquoise green, but parts of it have worn away, giving the surface a silvery, shimmering look.

CuratorialText: Bowl, deep ovoidal; low foot with kiln-slag attachments. Clay: hard. Glaze: brilliant olive-gray, finely crackled. Decoration: molded in the paste in light relief, under glaze; single large floral motif (peony?) filling bottom, lower half of walls, difficult to decipher under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This bowl has a deep, egg-like shape and a short base with some rough bits left over from the firing process. It's made of hard clay and covered in a shiny, olive-gray glaze with fine cracks running through it. The decoration is molded right into the clay itself, creating a subtle raised pattern beneath the glaze. A single large flower—possibly a peony—fills the bottom and lower sides of the bowl, though it's hard to make out clearly under the glaze.

CuratorialText: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.
persona3_adapted: This bowl is made from a dense, light brown type of clay called stoneware. It has a clear, crackled glaze on top of a white base layer, except for one area that was left unglazed and stained a dark brownish-black. The decoration is painted in blue under the glaze, with simple line patterns that look like maple leaves and water or pampas grass. There are also geometric bands around the sides of the lid and the base. A small square seal is stamped on the underside of the lid.

CuratorialText: Tall blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with bulbous lower body, cylindrical center body with pinched "snake" handles and slightly outflaring rim. Porcelain body with dark blue cobalt underglaze with gray-blue-white glaze. Floral decorative patterns.
persona3_adapted: This vase is made of porcelain, a type of fine ceramic. It is tall and decorated in shades of blue and white. The lower part of the vase is round and bulbous, while the middle section is straight and cylinder-shaped. On each side, there are handles that look like they have been pinched, often called "snake" handles. The top of the vase flares out slightly. The surface has a shiny, grayish-blue-white glaze, and the dark blue patterns were painted onto the clay before the glaze was applied. The decoration features floral designs.

CuratorialText: Dish, low, with rounded sides; one chip on rim; hair-line crack. Clay: white porcelain. Glaze: clear, feldspathic; a few pinholes and impurity flecks. Decoration: painted in underglaze cobalt blue of deep tone, copying a traditional design of the Ch'eng-hua period. See F1951.10 and .11. Center inside, "three friends of winter" (pine, plum, and bamboo) with garden rock, and fungus, enclosed by double line band which is repeated at rim. Outside, following model, a scene of figures on a garden terrace with plants and trees.
persona3_adapted:
This shallow dish has rounded sides and a small chip on the rim, along with a hair-thin crack. It is made of white porcelain with a clear, glass-like coating called a glaze. The glaze has a few tiny holes and small specks of impurities.

The decoration is painted in a deep blue color applied under the glaze, copying a traditional design from the Ch'eng-hua period (a famous era in Chinese ceramics). In the center of the dish, you'll see the "three friends of winter"—pine, plum, and bamboo—along with a garden rock and a type of fungus. This design is enclosed by a double line, which is repeated around the rim.

On the outside, following the original model, there is a scene of figures on a garden terrace, surrounded by plants and trees.

CuratorialText: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Black lacquer rim. Red lacquer repairs. Clay: hard, light-fawn. Shiny brown on surface. Glaze: yellow and gray over blue-green; inside--brilliant brown and blue. Outside: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Inside: clear glaze.
persona3_adapted: This small bowl was used for serving individual portions of food. It has a black rim and visible red lacquer repairs, showing it was valued and mended over time. The bowl is made of a hard, light tan clay. Its surface is a shiny brown. The glaze—a glass-like coating—is yellow and gray over a blue-green base. Inside, the colors are a brilliant brown and blue. The outside glaze was made by mixing iron and rice-straw ash, while the inside has a clear glaze.

CuratorialText: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration of grasses incised under clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (body indented in each area); and flower and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. Kiln mark stamped on base inside foot rim. Thick iron wash over half, appearing dull yellowish brown; clear glaze on other half of body, appearing gray where it partially overlaps wash. Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.
persona3_adapted:
This pot is made from white clay, which looks pinkish-buff in spots where it's been exposed. The decoration includes grass patterns scratched into the surface under a clear brown wash, and fine lines (hatching) carved under a clear glaze—the clay is slightly pressed inward in these areas. Flowers and grass are also painted on top of the glaze in black.

On the base, inside the foot rim, there's a kiln mark stamped into the clay. Half of the pot is covered with a thick iron wash that looks dull yellowish-brown, while the other half has a clear glaze that appears gray where it partly overlaps the iron wash. On the foot rim, you can see five small marks left by clay supports used during firing.

CuratorialText: Bowl, small, ovoidal, on a bold, spreading foot. Cracked and repaired. Clay: soft, grayish-white. Glaze: inside, a deep aubergine; outside now covered with an iridescent patina. Decoration: painted in black under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This small, egg-shaped bowl sits on a wide, flared base. It has been cracked and repaired. The bowl is made of soft, grayish-white clay. The inside is glazed a deep purple color. The outside is now covered with a shiny, rainbow-like film. A black design was painted on the bowl before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Mounted with F1937.39. Detached folio from a dispersed copy of Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Excellent book of counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; text: Persian in black and red naskhi script; recto: illustration and text: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree, 2 lines, 3 paintings; verso: illustration and text: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits, 4 lines, 2 paintings; one of a group of three folios.
persona3_adapted:
This page is part of a medieval Persian book called the *Excellent Book of Counsel*. It was written by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr and contains advice on health and medicine.

On the front of the page, there is a picture and text about the medicinal uses of the mulberry tree. Below the picture are two lines of writing in black and red Persian script. The picture shows three small scenes.

On the back of the page, there is a picture and text about the health benefits of the olive tree, including its leaves and fruit. This side has four lines of writing and two small scenes.

This page is one of three similar pages that were once part of a larger book, but are now kept separately.

CuratorialText: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are constellations of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is composed of seven stars while Centaurus, whose forepart is made up of a man and hindpart of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."
persona3_adapted: Both "The Raven" (Corvus) and "The Centaur" (Centaurus) are groups of stars, called constellations, that can be seen in the southern half of the sky. The Raven is made up of seven stars, while the Centaur—which looks like a man's upper body joined to a horse's lower body—has 37 stars. An old writer named al-Kazwini noted that the last two stars in the Centaur are called "the two sworn upon." According to tradition, one of these stars passes very close to the path of the Raven. Some people, al-Kazwini says, think this star belongs to the Raven, while others insist it is part of the Centaur.

CuratorialText: Bowl, large, deep, ovoidal; heavy foot; cracked. Clay: heavy, coarse, gray. Glaze: white, with tinges of green; crackled and discolored to a brownish tone. Chipped along the rim. Decoration: painted in blue and black under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This is a large, deep bowl shaped like an egg. It has a thick, heavy base. The bowl is cracked. It is made from coarse, heavy gray clay. The glaze—the glassy coating on the surface—is white with hints of green. The glaze has a cracked pattern and has turned a brownish color in places. There are chips along the rim. The decoration was painted in blue and black before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress-wood utensil stand (shin daisu) with four black-lacquered posts and two shelves; four black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress boards (shikiita) of various sizes for use with formal tea procedures.
persona3_adapted: A utensil stand made of cypress wood, coated in black lacquer. It has four black-lacquered posts and two shelves. Also included are four black-lacquered cypress boards in different sizes, used in formal tea ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.
persona3_adapted:
This vase is shaped like an ancient Chinese wine cup called a *gu* (pronounced "goo"), which was used in religious ceremonies over 3,000 years ago. It is decorated with colorful enamel patterns in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white, using a technique called *cloisonné* (pronounced "clwah-zoh-nay"). Inside the base, there is a raised inscription of eight characters that dates the piece to the reign of the Kangxi Emperor (1661–1722).

A separate flower holder fits inside the top of the vase. During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), collecting both ancient objects and new items made to look old was a sign of education and good taste. The vase’s shape and decoration—including the mask-like faces on the base—copy features of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a *gu*.

CuratorialText: Polychrome. Clay: fine white porcelain. Glaze: transparent, high fired. Decoration: dragons amid clouds incised in the paste and covered with brilliant green enamel; six-character Hung-chih mark on base. (Pair with F1952.20).
persona3_adapted: This bowl is made of fine white porcelain, a type of clay. It has a clear, shiny glaze that was applied and then fired at a very high temperature. The decoration shows dragons among clouds, which were carved into the clay before glazing. These carvings are then covered with a bright green enamel. On the bottom of the bowl, there is a mark with six Chinese characters that identifies it as being from the Hung-chih period. This bowl is part of a pair, with its match being F1952.20.

CuratorialText: Bulb-bowl: circular, on three cloud-scroll feet. Two rows of bosses. Incised numeral 1 (one) and spur-marks on base. Clay: hard, fine, light-gray. Glaze: inside:olive, strong lavender-blue and lighter green-blue; outside: rose-purple, blue, gray and olive.
persona3_adapted:
This bowl is round and sits on three small feet shaped like swirling clouds. Two rows of raised bumps decorate the surface. On the bottom, you can see the number "1" scratched into the clay, along with tiny marks left from the kiln during firing.

The bowl is made of hard, fine, light-gray clay. The inside is glazed in olive green, mixed with a strong lavender-blue and a lighter green-blue. The outside shows a blend of rose-purple, blue, gray, and olive tones.

CuratorialText:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are regarded as masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who headed a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu produced paintings such as fans for popular consumption. By the late 1620s, however, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. For his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist ecclesiastical title Hokkyo (Bridge of the Law), which is included in his signature on these screens.
Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous site (meisho) near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens the rock from which pine trees grow are rendered in brilliant mineral colors of green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in animated forms are delineated in alternating lines of ink and gold, producing a luminous effect. Clouds and embankments are rendered in particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.
Sotatsu's innovative composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting legacy. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a period of cultural efflorescence in Kyoto.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

These two folding screens are covered in brilliant paintings that are considered masterpieces. They were made by an artist named Sotatsu, who was both talented and inventive. Sotatsu ran a painting workshop called Tawaraya. Only six sets of screens by him survive today, and these are among the best.

Sotatsu started out as a regular city resident, or townsman, making things like painted fans for everyday people to buy. But by the late 1620s, he was painting for the imperial court—the emperor and his household. Some of his works are still kept in the Kyoto imperial palace. Because of his great artistic skill, he was given an honorary Buddhist title, *Hokkyo*, which means "Bridge of the Law." You can see this title in his signature on these screens.

The screens show a place called Matsushima, which means "Pine Islands." It's a famous scenic spot near the city of Sendai in northeastern Japan. Its beauty has inspired poets and painters for centuries. In Sotatsu's version, the rocks where pine trees grow are painted in bright mineral colors—green, blue, and brown—with gold highlights. The waves are drawn in lively, swirling shapes, using alternating lines of black ink and gold. This creates a glowing, shimmering effect. Clouds and riverbanks are made with tiny flakes of gold leaf, and silver was also used, but over time the silver has darkened to a soft black.

Sotatsu's creative way of arranging the picture makes the land, clouds, bending pines, and churning waves feel like they are moving and interacting with each other. He was always interested in how to compose a scene, and this had a lasting influence. Later artists from the Rimpa school, like Ogata Korin (1658–1716), also painted the Matsushima theme in their own work. These screens were probably made in the early Kan'ei era (1624–1644), a time when culture was flourishing in Kyoto.

CuratorialText:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility.
Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona3_adapted:
**The Story of Shuten Doji**

The tale of Shuten Doji has been a favorite in Japan for centuries. The earliest known illustrated version of this story dates back to the 1300s.

Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021) was a real-life warrior known for his great fighting skills. Over time, popular stories turned him into a legendary hero who saved the land from evil demons. Shuten Doji was a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto. He terrorized the local people by eating them and keeping young women prisoner. With help from the gods and clever tricks, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre's head and brought peace back to the area.

**About the Artist**

The artist Kyosai clearly loved telling stories through pictures. He also studied old artworks very carefully. You can see both of these interests in his paintings and sketchbooks. His own works are full of dark humor and strange, sometimes disturbing images. This style was very popular with audiences in the late Edo period (1615-1868) and early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in linear relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically excavated in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. It consists of four separate pieces: a tubular bronze handle, two caps with dragon motifs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was lengthened with additional elements that are now missing.
persona3_adapted: This unusual object is a ceremonial sickle made of four separate parts: three bronze pieces decorated with turquoise, and a jade blade with carved lines, a notched back, and a ground-down edge. No similar items have ever been found in a scientific excavation in China, so experts are unsure of its original purpose. The sickle includes a tubular bronze handle, two caps featuring dragon designs, and a jade blade with a serrated edge—which may not be the original blade. Traces of wood found inside the handle suggest it was once attached to a longer handle or other missing parts.

CuratorialText: This is one of a pair of folding screens illustrating two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a narrative composed in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen represents chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose monochromatic ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more commonly illustrated in lavish color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically evocative of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.
persona3_adapted:
This is one of a pair of folding screens that shows scenes from two of the fifty-four chapters in *The Tale of Genji*. That story was written in the early 1000s by a court lady known as Murasaki Shikibu. It’s a fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was full of romance and tragedy, and it became a very popular subject for Japanese artists.

This screen illustrates chapter fifty-one, called *Ukifune*. In the scene, a woman named Ukifune and her lover Niou drift together in a boat on a cold winter day. They are sheltered by a folding screen, which makes them feel temporarily cut off from the world in their sad love story.

The painter, Kano Naonobu, chose to use only black ink and thin, pale colors for this scene. That’s unusual because most artists painted this story in bright, rich colors and gold. The soft, dreamlike mist and washes of ink create a poetic mood that matches the feeling of each moment in the story. Naonobu was an official painter who worked for the Tokugawa shogun, the military ruler of Japan at the time.

CuratorialText: Ornament of roughly rectangular shape; translucent mottled tan and cream color with areas of white film of incipient disintegration; blunt blade edge is indicated by lightly incised line and beveling; upper edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection; satin smooth surface. (One small chip). Acquired with a box, now lost.
persona3_adapted: This small, roughly rectangular ornament has a smooth, satin-like surface. Its color is a mix of tan and cream, with some cloudy white patches where the material is starting to break down. A blunt blade edge is suggested by a lightly scratched line and a slanted edge. The top of the ornament has uneven notches and a narrow, sticking-out part. There is one small chip. It originally came with a box, but that box is now missing.

CuratorialText: Jar, large, ovoidal, on a low, thick foot rim. Clay: soft, grayish. Glaze: variable aubergine, frosted with whitish areas of partial disintegration, with a slight iridescence. Earthy attachments. Decoration: impressed, incised and modelled in the paste.
persona3_adapted: This large, egg-shaped jar sits on a short, thick base. It is made of soft, gray clay. The surface has a purple-brown glaze that is not uniform, with frosty white patches where the glaze has partly worn away. In some light, the jar has a faint, rainbow-like shimmer. There are earthy-looking attachments on the surface. The decoration was created by pressing, carving, and shaping the clay itself.

CuratorialText: Bowl: deep, ovoidal; low foot. Clay: hard, white. Glaze: pale green, mottled with blue-violet inside; gray-olive on rim and edge of flow; finely pitted.
persona3_adapted: This bowl is deep and egg-shaped, with a short base. It's made of hard, white clay. The glaze is a pale green with blue-violet spots inside, while the rim and the edges where the glaze drips are a gray-olive color. The surface has many tiny pits.

CuratorialText: Chinese Han Glazed pottery " Hill-jar"; cylindrical vessel with cover; three bear-shaped feet; spur marks. Clay: reddish, sandy texture; fired medium. Glaze: lead silicate; deep green with areas of silvery iridescence, earth incrustations. Decoration: horizontal panels moulded in relief with animals a nd horsemen; two simulated mask and ring handles; cover represents mountains with animals in relief.
persona3_adapted:
This jar, made in China during the Han Dynasty, is a type of container with a lid. It has three feet shaped like bears. The clay is reddish and sandy, and it was fired at a medium temperature. The jar is covered in a deep green glaze, which has turned silvery and shiny in some spots. There are also traces of dirt and minerals from being buried underground.

The decoration includes horizontal bands with raised images of animals and horsemen. On the sides, there are two handles that look like rings attached to masks. The lid is shaped like a mountain, with animals in raised relief.

CuratorialText: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A true titan of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual heft and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such profound success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was regarded as the pinnacle of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first comprehensive exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to illuminate the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the foremost writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, displaying a poetic sensibility that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full breadth of his life and career, from his origins in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as a peer of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, prolific decade at the papal court in Rome. To underscore the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With particular attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare opportunity to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.
persona3_adapted:
**Step into the world of one of history’s most famous and influential artists.** Raphael (1483–1520) was a giant of the Italian Renaissance—a period of great cultural change in Europe. He combined big ideas with a graceful, poetic style, creating works that were both smart and deeply emotional. This was a key skill in the tricky, power-driven world of Renaissance courts.

In just 37 years, Raphael became so successful as a painter, designer, and architect that for centuries after his death, people saw him as the ultimate example of artistic perfection.

**Raphael: Sublime Poetry** is the first major exhibition of his work in the United States. It brings together more than 170 of his greatest pieces—including some rarely seen treasures—to show the brilliance of his creativity.

Raphael was the son of a painter and poet. In Rome, he worked with the top writers and thinkers of his time, and his work had a poetic feel that amazed both his peers and later generations.

The exhibition follows his life and career from start to finish: from his early days in Urbino, to his rise in Florence (where he became a rival to Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo), and finally to his busy last ten years working for the pope in Rome.

To show the full range of his talent, the exhibition includes important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from museums and private collections across Europe and the United States. Many of these works have never been shown together before.

The show pays special attention to how Raphael painted women—from being one of the first Western artists to use nude female models, to his gentle images of Mary and baby Jesus. It also shares recent discoveries made with modern technology. This is a rare chance to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the entire course of art history.

CuratorialText: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative facility, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of assistants and collaborators to propagate his inventions and style, spawning a new generation of noteworthy artists.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Raphael (1483–1520) was one of the most influential artists of all time. For more than 300 years, people in Europe and beyond admired him as the perfect painter and looked to him as a model for how to become an artist. Even during his short life, other artists praised his incredible talent. Later, his fame grew even larger than that of other famous Italian Renaissance stars.

Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, he was an ambitious young man who quickly left his modest hometown in central Italy’s Marche region. He went looking for important supporters in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence—then the center of the European art world—he became an equal to the older masters Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his last ten years in Rome as the favorite artist of the popes. There, people called him the “prince of painters.”

Raphael was also a smart businessman and organizer. He worked with well-trained teams of assistants and partners to spread his ideas and style. This helped create a new generation of important artists.

CuratorialText: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an immersive look at his meteoric career. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on view present a portrait of his dashing artistic personality and astonishingly creative mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became an intimate friend to literary figures, and tried his hand at composing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture accustomed to thinking of painting and poetry as intertwined sister arts. The elegance and dramatic force of his imagery still have the power to invoke the ancient dictum, much discussed in his time, that “painting is a mute poetry, and poetry is a blind painting.”
persona3_adapted: This first major U.S. show dedicated to Raphael gives you an up-close look at his fast-moving, brilliant career. The drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on display together reveal his bold, charming personality and his incredibly inventive mind. The exhibition also tells the story of an artist whose father was both a poet and a painter, who became close friends with writers, and who even tried writing sonnets himself. Raphael lived and worked in a culture where painting and poetry were seen as closely connected art forms. The elegance and emotional power of his images still bring to life an old saying, much discussed in his time: “Painting is silent poetry, and poetry is painting that speaks.”

CuratorialText: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region, in east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.
persona3_adapted: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a small hill town in east central Italy. The town sits in the Marche region, surrounded by a dramatic, rocky landscape. Growing up in Urbino shaped Raphael's life and work in important ways. The town was known for its elegant court life and a special tradition of learning and ideas. Raphael was proud of where he came from. Throughout his career, he signed his paintings "RAPHAEL VRBINAS," which means "Raphael of Urbino."

CuratorialText: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy composing an epic poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino.
