A formidable general, scholar, and patron of art and architecture, Federico transformed his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and humanist learning. He founded a fabled library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale, whose rhythmic arcades and harmonic geometry embodied the humanist dedication to mathematics that distinguished the culture of Urbino. Those same ideals are evident in the vibrant architectural visions—enlivened with perspectival effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.
persona3_adapted: Raphael got his first art lessons from his father, Giovanni Santi (around 1439–1494), who worked as a painter for the local court and also wrote a lot of poetry. When Raphael was a child, his father was busy writing a long epic poem about Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who made the Duchy of Urbino peaceful, wealthy, and well-known. Federico was a strong military leader, a scholar, and a big supporter of art and building projects. He turned his court into a famous center for the arts and for humanist learning—a way of thinking that focused on human values and classical knowledge. He also built a legendary library and rebuilt Urbino’s impressive palace, the Palazzo Ducale. The palace’s repeating arches and balanced, geometric design showed the humanist love for mathematics, which was a key part of Urbino’s culture. You can see those same ideas in the lively architectural scenes created by Urbino’s painters, which are full of realistic perspective effects and references to ancient Greek and Roman styles.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s father soon brought his precocious son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, prolific painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on collaboration with assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael coursed quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a collaborator to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s father soon sent his talented young son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew from local projects. Perugino was a brilliant and highly productive painter and designer, with busy workshops in Perugia and Florence. To keep up with the many requests from clients all over Italy, he relied heavily on help from his assistants. Raphael moved quickly through the stages of student and apprentice, first becoming a partner to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s graceful figures, impressive technical skill, and efficient ways of reusing his designs had a lasting influence on the younger artist.

CuratorialText: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.
persona3_adapted: The young artist Raphael was hardworking, eager to learn, and very focused. He first tried to build his career on his own in a small, wealthy town called Città di Castello, near Perugia. There, he painted important religious artworks for churches. In the regions of Umbria and the Marche, groups called confraternities were also major supporters of artists. These were religious organizations for regular people, focused on doing good works and helping others. In this room, you’ll find paintings and related sketches made for these confraternities by the artist Perugino and a young Raphael. One of these paintings is the first work that Raphael painted entirely by himself—a fact confirmed by recent cleaning and restoration.

CuratorialText: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.
persona3_adapted:
From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael worked to build relationships with wealthy supporters by creating both large, impressive religious paintings for churches and smaller, personal devotional pieces. He did this across several regions of Italy: the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany.

A key example is the large, multi-panel painting at the center of this gallery. It was originally made for nuns in Perugia and is now called the Colonna Altarpiece (named after a later owner). For the first time since the painting was taken apart around 1663 and its individual panels were scattered, the entire work is brought back together here.

Other important projects include a huge altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello. That piece was later destroyed in an earthquake and only survives in fragments and early sketches. There is also a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, which is represented here by study drawings and the painting that formed its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
persona3_adapted: Full-scale sketches for the Oddi Altarpiece show the working methods Raphael learned while training and working alongside the artist Perugino. These impressive drawings on paper also reveal how he used different materials—such as black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on specially prepared paper—often with remarkable skill. Drawings like these probably acted as his portfolio and certainly made a strong impression on the people who hired him.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
persona3_adapted: Raphael might have moved to Florence because other painters were talking excitedly about two huge, rival mural designs there—one by Leonardo and one by Michelangelo. These designs were never actually painted on the walls, but they were incredibly new and powerful, like sculptures that seemed to jump out at you. The artworks in this room show you that same exciting, dramatic quality. While Raphael lived in Florence from 1504 to 1508, he also looked closely at Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings. He quickly and smoothly borrowed ideas from their styles and techniques and made them his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
persona3_adapted: Raphael also picked up new ideas from other artists about how to create his paintings. Leonardo da Vinci believed in "sketching quickly" while inspiration was fresh. He thought that spending too much time making drawings perfect could kill new ideas. Instead, he suggested working out ideas in rough sketches—like a poet writing a messy first draft with crossed-out words. Before moving to Florence, Raphael had worked more slowly and carefully, building his paintings figure by figure, the way his teacher Perugino had taught him. But now, by sketching freely and quickly, he started making paintings that felt more natural, full of movement and lively emotion.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
persona3_adapted: Raphael was deeply influenced by the art of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo. Their work taught him how to make his paintings feel more spacious, give his figures a strong, almost sculptural presence, and add emotional power. To achieve this, Raphael did a huge amount of experimenting on paper. He created many drawings in a series—starting with quick sketches and moving on to studies based on small clay or wax models. These drawings show how Raphael became more and more careful and organized in his process. For example, he would take a pyramid-shaped arrangement of figures (a style Leonardo liked) and bring it to life step by step. Even though this kind of setup could look still or boring, Raphael added movement by having the figures interact through their gestures and the way they looked at each other.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
persona3_adapted:
In 1507, when he was twenty-four years old, Raphael created what would become his most important work before moving to Rome. It was an altarpiece—a painting placed behind an altar in a church—showing the burial of Christ. The work was made for the Baglioni family chapel in the city of Perugia.

The early sketches for this painting show that Raphael was already a master of visual storytelling. They reveal how he built a dramatic scene with a clear, powerful story that builds to a high point. Through hard work and careful study—and using what he had learned from ancient Greek and Roman statues—Raphael turned a still, balanced arrangement of figures into an emotionally gripping action scene. He had taken to heart Leonardo da Vinci's idea that the way people move and hold their bodies can show what is going on in their minds and hearts.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
persona3_adapted: For centuries, people have loved Raphael’s paintings of Mary and baby Jesus because they show such a gentle, loving bond. Around the year 1300, religious art from the Eastern Orthodox tradition began to show a type of Mary called the “Virgin Eleousa,” which means “compassionate” in Greek. In these images, Mary holds the baby Jesus close, and he touches his cheek to hers. This kind of picture became very popular and inspired many artists in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, including sculptors and painters in Italy. Raphael used this older style as a starting point, but he changed it completely to create something new and his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona3_adapted:
Raphael was a master at painting scenes full of life and innocent joy, even while showing an expert understanding of how the human body actually looks and moves. This is especially striking when you consider how dangerous pregnancy, childbirth, and early childhood were in his time.

The rare account book on display here records the funeral costs for Raphael’s mother, who died from complications related to childbirth in 1491, as well as for two of his siblings who died as babies. At that time, people knew very little about women’s health and childbirth. Pregnancy and giving birth were extremely risky, and many babies did not survive.

These harsh realities, combined with a trend among Renaissance artists to make religious figures feel more human and relatable, help explain why the gentle image of Mary holding the baby Jesus was so popular—both as a subject for art and as a symbol people prayed to for protection.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
persona3_adapted: Despite his big dreams, Raphael had a hard time making a name for himself in the busy art scene of Florence. His best chance for work came from the city's rich merchants, who hired him to paint portraits and gentle religious scenes, mostly of Mary and the baby Jesus. Raphael kept making this kind of art for private buyers even after he moved to Rome in 1508, and many of his fellow artists did the same.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
persona3_adapted: Raphael wanted to show the Virgin Mary as both heavenly and beautiful. Like other artists and poets of his time, he followed the popular style of painting her as a graceful, noble woman with delicate features and golden hair. But he also stayed true to older Christian traditions. Religious writings, songs, and poems described Mary’s beauty as a reflection of her perfect spiritual goodness. Raphael added a human touch to his paintings, showing emotion and personality through the figures’ gestures and expressions. His work stands out for its masterful use of light, color, space, and shape. He also painted babies more naturally—with realistic body proportions, poses, and facial expressions—than many other artists of his day.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s portraits show a deep understanding of the people he painted. This came from years of practice drawing, which helped him capture a quiet, focused sense of the person in front of him. The way Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa, starting in 1503, was a groundbreaking model for showing someone dressed in fine clothes. In that painting, the person is shown from a three-quarter angle, with her head turned to the left, her body upright, and her hands resting gently at her waist. The graceful poses in Raphael’s work also suggest a desire to match the polished manners of Italian Renaissance courts and the beauty standards praised by famous poets of the time. Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), who wrote a book about how to behave stylishly at court. That book promoted a way of acting that valued *sprezzatura*—a kind of practiced ease or casual confidence that looks effortless but is actually carefully learned.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona3_adapted: In Renaissance times, many portraits of women were created to mark engagements and weddings. These marriages were often about money and family alliances, not romantic love. The women in these paintings wore fancy clothes and jewelry to impress their future husband’s family while families discussed dowries (money or property the bride’s family gave) and other property deals. At the same time, poets and artists openly praised women’s looks, which helped create a culture where young women could become *innamorate*—meaning either a pure, idealized love interest or an actual mistress—for wealthy, powerful men.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
persona3_adapted: In 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome. He would go on to become the favorite artist for two popes: Julius II (who led the church from 1503 to 1513) and Leo X (1513–1521). A fascinating question is how this 25-year-old managed to outshine older painters already working in the Vatican Palace and take over the fresco decoration of its most important room, the Stanza della Segnatura. His incredible talent and personal charm certainly helped. But research shows that political connections between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also played a big role. For example, his friend and fellow Urbino native, Donato Bramante (1444–1514), who was the pope’s favorite architect, supported him.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
persona3_adapted: Here are some of Raphael’s sketches for his famous Vatican frescoes. One is *The School of Athens*, which shows a gathering of famous philosophers. The other is the *Disputa*, which represents Catholic theology. These drawings show an artist who had complete control over the different tools he used. Some sketches, made quickly with pen and ink, feel full of energy and fresh ideas. Others, done in black chalk, feel grand and important even though they are small. In the next room, a digital video shows the power of Raphael’s finished paintings in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
persona3_adapted: By around 1510, Raphael was earning praise as the most famous and productive artist working for the pope in Rome. At the same time, the more private Michelangelo was working alone in the Sistine Chapel on his famous ceiling paintings. These paintings would later have a big impact on Raphael, who secretly went to see them. The drawings in this room are connected to projects Raphael worked on as he finished his first set of wall paintings in the Vatican Palace and started his second set, in a room called the Stanza di Eliodoro. These later paintings feel bigger and more dramatic. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X became pope, some of the more complex, scholarly images were replaced with simpler, more direct messages about the pope's power.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
persona3_adapted: Other drawings and prints show how Raphael worked with Marcantonio Raimondi (around 1480–1534), a printmaker from Bologna who was also a close friend. Their partnership began in 1510 and was very productive. Together, they made engravings that showed amazing skill and artistic strength. This method allowed images to be reproduced many times, helping Raphael’s designs live on long after his death. Artists kept copying these prints into the late 1800s, and they were also used in other art forms, like the painted ceramic plates you can see in a nearby case.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
persona3_adapted: In the next room, you’ll find a digital video that shows Raphael’s huge wall paintings in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. This video helps you understand the size and setting of these paintings, which connects to the many drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, Raphael and his team painted these works in rooms that are almost square—now called the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio—and in a very large, long room called the Sala di Costantino. (Raphael died in 1520, so his assistants finished that last room.)

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges. Water-based colors applied to moist surface plaster (intonaco) dry and carbonate in a chemical process, permanently bonding the colors to the wall. Executed primarily by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura are the most famous and include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the following three rooms Raphael worked with a greater involvement of collaborators who followed his designs closely.
persona3_adapted:
Raphael's frescoes in the Vatican are some of the most famous paintings in the world. A fresco is a special way of painting on walls that is both long-lasting and very tricky. The artist has to work fast and carefully. First, they make a full-size drawing, called a cartoon, to plan the picture. Then, they apply wet plaster to the wall and paint onto it with colors mixed in water. As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction happens that locks the colors into the wall permanently. This means the painting becomes part of the wall itself.

Raphael painted the most famous set of these frescoes in a room called the Stanza della Segnatura between 1508 and 1512. His works there include the well-known *School of Athens*, the *Disputa*, figures representing Justice, and the *Parnassus*. In the three rooms he painted after that, Raphael had more help from assistants, but they still followed his designs closely.

CuratorialText: The paintings here represent the breathtaking visual eloquence of Raphael’s later years. New harmonies of color are transformed by a dark chiaroscuro reminiscent of Leonardo. Sculptural forms seem to project from dense compositions. Charged gazes, poses, and gestures communicate a sense of impending drama.
persona3_adapted: These paintings show the stunning visual power of Raphael’s later work. He uses new combinations of color, and his strong contrasts between light and dark are similar to what Leonardo da Vinci did. The figures look almost three-dimensional, as if they are pushing out from the crowded scenes. The intense looks, body positions, and hand movements all suggest that something dramatic is about to happen.

CuratorialText: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X rained project after project upon Raphael. He produced few paintings independently; using his drawings as a guide, skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. He was often disparaged for this practice by rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Executive help freed the artist to concentrate his creative energies on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about authorship. Labels in this exhibition attempt to indicate instances of their likely intervention, and a nearby display allows comparison of studies by Raphael and his fellow artists in an effort to characterize their individual styles.
persona3_adapted:
Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X kept giving Raphael one big project after another. Raphael didn't paint many works entirely on his own during this time. Instead, he would create the initial designs, and his skilled assistants would do most of the actual painting—especially for the large wall paintings in a room at the Vatican called the Stanza dell'Incendio. 

This way of working made Raphael unpopular with his rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. They criticized him for not doing the painting himself. But thanks to his well-organized team, Raphael and his workshop completed an amazing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Having help with the actual painting allowed Raphael to focus his creative energy on coming up with new ideas and trying out new approaches.

Because assistants worked on many of the paintings and some of the drawings, experts often disagree about who actually made what. In this exhibition, the labels try to show where assistants likely helped. There's also a display nearby where you can compare drawings by Raphael and his assistants, so you can see the differences in their individual styles.

CuratorialText: The imperious Pope Leo X charged Raphael with the daunting task of designing a set of monumental tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during special occasions—a project that put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo and his ceiling frescoes above. Starting in 1515, Raphael began drawing small preliminary studies. Aided by workshop assistants, he then painted in color gouaches on paper the enormous full-scale cartoons that would guide the weavers. Woven in Brussels using rich materials, the stratospherically expensive tapestries contributed to the bankruptcy of Leo’s papacy. With their inventive compositions, striking color harmonies, and monumental scale, they were soon the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings commissioned second editions woven from Raphael’s cartoons, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.
persona3_adapted:
Pope Leo X, a powerful and demanding leader, gave Raphael a huge challenge: design a set of giant tapestries for the Sistine Chapel. These tapestries would only be hung on special occasions. This project put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo, who had already painted the famous ceiling of the same chapel.

Starting in 1515, Raphael made small sketches to plan the designs. Then, with help from his assistants, he created full-size, detailed paintings on paper. These large-scale paintings, called cartoons, were used as guides for the weavers. The tapestries were woven in Brussels using expensive materials. They cost so much that they helped push the Pope's finances into bankruptcy.

Despite the cost, the tapestries were a huge success. Their creative designs, bold colors, and huge size made them the envy of kings all across Europe. Many rulers ordered their own copies, using Raphael's original cartoons as a guide. The set owned by King Philip II of Spain is shown here with three stunning examples.

CuratorialText: As the tapestry designs progressed, the Raphael team largely took over the fresco work in the Vatican Palace, endeavoring to complete the Stanza dell’Incendio and assisting Raphael as he began to conceive decorations for the final space. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) acted as his studio foreman and went on to enjoy a brilliant independent career.
persona3_adapted: As the designs for the tapestries moved forward, Raphael's team took on most of the painting work in the Vatican Palace. They worked to finish the Stanza dell’Incendio (a room named after a famous fire scene) and helped Raphael as he started planning decorations for the last room. One of his key assistants, Giulio Romano (around 1499–1546), managed the workshop and later had a very successful career of his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the enormous Transfiguration, commissioned by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (the future Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Spurred by competition with artistic rivals, Raphael produced a work markedly more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although its execution was interrupted by his untimely death in 1520, the Transfiguration became Raphael’s artistic testament. The main event is Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, during which he transformed before the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divinity.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the massive *Transfiguration*. It was commissioned around 1517 by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici, who later became Pope Clement VII. Pushed by competition with other artists, Raphael created a work that was much more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although his sudden death in 1520 interrupted its completion, the *Transfiguration* became Raphael’s artistic legacy. The main scene shows Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, where he changed before the amazed apostles Peter, James, and John, revealing his divine nature.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s studies for the Transfiguration, displayed in the niche at left, represent the culmination of his career as a draftsman. They are reunited here to show the range of his design process. Raphael used red chalk primarily when drawing nude models from life. He reserved black chalk for the full-scale studies of apostles’ heads and hands, a type of drawing called an “auxiliary cartoon” because the design starts from a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s drawings for the *Transfiguration*, shown in the alcove on the left, are some of the best examples of his skill as an artist. These pieces are brought together here to show how he developed his ideas. Raphael mainly used red chalk when sketching live models who were not wearing clothes. He used black chalk for larger drawings of the apostles’ faces and hands. This type of drawing is called an “auxiliary cartoon,” because the design begins with a dotted outline that was copied from another piece of paper.

CuratorialText: Raphael rarely interrupted his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to make room for other commissions. The notable exception was his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met in about 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the richest banker in Europe, and his legendary wealth financed the popes’ cultural and military enterprises. Raphael’s work for Chigi included fresco paintings, as well as designs for his remodeled stables and the multimedia ensembles of his two chapels.
persona3_adapted: Raphael usually focused on his work for Popes Julius II and Leo X, and he rarely took on other projects. But there was one important exception: his close friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met around 1510. Chigi was born in Siena and became the richest banker in Europe. His enormous wealth helped pay for the popes’ cultural projects and military efforts. For Chigi, Raphael created wall paintings, designed plans for his remodeled stables, and worked on the art and decorations for two of Chigi’s chapels.

CuratorialText: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s language of contorted poses and powerful muscularity is transformed with an effortless grace and the sensuality of living flesh. The drawings also allude to the legacy of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which led to the style known as Mannerism after his death. Male and female forms here anticipate prototypical Mannerist poses—as with Raphael’s primeval personification of an earthquake in a Sistine Chapel tapestry design, which spawned the figural vocabulary that his former assistant Giulio Romano would exploit to spectacular effect in his later frescoes.
persona3_adapted:
Here are the drawings Raphael made as he planned projects for his patron, Chigi. In these chalk sketches of nude figures, you can see how Raphael took Michelangelo’s style—full of twisted poses and strong, muscular bodies—and made it feel graceful, effortless, and alive.

These drawings also show how Raphael’s way of drawing the human body influenced the next generation of artists. After Raphael died, his style helped create a new approach called Mannerism. The male and female figures here already show the kinds of poses that would become typical of Mannerist art. For example, Raphael’s drawing of a person representing an earthquake—made for a tapestry in the Sistine Chapel—introduced a set of poses that his former assistant, Giulio Romano, later used with great dramatic effect in his own wall paintings.

CuratorialText: Rome and its ancient monuments transformed Raphael’s art, and his work likewise changed the face of Rome. Following his arrival in the Eternal City in 1508, he threw himself into archaeological studies and made drawings of monuments that demonstrate a nearly scientific rigor. Pope Leo X tasked Raphael with creating an enormous drawing of ancient Rome, “making those members that are entirely ruined and are completely invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter to Leo, written on Raphael’s behalf, attests to the artist’s deep archaeological knowledge and technical skill, including a highly original method for using a magnetic compass in conducting surveys of ruins.
persona3_adapted: Rome and its ancient ruins had a huge impact on Raphael’s art, and in turn, his work helped shape the look of the city. When he arrived in Rome in 1508, he dove into studying ancient sites and made drawings of monuments with almost scientific precision. Pope Leo X asked Raphael to create a massive map of ancient Rome, “matching the parts that are completely destroyed and invisible with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter written on Raphael’s behalf to the Pope shows just how much he knew about archaeology and how skilled he was technically—including a very clever way of using a magnetic compass to measure ruins.

CuratorialText: Around 1503 Pope Julius II had charged Bramante, Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino, to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica that would convey the majesty of the papacy in the aesthetic language of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here illuminate Raphael’s career as an architect after he took over this massive project upon Bramante’s death. In 1517 Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, designed by Bramante and now destroyed but pictured in two works here, where he lived out his final years in Rome as the “prince of painters.”
persona3_adapted: Around 1503, Pope Julius II asked Bramante—a close friend and mentor to Raphael, both from the city of Urbino—to design a new Saint Peter's Basilica. The goal was to create a church that would show the power and importance of the papacy, using the style and look of ancient Roman buildings. The drawings and prints in this section show how Raphael later became an architect and took over this huge project after Bramante died. In 1517, Raphael bought a house called the Palazzo Caprini, which Bramante had designed. That building no longer exists, but you can see it in two works displayed here. Raphael spent his final years in Rome living in this home, known at the time as the "prince of painters."

CuratorialText: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
persona3_adapted:
The Met got its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most complete collections in the world. Spanning 1,500 years of cultural history and including many different types, techniques, and styles, the Met’s collection has become a key resource for studying Chinese painting and calligraphy.

This exhibition shows a rich selection of works from the collection, arranged mostly in order of when they were made. The final room tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), who was the head of the museum’s Asian Art department from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). In the 1940s, when Fong was a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai, he studied brush arts with Li. The display focuses on a group of fans painted and written by Li. These fans were meant to pass on the classic models of the tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and kept by the Fong family ever since, these fans are shown to the public for the first time.

CuratorialText: The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art. Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
persona3_adapted:
The hand-held fan became an unexpected source of inspiration for some of the most creative artists in 19th-century Europe. During this time, fans grew extremely popular across all levels of society. They were both useful and stylish—people used them to stay cool, to accessorize their outfits, and even to communicate without words.

Well-known French painters like Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro didn’t just include fans in their paintings; they also used fans as a new and experimental surface for their art. This exhibition, called *Fanmania*, explores why forward-thinking artists started incorporating fans into their work. It also looks at related themes like gender roles, dating and romance, shopping and consumer culture, and the borrowing of ideas from other cultures.

Artists were drawn to the fan’s half-circle shape for many reasons. Some were fascinated by fans from Asia and Spain. Others saw a commercial opportunity, or were interested in trying new artistic techniques and forms. Featuring more than 75 artworks from across The Met’s collection, this multimedia exhibition includes painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia, as well as artworks showing women holding fans. Together, they help explain the craze known as “fanmania.”

CuratorialText: During the nineteenth century, handheld fans achieved unprecedented popularity throughout European society. A newfound artistic appreciation for these objects also emerged, fueled by cultural crazes for all things Japanese and Spanish as well as by exhibitions and publications devoted to fan making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, Edgar Degas envisioned an entire gallery devoted to fans. Though the fan-shaped paintings his colleagues submitted were ultimately dispersed throughout the show, they nevertheless elicited strong responses from the public, including bewilderment, as illustrated below. Today, these works continue to raise questions—mainly, why did ambitious European artists adopt this accessory as a format for their work? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, Fanmania explores the reasons artists were drawn to this semicircular form, including its commercial potential, its fashionability, and the opportunities it offered for formal and technical innovation. Although avant-garde fans were primarily designed for display rather than use, they retain many of the associations of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, appropriation, and experimentation unfold in this examination of fans as objects of adornment, decoration, and communication in the nineteenth century.
persona3_adapted: During the 1800s, handheld fans became incredibly popular across Europe. People also started to see them as art, thanks to a growing fascination with Japanese and Spanish culture, as well as books and exhibitions about fan-making and its history. In 1882, the writer Octave Uzanne noted, “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans.” For the fourth Impressionist exhibition in 1879, artist Edgar Degas imagined a whole room just for fans. Even though the fan-shaped paintings his fellow artists created were spread out across the show, they still got strong reactions from the public—including confusion, as shown below. Today, these works still make us wonder: why did serious European artists choose to use this accessory as a canvas for their art? *Fanmania* brings together pieces from across The Met to explore what drew artists to this half-circle shape—things like its potential to make money, its trendiness, and the chance to try new techniques and styles. While these avant-garde fans were mostly made to be displayed, not used, they still carry many of the meanings of real fans. This exhibit looks at fans as objects of fashion, decoration, and communication in the 1800s, touching on themes like gender, romance, borrowing from other cultures, and artistic experimentation.

CuratorialText: Handheld folding fans are multimedia objects that involve many types of artistry and artisanal skill. This show focuses primarily on the role of painters and printmakers in the production of the fan’s semicircular leaf. In Europe, prior to 1700, most of these designs were unsigned. In the nineteenth century, major artists grew increasingly interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did preeminent fan-making houses, which evolved out of earlier guilds. Some of the precedents for these practices are on display here.
persona3_adapted: Handheld folding fans are objects that combine different kinds of art and craft. This exhibition focuses mainly on the painters and printmakers who created the fan's semicircular leaf—the part that opens up. In Europe, before 1700, most fan designs were not signed. In the 1800s, important artists became more interested in making fans and began signing their work. The top fan-making companies also started signing their pieces. These companies grew out of earlier trade groups called guilds. You can see some early examples of these practices in this gallery.

CuratorialText: Historically, European fans have been most closely associated with the eighteenth century, when their use became widespread. The examples here show the various roles fans played, including as tools for flirtation, amusement, education, propaganda, observation, and masquerade. Fan leaves communicated through their subjects and style, while users brought them to life through their gestures. Women were not only the consumers of fans but also among their producers, as fan making was considered an art form appropriate for female practitioners, whether amateur or professional.
persona3_adapted: Fans have a long history in Europe, but they are most often linked to the 1700s, when they became very popular. The fans on display here show how they were used for many different purposes—like flirting, having fun, learning, spreading ideas, watching others, and wearing at costume parties. The pictures and designs on the fan leaves sent messages, and the way people held or moved the fans brought those messages to life. Women didn’t just buy and use fans—they also made them. Fan-making was seen as a proper art form for women, whether they did it as a hobby or as a job.

CuratorialText: Handheld fans found perhaps their most prominent public display in the opera house. The atmosphere of spectatorship and performance—present both onstage and among the audience—heightened attention to fans in the hands of female theatergoers. The emphasis on seeing and being seen even gave rise to fan designs that incorporated opera glasses, or lorgnettes (from the French lorgner, “to ogle”), and small mirrors. A lorgnette fan is on view in the case nearby. Fan makers also developed a genre of fans devoted to the opera. These fans incorporated scores, lyrics, and homages to celebrated composers.
persona3_adapted: Handheld fans were most famously seen in public at the opera house. There, the drama wasn't just on stage—it was also in the audience. Women used fans to draw attention to themselves, and to watch others. This focus on seeing and being seen led to fans that included opera glasses (small binoculars, also called lorgnettes, from the French word for "to ogle") and tiny mirrors. You can see a fan with opera glasses in the case nearby. Fan makers also created a special type of fan just for the opera. These fans featured sheet music, song lyrics, and tributes to famous composers.

CuratorialText: As theatergoing grew more popular later in the century, Impressionist artists seized on the novel subject of spectators in the theater loge. The fashionability and ubiquity of fans at the opera had become fully apparent by the early 1880s, as evidenced by the cartoon below.
persona3_adapted: As theatergoing became more popular later in the 1800s, Impressionist artists started painting a new and interesting subject: people watching the show from theater boxes. By the early 1880s, it was clear that fans had become a common and stylish accessory at the opera, as you can see in the cartoon below.

CuratorialText: Movement is an essential aspect of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The expressive qualities of a fan’s motions are among the ways it can convey meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at a particularly opportune moment before a potential suitor.
persona3_adapted: A fan isn't just for keeping cool—it's also about how you move it. In the 1800s, a critic named Charles Blanc said that a fan is really just an excuse to make graceful movements while pretending to stir the air for a breeze. The way you move a fan can say a lot about you. It can show your personality, your mood, or even what you're thinking. Fans can flutter, vibrate, snap open, snap shut, rise, or even fall—sometimes on purpose, like when someone drops one at just the right moment in front of someone they're interested in.

CuratorialText: Artists in the late nineteenth century understood the fan as a moving object and attempted to capture its dynamism in myriad ways. The fleeting and ephemeral nature of its movement surely made it an appealing subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs frequently incorporate metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, spangles, and sequins, the visual impacts of which are heightened when in motion.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1800s, artists saw the fan as something that moves, and they tried to show that movement in many different ways. Because a fan's motion is quick and doesn't last long, it was a perfect subject for the Impressionists—a group of artists who liked to capture passing moments. Fan designs often include shiny materials like metallic paint, gold leaf, feathers, and sparkly decorations. These details look even more striking when the fan is in motion.

CuratorialText: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the passion for Japanese art and culture they observed among their contemporaries. Japanese goods became increasingly available in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. The 1867 and 1878 Universal Exhibitions in Paris were pivotal events in Europeans’ exposure to the arts of Japan. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected such works, including fans, acquiring them from newly established specialist dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, Japanese fans were imported in the millions to France, where they were used as women’s accessories and decoration.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

In the early 1870s, French critics started using the word “Japonisme” to describe the widespread fascination with Japanese art and culture they saw around them. This interest grew after the United States pressured Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54, which made Japanese goods much easier to get in Europe. Two major world fairs in Paris—one in 1867 and another in 1878—played a key role in introducing Europeans to Japanese art. Many Impressionist painters and their friends began collecting Japanese items, like fans, buying them from new shops and dealers that specialized in these goods. By the late 1880s, millions of Japanese fans were being imported into France, where women used them as fashion accessories and as home decoration.

CuratorialText: European artists focused on the aesthetic value of these objects and borrowed from them liberally, often with little understanding of their original contexts. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though subtleties of interpretation varied. Certainly, the history of Japonisme is fraught, but the admiration for Japanese fans freed European artists to imagine new techniques and strategies for their art.
persona3_adapted: European artists were drawn to the beauty of these objects and freely took ideas from them, often without knowing much about their original meanings or uses. Today, some people might call this cultural borrowing, though opinions on it have changed over time. The story of Japonisme is certainly complicated, but the love for Japanese fans gave European artists the freedom to try new methods and approaches in their own work.

CuratorialText: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a center of fan production at the same time that the country’s tourism industry emerged and its culture was increasingly exported throughout Europe. Reporting from his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s prominent role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became inextricably linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and remains so today. In Paris at mid-century, Spanish culture was in vogue, a phenomenon known as Hispagnolisme. Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, exerted considerable influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians performed often in the capital, and avant-garde artists, such as Edouard Manet, took them up as subjects. For some artists who made fans in this period, it was Spain that they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.
persona3_adapted:
In the 1800s, Spain became a major center for making fans, just as the country's tourism industry was growing and Spanish culture was becoming popular across Europe. While traveling through Spain in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier noted, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan's important role in Spanish flamenco dance is another reason it became so closely tied to Spain in people's minds during the 1800s—and still is today.

Around the middle of the century, Spanish culture was very fashionable in Paris. This trend was called *Hispanolisme*. The Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, had a big influence on fashion and style. Spanish dance groups and musicians often performed in the city, and cutting-edge artists like Edouard Manet used them as subjects for their work. For some artists making fans at this time, Spain was the main inspiration. Both Manet and another artist named Degas chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.

CuratorialText: The theme of the bourgeois woman with her fan in a domestic setting proliferated in art of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book The Fan, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the role of fans to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or on ventures out, the handheld fan served as a companion, trusted protector, and elegant accoutrement.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1800s, many artists painted scenes of middle-class women holding fans in their homes. In 1882, French writer Octave Uzanne wrote a book called *The Fan*, where he told his female readers: "In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante." Whether at home or out and about, the handheld fan was more than just a stylish accessory—it was a companion, a trusted protector, and a sign of elegance.

CuratorialText: As fashion accessories, fans signaled facets of their user’s identity through their materials and stylistic form as well as in the way they were handled. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer cited by Uzanne. A Japanese uchiwa fan in the hands of the Impressionist Berthe Morisot or a plumed fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s sitters displayed their alignment with current taste and confirmed their social status.
persona3_adapted:
Fans weren't just for keeping cool—they were also a way for people to show who they were. The materials, the style, and even how someone held a fan could say a lot about their social status. As one woman quoted by writer Octave Uzanne put it, "One wave of the Fan is enough to tell the difference between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a commoner."

For example, when the Impressionist painter Berthe Morisot held a Japanese uchiwa fan, or when a woman in a painting by Mary Cassatt carried a feathered fan, it showed that they were up-to-date with the latest fashion. These choices also confirmed their place in society.

CuratorialText: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the seventeenth century to commemorate events and to remember visits to important sites. As travel increased in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, fans became popular as small, practical, and artistic objects that could serve as mementos. The Universal Exhibitions of the second half of the nineteenth century played a crucial role in promoting the French fan industry and exposing the European public to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were produced for each of these events, providing visitors with information and maps while also recording the remarkable buildings erected specifically for these occasions.
persona3_adapted:
Fans were some of the first souvenirs people ever bought. Starting in the 1600s, they were used to remember special events or trips to important places. As more people began to travel in the 1700s and 1800s, fans became popular because they were small, useful, and beautiful—perfect keepsakes.

The big world fairs (called Universal Exhibitions) in the late 1800s helped make French fan-making famous and introduced European visitors to fans from other countries. Special souvenir fans were made for each of these fairs. They gave visitors information and maps, and also showed pictures of the amazing new buildings built just for the event.

CuratorialText: This monumental work was one of a series of carved architectural supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most renowned master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-tiered composition embodies a formal dynamism that established Olowe's exceptional artistic reputation. Its principal personage, an equestrian warrior, is depicted frontally above a female caryatid with arms raised in three-quarter view. Through this contrast in their alignment, the figures at once reflect distinct attitudes while relating to each other fluidly. The compressed style of the upper half also contrasts with the greater degree of openness below. At the base two smaller figures radiate outward at oblique angles. In addition to the inventive dynamism of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and horse's headgear are articulated through deeply carved linear motifs. While innovative in its formal interpretation, the subject of this work is a classic emblem of regional leadership. In Yoruba art such equestrian figures identify their patrons with martial conquest achieved through physical might.
persona3_adapted:
This massive carved pillar was one of several designed to support the roof of an outdoor courtyard in a Yoruba palace. A king commissioned it from Olowe of Ise, the most famous master sculptor in Yoruba art history.

The carving has two main levels, and its lively, balanced design helped make Olowe's reputation. At the top, a warrior on horseback faces straight forward. Below him, a woman stands with her arms raised, shown at a slight angle. This contrast in how the two figures are positioned shows their different roles, yet they still feel connected to each other. The upper half is carved in a tighter, more compact style, while the lower half feels more open and spacious. At the very bottom, two smaller figures lean outward at angles.

Beyond the creative overall design, Olowe's work is also known for its careful attention to surface details. The mounted warrior holds a spear in one hand and a pistol in the other. His vest, saddle, and the horse's headgear are all decorated with deeply carved lines and patterns.

Although the carving's style is fresh and inventive, its subject is a classic symbol of local leadership. In Yoruba art, images of people on horseback connect their owners to military victory and physical strength.

CuratorialText: This saltcellar is both an extraordinary example of skilled workmanship and an artifact that epitomizes a singularly important convergence of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they were inspired to commission works of this kind for their patrons, which ingeniously combine both European and African aesthetics and forms. During this period, salt and pepper were costly commodities and elaborate receptacles were appropriate for their storage in princely homes. This work contains imagery relating to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. The four snakes, associated with mystical wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to regional traditions, dogs are considered spiritually astute animals able to see spirits and ghosts that are invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs, with teeth bared, hair bristling, and ears laid back, may relate to that ability. However, the level of animation in this scene could also derive from chivalric hunting scenes in European woodcuts, which were furnished to local African artists by their European patrons.
persona3_adapted:
This saltcellar is a remarkable example of skilled craftsmanship and a unique symbol of cultural exchange. In the late 1400s, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. They commissioned works like this for their wealthy patrons, blending European and African styles and designs.

At that time, salt and pepper were expensive luxuries, so elaborate containers like this were used to store them in noble homes. The carvings reflect early beliefs of the Temne and Bullom peoples. Four snakes, symbols of mystical wealth, face four growling dogs. In local traditions, dogs are seen as spiritually aware animals that can see spirits and ghosts invisible to humans. The dogs’ bared teeth, bristling fur, and flattened ears may represent this ability. However, the lively scene might also be inspired by European hunting images from woodcuts, which European patrons provided to African artists.

CuratorialText: By the early nineteenth century, Chokwe chiefs in the savanna of present-day Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola had become active trading partners with merchants from Europe and the New World. Profiting from commerce in ivory, rubber, wax, and African slaves, they emerged as important regional rulers whose prestige and power was reflected in the art they commissioned for their courts at this time. This figure depicts a Chokwe chief, or mwanangana, and epitomizes the balance of power and refinement that is characteristic of Chokwe court art. His large, spreading hands, muscular shoulders, and aggressive posture epitomize the strength and vigor of a ruler. A bulging, domelike forehead and large eyes suggest mental acuity and keen eyesight. The sweeping curves and swelling volumes of the distinctive headdress, rendered here in wood, depict an actual Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basketry. Horns of the tiny duiker antelope, which bear associations with hunting and the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery, are placed on the sides of the headdress. The chief holds a lamellophone, or "thumb piano," an instrument whose music often accompanies recitations of oral histories and the praise songs of important individuals. Instrument in hand, the chief is cast as a repository and guardian of historical and cultural knowledge. While depicting a nineteenth-century Chokwe chief, this sculpture also evokes the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga, a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. Cibinda Ilunga is said to have introduced new principles of governance to the Chokwe peoples, and figures such as this one honor the productive union of these two cultures.
persona3_adapted:
This carved wooden figure shows a Chokwe chief from the early 1800s. At that time, chiefs in what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola were powerful regional rulers. They grew wealthy by trading ivory, rubber, wax, and enslaved people with European and American merchants. Their status and authority were proudly displayed in the art they paid artists to create for their royal courts.

The chief in this sculpture has large, open hands, strong shoulders, and a bold stance. These features are meant to show his physical strength and energy as a leader. His large, rounded forehead and big eyes suggest he is smart and sharp-sighted. The tall, curved headdress is carved from wood but copies a real Chokwe crown made from cloth-covered basketwork. On each side of the headdress are small antelope horns. These horns are linked to hunting and to the dangerous, mysterious world of medicine and magic.

The chief is holding a "thumb piano," a small instrument that makes music when you pluck its metal keys. This kind of music often goes along with storytelling and songs that praise important people. By holding the instrument, the chief is shown as someone who protects and remembers the history and knowledge of his people.

Although this sculpture shows a 19th-century Chokwe chief, it also honors a legendary figure from the neighboring Luba kingdom: a hunter and culture hero named Cibinda Ilunga. According to tradition, he brought new ideas about how to govern to the Chokwe people. This artwork celebrates the positive blend of these two cultures.

CuratorialText: The Dogon live in the elevated rocky heights of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They rely on agriculture and manage to wrest subsistence crops from poor soil in an area that receives little rain. This monumental figure is one of the largest Dogon sculptures known. The region's dry climate has preserved Dogon works far longer than is usual for African wood sculpture, and radiocarbon testing of this work suggests that it may date as far back as the fifteenth century. Dogon sculpture is primarily concerned with the spirits responsible for the fertility of both land and people. These include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. While the exact functions and meanings of individual works often remain obscure, scholars agree that Dogon sculptures were created for shrines. The most distinctive subject rendered by Dogon sculptures is that of a single figure standing with raised arms. This posture has usually been interpreted as a gesture of prayer—an effort to link earth and heavens—and it has been suggested that it may represent an appeal for rain. This masterpiece of Dogon figurative art depicts a man with a well-modeled body in a naturalistic stance. The artist has carefully rendered the figure's musculature, anatomical detail, and body ornament. His stylized beard identifies him as an elder and an individual whose age and experience entitle him to participate in the most important religious, political, and social affairs of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that indicate his status, as well as a belt and neck pendants resembling leather talismans that also suggest his spiritual importance.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The Dogon people live in the rocky highlands of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They farm for a living, growing crops in poor soil with very little rainfall. This large statue is one of the biggest Dogon sculptures ever found. Because the region is so dry, Dogon wooden artworks have lasted much longer than most African wood sculptures. Radiocarbon testing suggests this piece might date back to the 1400s.

Dogon sculpture focuses on spirits that help the land and people stay fertile. These spirits include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. We don't always know exactly what each sculpture was used for, but experts agree they were made for shrines. The most common subject in Dogon sculpture is a single figure standing with arms raised. This pose is usually seen as a prayer gesture—a way to connect earth and sky—and some think it might be a request for rain.

This masterpiece shows a man with a well-shaped body in a natural pose. The artist carefully carved the muscles, body details, and decorations. The figure's stylized beard tells us he is an elder—someone old and wise enough to take part in the most important religious, political, and social events of Dogon society. He wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that show his high status. He also has a belt and neck pendants that look like leather charms, suggesting his spiritual importance.

CuratorialText: When works from Equatorial Africa in this refined style began to enter Western consciousness in the early twentieth century, they were a great enigma to art critics. Many speculated about the sources of their exotic aesthetic and even proposed possible Asian influence, though the art form was in fact indigenous to southern Gabon. Such masks were worn by virtuosic male performers of a stilt dance called "mukudj," which involved towering impressively while executing complex choreography and astonishing feats of acrobatics. The creator of a "mukudj" mask would attempt to capture the likeness of the most beautiful woman in his community. The subject of this particular idealized and stylized portrait was embellished in classic nineteenth-century fashion with a coiffure composed of a central lobe and two lateral tresses and with cicatrization motifs on the forehead and temples. Kaolin taken from riverbeds, which was associated with healing and with a spiritual, ancestral realm of existence, was applied to the surface of the face. By using this material, the artist both celebrated the beauty of a mortal woman and transformed her into a transcendent being.
persona3_adapted:
In the early 1900s, when artworks from Equatorial Africa in this elegant style first became known in the West, art critics found them deeply puzzling. Many guessed about where their unusual beauty came from, and some even suggested they might have been influenced by Asian art. But in fact, this art form was originally from southern Gabon. 

Masks like this one were worn by highly skilled male dancers who performed a stilt dance called "mukudj." The dancers would tower above the crowd while doing complex dance moves and amazing acrobatic stunts. The person who made a "mukudj" mask tried to create a portrait of the most beautiful woman in his community. 

This particular mask shows an idealized and stylized version of a woman, decorated in the classic style of the 1800s. Her hair is arranged with a central section and two side braids, and there are scar patterns on her forehead and temples. The artist also applied kaolin—a white clay taken from riverbeds—to the face. In local tradition, kaolin was linked to healing and to a spiritual world of ancestors. By using this material, the artist both honored the beauty of a real woman and turned her into something beyond ordinary life.

CuratorialText: A lü me was typically commissioned by a man of standing in honor of a favorite wife. The subject’s distinguishing traits, including her facial features, cicatrices, and braided coiffure, would be captured by the artist through direct observation. The unveiling of a completed figure was celebrated with lavish feasting sponsored by its patron. Named for their subjects and stored in special baskets, such portraits were subsequently displayed on special occasions. This example was carved by celebrated early 20th century carver, Zlan of Belewale, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan’s calling was revealed to him as an adolescent through the miraculous appearance of his signature tool, an adze, falling from the branches of an oil palm. The sculptor honed his skills on soft tuber roots and soapstone before his mastery of wood earned him the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), meaning "creator" or "god." As the most sought-after Dan artist of the first half of the twentieth century, Zlan mentored scores of apprentices. Many of these trainees were sent by regional chiefs to acquire his expertise for their own communities. Through his efforts, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became an important carving center. Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and figurative portraits, have been collected globally. Defying the convention that woodcarving was a man’s vocation, Sonzlanwon (literally, "a snail, if god agrees") collaborated with her husband on many of his greatest works. She accompanied him on commissions for important patrons—the intense concentration required to execute these was such that they often worked side by side in seclusion. Sonzlanwon is credited with blocking out Zlan’s works’ initial form, using wood felled by his apprentices. Following his refinement of the contours, she handled final detailing, including the braided raffia-palm coiffures added to figures and ladles. As Zlan aged, it is believed that Sonzlanwon’s role in their collaboration expanded. Documentation of her contributions derives from interviews conducted by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from remembrances of surviving relatives and acquaintances.
persona3_adapted:
In traditional Wè culture, a man of high status would sometimes commission a special carved figure to honor his favorite wife. The artist would study the woman closely to capture her unique features—like her face, any scar patterns, and her braided hairstyle. When the carving was finished, the man who paid for it would throw a big celebration with lots of food.
