Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This broken piece is shaped like the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storage room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud. The fort was probably used to store treasure and goods that the Assyrians took from other places during their military campaigns.

When the Assyrian Empire was finally defeated at the end of the 600s B.C., the royal buildings at Nimrud were looted and burned. The lion's face was likely blackened by fire at that time.

The back of the piece is flat, which suggests it was originally attached to a piece of wooden furniture, like the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and mouth area are decorated with small carved dots. The eyes are carved deeply, probably to hold precious stones or colored glass that have since been lost. On the right side at the back of the face, there is a pattern of white triangles outlined with raised lines. This may have been meant to show the lion's mane.

Scenes of Assyrian kings hunting lions appear on stone carvings from Assyrian palaces. Because this piece shows the same subject, experts believe it was made in the Assyrian style.

The palaces and storage rooms at Nimrud were built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II. They once held thousands of carved ivory objects. Most of these ivories were used as decorations on furniture or as small precious items like boxes. Some were carved in the same style as the large stone carvings that lined the walls of the Northwest Palace. But most of the ivories show images and styles from North Syria and the Phoenician city-states.

Ivories in the Phoenician style often use images from Egyptian art, such as sphinxes (mythical creatures with a lion's body and a human head) and figures wearing Egyptian-style crowns. They also use detailed carving techniques like openwork (cutting all the way through the ivory) and colored glass inlays.

Ivories in the North Syrian style tend to show thicker, heavier figures in more active poses. They are carved as solid pieces with fewer added decorations.

However, some pieces don't fit neatly into any of these three styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute (gifts from weaker states) or as loot from conquered enemies. A few may have been made in workshops at Nimrud itself. The ivory tusks used to make these objects almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt. Elephants did live in several river valleys in Syria, but they were hunted to extinction by the end of the 700s B.C.

CuratorialText: Of the many legacies left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is paramount. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as pictographs and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn in the clay with a pointed implement. Circular impressions alongside the pictographs represented numerical symbols. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was written by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a fine surface for writing, and when fired the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used primarily as a means of recording and storing economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the absence of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing that appears on this tablet, the imagery of the cylinder seal, which was incompletely impressed on both faces and the edges of the tablet before it was inscribed, also records information. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression depicts a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. He is the so-called priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.
persona3_adapted:
Of all the achievements of the ancient civilizations in southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is the most important. Around 3200 BCE, written language began to develop in this region. It started as simple picture symbols, called pictographs, and later evolved into abstract shapes known as cuneiform.

The pictographs on this tablet are called proto-cuneiform. They were drawn into soft clay using a pointed tool. The round marks next to the pictures are numbers. Cuneiform, which means "wedge-shaped," was made by pressing a reed pen with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. When clay dried to a firm but not fully hard state, it made a good surface for writing. If the clay was then fired (baked), the writing became permanent.

Early writing was mainly used to record and store economic information—like keeping track of goods and payments. This tablet most likely records grain distributed by a large temple. However, because early texts don't include verbs, it's hard to know for sure what they mean.

In addition to the writing on this tablet, there is also an image from a cylinder seal. A cylinder seal is a small carved stone that, when rolled across wet clay, leaves a picture. This seal was pressed onto both sides and the edges of the tablet before the writing was added. The original seal itself has not survived.

The seal shows a male figure leading two dogs on a leash, while hunting or herding wild boars in a marsh full of reeds. This figure is called the "priest-king," a title based on his clothing and pose. Here, he appears in the role of a good shepherd—someone who protects his flocks from wild animals.

CuratorialText:
In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled on a variety of objects made of clay. When seals were impressed on tablets or tablet cases the seal impressions served to identify the authority responsible for what was written in the documents, much as a signature does today. When seals were impressed on sealings — lumps of clay that were used to secure doors and the lids of storage jars— the seal impressions served to identify their owner and protect against unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made of durable materials, particularly stone, but also metal and fired clay. Perforated through the middle like a bead, seals were also believed to have apotropaic, or protective, functions and were worn as jewelry or pinned on garments.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene is composed of two basic groupings that form an overall continuous design unified by the stylized landscape setting. In one group, two tall trees flank a hunter grasping an ibex. Above the man a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by the seal engraver expanded to include a variety of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas, fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms were cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge of the seal carver was to create a design that would maintain its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression one can see how the rolled out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in a fragmentary state, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.
persona3_adapted:
In ancient Mesopotamia, people used small cylinder-shaped seals made of stone, metal, or baked clay. These seals could be rolled across soft clay objects. When pressed onto clay tablets or tablet cases, the seal's design worked like a signature—it showed who was responsible for the document. When rolled onto lumps of clay used to seal doors or storage jars, the design proved who owned the item and helped prevent unauthorized opening. Many of these seals have survived because they were made from durable materials. They were often pierced through the middle like a bead, and people wore them as jewelry or pinned them to their clothes, believing they offered protection.

The image you see here is a modern copy of the seal's design, so you can see the full pattern. The scene has two main parts that flow together into one continuous picture, set in a stylized landscape. In one part, two tall trees stand on either side of a hunter who is grabbing an ibex (a type of wild goat). Above the man, there is an inscription in cuneiform (an ancient writing system) that gives the seal owner's name, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his job title, cupbearer. In the other part, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other with three trees between them. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when seal carvers began to include more mythological, thematic, and story-based subjects. Here, the very old theme of hunting is shown in a clearly defined landscape. If there was any connection between this picture and the written text, we no longer know what it meant.

Both sacred and everyday ideas, which were central to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian people, were shown in these tiny carvings. The carver used a technique called intaglio, where the design is cut into the stone, so that when rolled, the raised parts create the image. The carver's challenge was to make a design that would look balanced and clear whether it was rolled out only halfway on a small surface or twice as long on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression, you can see where the design starts to repeat. Unlike much of the art from the ancient Near East that survives only in broken pieces, cylinder seals are special because they appear almost exactly as they did to the people who used them long ago.

CuratorialText: This type of figurine known as an eye idol, made of stone and having incised eyes, has been excavated at Tell Brak, where thousands were found in a building now called the Eye Temple. They were probably dedicated there as offerings. Many are incised with multiple sets of eyes, others with jewelry, and still others with representations of "children"—smaller eyes and body carved on the body of the larger idol. Wide eyes demonstrate attentiveness to the gods in much of Mesopotamian art.
persona3_adapted:
These small stone figurines are called "eye idols." They were found in large numbers at an ancient site called Tell Brak, in a building that archaeologists now call the Eye Temple. It's likely that people left them there as offerings.

Many of these idols have more than one set of eyes carved into them. Some also show jewelry, and others include tiny figures that may represent children—small eyes and bodies carved onto the larger idol. In ancient Mesopotamian art, wide-open eyes were a way of showing that a person was paying close attention to the gods.

CuratorialText: Some of the most elaborate representations of females in the art of the ancient Near East are images of divine and cult figures whose association with certain aspects of life made them essential to the welfare of humanity. Fertility, procreation, and the growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts identified with female divinities. Representations of nude females in clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most obvious expression of these concepts, and such figures appear throughout antiquity in many regions. This striking example in clay from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran is hollow and probably served as a cult vessel as well as a sacred image.
persona3_adapted:
Some of the most detailed images of women in ancient Near Eastern art show goddesses and religious figures. These figures were linked to important parts of life, like fertility, having children, and the growth of crops and animals. Because of these connections, people believed they were essential for human well-being.

Simple statues of naked women, made from clay, stone, or metal, are the clearest and most direct way to express these ideas. Such statues were common in many ancient cultures over long periods of time.

This striking clay example comes from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran. It is hollow, which suggests it was used both as a sacred image and as a container for religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Distinctive clay female figures like this one were produced at sites belonging to the Halaf culture. This example displays a strong stylization with an emphasis on the sexual features. She sits with her large thighs extended, supporting her breasts with her arms; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing; in other figures of this type when the head is intact, it is elongated into a large noselike projection but otherwise is featureless. Remains of paint may represent jewelry. The meaning of such representations is unknown but may be connected with fertility. The stylized depiction of the nude female form remained an artistic convention in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for several millennia.
persona3_adapted: This clay figure of a woman comes from the Halaf culture, an ancient society that created similar figures at several of their settlements. The figure is highly simplified and focuses on sexual features. She sits with her large thighs spread apart, and her arms support her breasts. She has no hands or feet. Her head is missing, but in other figures like this one that still have heads, the head is stretched into a long, nose-like shape with no other facial features. Traces of paint may show that she once wore jewelry. We don’t know exactly what these figures meant, but they may have been linked to ideas about fertility. This way of showing the female body—simplified and nude—remained a common artistic style in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean region for thousands of years.

CuratorialText: Furniture inlaid with carved ivory plaques was highly prized by the Assyrian kings. During the ninth to seventh centuries B.C., vast quantities of luxury goods, often embellished with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles, accumulated in Assyrian palaces, much of it as booty or tribute. This object belongs to a group of plaques depicting animals and stylized plants. They were made by master carvers in a delicate openwork technique characteristic of Phoenician ivory carving. However, the style and subjects depicted have close parallels on stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf, in northern Syria, and a debate exists over which tradition produced these fine panels.
persona3_adapted:
This piece of furniture was decorated with carved ivory panels, and it was considered very valuable by the Assyrian kings. Between the 800s and 600s B.C., huge amounts of luxury goods—often decorated with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles—were brought into Assyrian palaces. Much of it came from war or was given as tribute.

This object is part of a set of panels showing animals and stylized plants. Skilled carvers made them using a delicate technique that involved cutting through the ivory, which is typical of Phoenician ivory work. However, the style and subjects look very similar to stone carvings found at Tell Halaf in northern Syria. Because of this, experts disagree about which tradition actually created these fine panels.

CuratorialText: This statuette group, carved in the round, was found with five other statuettes with similar imagery. The six statuettes were excavated in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. Those found just below the niches could have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory plinths and exhibited in the arched niches, perhaps arranged in a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A frontally facing male, striding to the right with his lower body in profile, grasps the horns of an oryx (a species of desert-dwelling antelope) that strides behind him. The male figure’s eyes, necklace, and armlet were carved to receive colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt belted with a long sash, embroidered with decoration including two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is rendered with short incisions, sits erect on his left shoulder and grasps his short curly hair. Although his right arm does not survive, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona3_adapted:
This small carved group, along with five others showing similar scenes, was discovered in and just below two arched wall niches in a room at Fort Shalmaneser. This was a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud, likely used to store loot and gifts collected by the Assyrian army during their military campaigns. The pieces found below the niches may have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during Assyria's final defeat at the end of the 600s B.C.

Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory bases and displayed in the arched niches, possibly arranged to show a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as gifts to the Assyrian king. The carving shows a man facing forward, walking to the right with his lower body turned sideways, as he grips the horns of an oryx (a type of desert antelope) walking behind him. The man's eyes, necklace, and armband were carved to hold colored glass or semi-precious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt with a long sash belt, decorated with patterns including two mythical fire-spitting serpents, zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is shown with short carved lines, sits upright on his left shoulder and holds his short curly hair. Although his right arm is missing, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder.

Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud held thousands of carved ivory pieces. Most of these ivories were used as furniture decorations or small precious objects like boxes. While some were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian stone reliefs that lined the walls of the Northwest Palace, most show images and styles related to the art of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician-style ivories are known for using images linked to Egyptian art, such as mythical creatures and figures wearing Egyptian-style crowns, and for using detailed carving techniques like openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian-style ivories tend to show heavier figures in more active poses, carved as solid panels with fewer added decorations. However, some pieces don't fit neatly into any of these three styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as gifts from allied states or as loot from conquered enemies, though some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud itself. The ivory tusks used as raw material almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did live in several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the 700s B.C.

CuratorialText:
This bronze monumental statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues of the third century preserved today. It is a reminder that many of the disembodied portrait heads displayed in these galleries were once attached to freestanding sculptures, whose original appearances were undoubtedly quite different. Portraits of the emperor served the highly important function in imperial propaganda of presenting the ruler to the public and of projecting the personality with which he wished to be perceived. Leadership and military strength are evoked in the heroic nudity of the figure and in the pose, which recalls the famous statue of Alexander the Great with the Lance by Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head represents the emperor with brutish realism. Trebonianus likely would have cradled a parazonium, or short sword, in his left arm, and held a spear in his upraised right hand.
The statue has undergone several campaigns of restoration since its discovery in the early nineteenth century and was examined in great detail as part of the conservation treatment in preparation for its display here. Visual examination inside and out, combined with x-radiography, made possible the clear identification of ancient and restored areas, which are indicated in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Despite the discrepancy in scale, the head belongs to the body. Although the mantle draped over his left shoulder is a modern restoration, cast edges beneath it confirm that the statue had a similar embellishment in antiquity. The left foot with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot appears to be ancient but may not belong.
persona3_adapted:
This bronze statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues from the 200s AD that still exist today. It reminds us that many of the disconnected heads you see in these galleries were once attached to full-body statues, which probably looked very different when they were whole.

Statues of the emperor were a key part of Roman propaganda. They showed the ruler to the public and helped project the image he wanted people to see. This statue suggests leadership and military strength. The body is shown naked, like a hero, and the pose copies a famous statue of Alexander the Great holding a spear. But unlike the perfect, idealized body, the emperor's face is shown with rough, realistic features. Trebonianus likely held a short sword in his left arm and a spear in his raised right hand.

Since it was found in the early 1800s, the statue has been repaired several times. Before it was put on display here, experts carefully studied it, looking inside and out and using X-rays. This helped them clearly tell which parts are original and which were added later. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Even though the head is a different size, it does belong to this body. The cloth draped over his left shoulder is a modern addition, but marks underneath show the statue had something similar in ancient times. The left foot, with its fancy open-fronted boot, seems to be ancient but may not be original to this statue.

CuratorialText: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (Seasons), maidens closely associated with the fertility of the earth and the nurturing of children. The original bronze was erected in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. Rarely can an ancient monument be dated so exactly. We know from literary sources that the cult of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently found Panathenaic amphorae dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was represented as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.
persona3_adapted:
**Eirene: Goddess of Peace**

Eirene was the daughter of Zeus (the king of the gods) and Themis (the goddess of justice). She was one of the three Horai, or Seasons—young women linked to the earth's fertility and the care of children.

The original bronze statue of Eirene was set up in the marketplace of Athens, called the Agora, sometime between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. It's rare for historians to be able to date an ancient monument this precisely. We know from ancient writings that the worship of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374. Also, six recently discovered jars called Panathenaic amphorae, dated to 360/359, show an image of the statue. A Greek traveler named Pausanius saw the statue in the Agora in the second century A.D. and wrote that it was made by the sculptor Kephisodotos.

In the statue, Eirene is shown as a beautiful young woman. She wears a peplos (a simple, long dress) and a himation (a cloak). In her right hand, she holds a scepter (a royal staff). On her left arm, she carries a young child named Ploutos, who represents wealth, along with a cornucopia (a horn-shaped basket overflowing with fruits and grains). This image reminds viewers of Demeter, the main goddess of farming and plenty, who was also the mother of Ploutos.

CuratorialText: Many private summer villas were located along the coast near Naples. One of the most sumptuous must have been the villa built by Agrippa, friend of the emperor Augustus and husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood overlooking the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905 after its accidental discovery during work on a railway. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms and the Archaeological Museum at Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C. and his son, Agrippa Postumus became the villa's proprietor in 11 B.C. as inscriptions found there indicate. The frescoes must have been painted during renovations begun at the time. Painted by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest existing examples of Roman wall painting. The so-called Black Room was one of a sequence of bedrooms facing south toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. The source of light was a wide doorway giving onto a terrace or promenade. This ambiguous and sophisticated decoration is a masterpiece of the so-called third style of Roman wall painting, which flourished during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful rendition of architectural motif. A low red dado serves as the base from which a skeleton of thin white columns appears to rise against a black background. There almost weightless columns support pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow cornice that runs around the room. They were embellished with jewel-like decorations. On the back wall tiny swans, the bird of Apollo, patron god of Augustus, perch improbably on threadlike spirals, and yellow panels with Egyptianizing motifs must have brought to mind the recent annexation of Egypt after the death of Cleopatra in 30 B.C. This architectural scheme creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear at once to be flat and to dissolve into limitless space. Tiny landscape vignettes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Burnished to a high polish, these walls must have appeared magical indeed when illuminated by lamps at night.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Many wealthy Romans built summer homes along the coast near Naples. One of the most impressive must have been the villa owned by Agrippa, a close friend of Emperor Augustus and the husband of his daughter, Julia. The villa overlooked the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase.

Workers accidentally discovered the villa while building a railway, and it was partially dug up between 1903 and 1905. The wall decorations from four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum got sections from three rooms, and the rest went to the Archaeological Museum in Naples.

Agrippa died in 12 B.C., and inscriptions found at the site show that his son, Agrippa Postumus, became the owner in 11 B.C. The paintings were likely created during renovations that started around that time. Artists working for the emperor's household painted them, and they are now considered some of the finest surviving examples of Roman wall painting.

The so-called Black Room was one of several bedrooms that faced south, looking down the mountain slope toward the sea. Light came through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walking area. The room's decoration is clever and complex—a masterpiece of what art historians call the "third style" of Roman wall painting, which was popular during Augustus's rule.

The design plays with architectural ideas. A low red base runs along the bottom of the wall. Above it, thin white columns seem to rise against a black background. These almost weightless columns support small pavilions, candelabras, tripods, and a narrow ledge that goes around the room. They are decorated with jewel-like details.

On the back wall, tiny swans—birds sacred to Apollo, the god who protected Augustus—perch impossibly on thin, spiral lines. Yellow panels with Egyptian-style designs would have reminded viewers of Egypt's recent takeover by Rome after Cleopatra's death in 30 B.C.

This architectural setup creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind seem both flat and like they dissolve into endless space. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of the blackness. Polished to a high shine, these walls must have looked magical when lit by lamps at night.

CuratorialText:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)
Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda This fresco from the Imperial villa at Boscotrecase depicts two consecutive events from the myth of Perseus and Andromeda. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the ketos, a snaky sea monster painted in a brilliant blue-green palette. The creature raises his head with gigantic open jaws and frightful teeth toward Andromeda, who stands with outstretched arms in the center of the panel. One hand appears to be chained to the crag; the other elegantly placed on the rocks. Perseus flies in from the left with his lyre in one hand, winged shoes on his feet, and a windblown cloak over his shoulder. In the upper right portion of the fresco, he is greeted by Andromeda's grateful father, a scene that alludes to the myth's happy endingâ€“the marriage of hero and princess. The fortunes of love and the ever-present sea are the themes that link this fresco and that of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same cubiculum at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both frescoes also unifies the disparate episodes combined in each painting, and must have brought a sense of coolness to the room.
persona3_adapted:
From the Villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)

**Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda**

This wall painting comes from a Roman emperor's country house at Boscotrecase. It shows two moments from the ancient story of Perseus and Andromeda.

In the first scene, Perseus is about to save Andromeda from a sea monster called a *ketos*. The creature is painted in bright blue-green colors. It lifts its huge head, showing its giant open mouth and scary teeth, toward Andromeda. She stands in the middle of the painting with her arms stretched out. One hand seems to be chained to a rock; the other rests gracefully on the stones.

Perseus flies in from the left side. He carries a lyre (a stringed instrument) in one hand, wears winged shoes on his feet, and has a cloak blowing over his shoulder.

In the upper right part of the painting, we see a second scene: Andromeda's grateful father welcomes Perseus. This hints at the happy ending of the story—the hero and princess get married.

The themes of love's fortunes and the always-present sea connect this painting to another one from the same room at Boscotrecase (showing Polyphemus and Galatea). Both paintings share a see-through blue-green background, which helps tie together the different scenes in each picture. This cool color would have made the room feel refreshing.

CuratorialText: This noble image of a woman brings to mind the philosopher Aristotle's description of commonly held beliefs about the dead: "In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors" (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.
persona3_adapted: This impressive portrait of a woman recalls what the philosopher Aristotle said about how people commonly view the dead. He wrote: "We believe that those who have passed away are blessed and happy. We also think it is wrong to say anything false or insulting about them, because we feel they have become better and greater than us." (From *Of the Soul*, quoted in Plutarch's *A Letter to Apollonius*). The woman is shown larger than life, sitting on a chair that looks like a throne, which gives her an almost heroic presence.

CuratorialText: A stylized octopus flanked by fish covers each side of this stirrup jar, named for the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often reflects Minoan-inspired themes and techniques that originated on the island of Crete. Animated marine motifs adopted from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels in about 1500 BCE and were initially rendered in a very naturalistic manner. Here, the symmetrical composition and abstract depiction of the sea creatures are characteristically Mycenaean. Such jars were commonly used to transport liquids.
persona3_adapted:
This jar is called a "stirrup jar" because its handles look like the stirrups on a saddle. On each side, you can see a stylized octopus surrounded by fish.

The pottery of the Mycenaean people was often inspired by the art of the Minoan civilization, which came from the island of Crete. Around 1500 BCE, Mycenaean artists started using lively sea-life designs borrowed from the Minoans. At first, these designs were very realistic.

But here, the octopus and fish are arranged in a balanced, symmetrical pattern, and they are shown in a more abstract way. This style is typical of Mycenaean art. Jars like this one were commonly used for carrying liquids.

CuratorialText:
On the shoulder, women dancing between musicians playing flute and lyre
On the body, wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of an Attic wedding. The bridal couple with the best man behind them sit in a cart drawn by two donkeys. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other members of the procession are on foot. The woman in the lead holds two torches, indicating that the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the bridegroom's house where a woman, probably the mother of the groom, awaits. The architecture of the house is carefully indicated; the white columns of the porch may be painted wood.
persona3_adapted:
On the vase's shoulder, women are shown dancing between musicians playing a flute and a stringed instrument called a lyre.

On the main body of the vase, there is a wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete picture we have of a wedding in ancient Athens. The bride and groom sit in a cart pulled by two donkeys, with the best man behind them. A mule cart carrying four guests follows behind. Other people in the procession walk on foot. The woman at the front carries two torches, which tells us the scene takes place at night. The procession is heading toward the groom's house, where a woman—likely the groom's mother—is waiting. The house's architecture is shown in detail; the white columns on the porch may be made of painted wood.

CuratorialText: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.
persona3_adapted:
This ornate and remarkably well-preserved Roman marble coffin, called a sarcophagus, came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort. It was once displayed at their country home, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus tells us it was installed there in 1733.

While the back is rough and unfinished, the sides and front are covered with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief—meaning they stand out prominently from the stone. At the center sits the god Dionysos on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures representing the four Seasons (from left to right: Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). These figures are unusual because the Seasons are usually shown as women, but here they appear as strong young men.

Around these five central figures are other smaller figures and objects related to the worship of Dionysos. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two more groups of large figures, mixed with smaller ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is shown lying on the ground, accompanied by a satyr (a mythical half-man, half-goat creature) and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure—likely meant to represent a river god—reclines in front of two winged youths, who may be two additional Seasons.

This sarcophagus is a superb example of Roman funerary art, showing the great skill of the workshop that carved it. The marble came from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was carved. Only a very wealthy and powerful person could have afforded such a piece, and it was likely made for a member of one of Rome's old aristocratic families.

However, the subjects—the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons—probably had no special meaning for the person buried inside. It seems the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in a museum in Kassel, Germany, has the same layout of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, though the carving style is quite different.

On this sarcophagus, the figures are carved in high relief, creating many areas of light and shadow that make the crowded scene easy to explore with your eyes. You should also imagine that certain details were once highlighted with color and even gold, making the whole design a stunning visual achievement. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although this one is missing its lid, the fact that it was discovered in the early 1700s and soon placed in Badminton House means it has been preserved almost completely, with only a few small parts now missing.

CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.
persona3_adapted:
On the neck of the vase, one side shows a battle between centaurs (half-human, half-horse creatures) and Lapiths (a legendary Greek tribe). The other side shows young men and women. Around the body of the vase, there's a scene of an Amazonomachy—a battle between Greeks and Amazons.

The ancient Greeks almost never made art about real historical events or people from their own time. Even though Greek writings from the 400s B.C. show that they knew how important their victory in the Persian Wars was, artists didn't bother to show major events or famous figures. Instead, they preferred to paint grand, mythical battles between Greeks and enemies from the east, especially the Amazons. The Amazons were a legendary race of warrior women who supposedly lived far to the east and north.

In Athens, during the first half of the 400s B.C., the most famous Amazon battle scenes were huge wall paintings that decorated two important buildings: the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (also called the Painted Portico). These paintings had a big influence on later art, and you can see that influence in this vase and in the nearby krater (a large mixing bowl for wine), labeled 06.286.86.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 paintings that are still lifes. This type of painting became popular in the 1500s, starting with artists from Flanders (now part of Belgium). 

A still life shows objects arranged together—like shells, books, food, drinks, dishes, skulls, or dead animals. You might also see flowers, insects, or even a person in the background. The name "still life" comes from the idea that everything in the painting is perfectly still, as if time has stopped.

Over time, artists began to focus on different subjects within this style. For example, Rachel Ruysch was known for painting flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to create scenes with food, cups, and plates. 

There is also a special type called a "vanitas" still life. These paintings often include a skull and remind us that life is short and material things don't last forever.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.
persona3_adapted:
**Orange Above!**  
In 1815, the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time when King William I was crowned. He was the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier.  

King William I focused heavily on building up the country’s economy. He had canals and roads constructed, and he invested in trade, shipping, and industry. But because he was so focused on money and business, he paid less attention to other matters of government.  

This caused growing unhappiness in the Southern Netherlands. People there rose up in revolt, and in 1831, Belgium broke away and became its own country. King William I tried to stop this, but he couldn’t win back the territory. After years of struggle, he finally recognized Belgium as an independent nation in 1839.  

Disappointed by this outcome, he gave up the throne a year later. His son, William II, became king in his place.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum has the oldest collection of clothing in the Netherlands, and it tells many fascinating stories. The collection includes clothes for women, men, and children. Each piece reveals something about the person who wore it and their personal style, how the garment was made, and the local and global influences that shaped it. Many items were made especially for a special event, while others show how technology changed the way clothes were mass-produced. From the 17th-century Frisian branch of the Dutch royal family to designs by Yves Saint Laurent, the collection highlights important moments in Dutch and international fashion. It offers a remarkable look at how fashion has evolved over the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new style of art appeared. It was created by artists who were tired of the old trend of copying styles from earlier centuries. This fresh style, called Art Nouveau (which means "new art" in French), focused on shapes found in nature—like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The key features were smooth, flowing lines, simple geometric patterns, and designs that weren't perfectly balanced. Starting in cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau quickly became popular across Western Europe and the United States. This period lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, and Art Nouveau could be seen in everything from paintings and buildings to furniture and everyday objects.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona3_adapted:
Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. He started out as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, and later became a well-known painter. He followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem, a city in the Netherlands. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he helped bring a new artistic style called Mannerism to the region. This style was originally developed by Bartholomäus Spranger. You can recognize Mannerism by its muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads, all arranged in complex compositions.

During a trip to Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of Italian masters. When he returned to the Netherlands, he moved away from Mannerism and adopted a more classicist style, which focused on balanced, classical proportions. His engravings, in particular, were technically very complex and admired across Europe. Goltzius's body of work offers a clear picture of how Dutch painting was evolving around the year 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.
persona3_adapted: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606–1669) knew from a young age that he wanted to be a painter. He started learning his craft with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden, then continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt didn't like to follow strict rules. Instead, he experimented freely with bold compositions, the way he handled paint, and how he showed different textures. He made his scenes more dramatic by using strong contrasts between light and dark. His early works are painted with a lot of detail; later, his brushstrokes became looser and more free. He often received important jobs, including his most famous and largest painting, *The Night Watch*. Rather than painting a still, formal group portrait, Rembrandt captured a lively moment where everyone is shown in action. Besides this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum owns 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and about 1,300 prints by Rembrandt.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.
persona3_adapted:
Johannes Vermeer (1632–1675) is one of the most famous painters in the world today, but surprisingly, we know very little about his life. What we do know is that he lived in the Dutch city of Delft, worked as an art dealer, raised a large family, and, of course, painted.

In his early career, Vermeer created paintings based on stories from the Bible and ancient mythology. Some of his other early works show the influence of a group of artists known as the Utrecht Caravaggists—painters who were inspired by the dramatic use of light and shadow seen in the work of the Italian artist Caravaggio.

Vermeer mostly painted indoor scenes and what are called "tronies"—which are portrait-like studies of people, not meant to be specific individuals. His most famous works are quiet, intimate scenes of everyday life: a woman reading a letter, someone doing a household chore. These paintings all share a sense of peaceful calm, and sometimes even a bit of mystery.

People admire Vermeer for his amazing use of light, his rich colors, and his incredible attention to detail. During his lifetime, he achieved a certain level of fame and could sell his paintings for high prices. But after he died, his work was largely forgotten. It wasn’t until the mid-1800s that art lovers rediscovered him and brought his paintings back into the spotlight.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.
persona3_adapted:
Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) was one of the most successful artists of her time. She specialized in painting flowers, and her still lifes are incredibly detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises—they all look almost exactly like the real thing. Her work shows a deep understanding of nature.

Ruysch learned about plants and flowers at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, was a professor. She also studied his collection of preserved flowers and insects. She learned the skills of painting from a still-life artist named Willem van Aelst.

In 1701, Ruysch became the first woman to join Pictura, an artists' society in The Hague. From 1708 to 1716, she worked as a court painter for a German ruler, Elector John William of the Palatinate. This was a very respected position. He wasn't the only one who admired her work. People from other countries also collected her flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.
persona3_adapted:
Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) grew up in a family of artists—her father, Johann Georg Schwartze, was a painter. She became a talented portrait artist, working in drawing, printmaking, and painting. She learned the basics in her father's studio and later studied art in Munich and Paris, where her natural skill really took off. When her father died unexpectedly, Schwartze was only 23 years old, but she stepped up to support her family financially.

Her painting style is bold and energetic. She used quick brushstrokes and broad areas of color, which made her portraits feel full of personality—and also very flattering to her subjects. People loved her work, and she became more and more famous, both in the Netherlands and abroad. By around 1900, she was the most successful portrait artist in the country, painting members of the Dutch royal family. She worked fast and produced hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint over her career. The Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam now holds many of her artist portraits in its collection.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.
persona3_adapted:
If you were walking through Amsterdam in the late 1800s, you might have spotted George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). This painter spent days wandering his beloved city with a sketchbook in hand. He called himself "the painter of the people" and focused on drawing and painting everyday city residents—ordinary folks going about their lives.

Breitner also used a camera to capture scenes of city life. He then brought a photographic style into his paintings, often showing figures cut off at the edges of the frame, like a snapshot. This approach, along with his thick brushstrokes and smudgy paint, wasn't appreciated by everyone. Journalists criticized his work, but fans loved it. Even today, Breitner is remembered as one of the most famous Impressionist painters of his era.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.
persona3_adapted: Van Gogh drew a lot and got his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, who was a famous painter and also a relative by marriage. In his early years, Van Gogh mostly drew and painted scenes of peasants, using dark colors. After a short time studying in Antwerp, he moved to Paris. There, he was influenced by two new styles of painting: Impressionism and Pointillism. He started using short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors placed next to each other. In 1888, he moved to sunny southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health problems and was sometimes in the hospital, but he kept painting. It was only after his death in 1890 that his work became known to the public and he became famous around the world.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.
persona3_adapted:
Adam van Vianen (1568/69–1627) was born into a family of silversmiths in Utrecht, Netherlands. Because of this, his future career was pretty much set from the start. Around 1590, he likely passed his master's exam, which allowed him to work on his own and join the local blacksmiths' guild.

The objects he created are full of imagination, decorated with curvy, ear-shaped patterns and forms that seem to tell a story. In 1614, the Amsterdam guild asked him to make a jug in memory of his brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had recently died. This jug became a tribute to Paulus’s work and creativity. For the first time in Van Vianen’s work, the decoration—not the object’s shape—took center stage.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. persona3_adapted: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. He trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, and later became a well-known painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, along with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he brought a dramatic style called Mannerism to the Netherlands. This style is easy to spot: it features muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in busy, detailed compositions. CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

In 2012, visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (born 1972) traveled to the village of Pikin Slee, located deep in the rainforest of Suriname. The people who live there have no running water, electricity, roads, or internet. Even though the village is very remote, you can still see the influence of the modern world. For example, the community uses mobile phones and plastic household items.

The photo series Sassen created in Pikin Slee shows the beauty of everyday life in a style that focuses on shapes and patterns, rather than realistic details. Most of the photos are in black and white, but they include bright bursts of color—a signature feature of Sassen's earlier work.

In 2013, this series became part of the Rijksmuseum's collection. It added a modern touch to the museum's existing collection of photographs from Suriname, which had mostly been historical up to that point.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.
persona3_adapted: William of Orange (1533–1584) was a powerful nobleman who became the leader of the Dutch Revolt and is remembered as the 'father of the country.' When he was 11 years old, he inherited a French territory called Orange and gained the title 'Prince of Orange.' His life was full of drama and success. Philip II, the son of Emperor Charles V, appointed William as the governor of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht—the most important regions in the Netherlands. But the two men clashed. Philip wanted a strong central government with one official religion: Roman Catholicism. William, however, believed in freedom of religion and personal conscience for everyone. This conflict ended tragically on July 10, 1584, when William was shot and killed.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.
persona3_adapted: Who’s the girl in Simon Maris’s painting? We don’t know much about her. What we do know is that around 1906, she posed for Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498 in Amsterdam. She was about 12 years old at the time. We can tell this from photographs Maris took of her in the studio. In Maris’s personal records, he refers to her as “Isabella.” He also gave that name to some of his other paintings of her. Still, we can’t be sure that was her real name. Maris might have made it up. Or, like many models back then, she might have used a false name.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.
persona3_adapted: Amalia of Solms (1602–1675) was a key figure in boosting the reputation of the House of Orange and putting the Netherlands on the map internationally. She acted as a trusted advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he became ill, she took over some of his responsibilities and helped steer the country toward the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband died, her son also passed away, which led to a period known as the first stadtholderless era—a time without a formal leader from the House of Orange. To protect her family's future, Amalia used her influence both at home and abroad. She turned to art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and objects from Asia was meant to rival those of other European royal courts. Through this, she helped establish the Netherlands—led by the House of Orange—as a major European center for art and trade.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.
persona3_adapted: Between 1650 and 1672, the Netherlands had no single leader, or Stadtholder. Instead, the country was run by its most powerful citizens. Johan de Witt, serving as Grand Pensionary of Holland, became the most influential politician of the time. He cleverly played England and France—the Republic's enemies—against each other to keep them from attacking. Despite his efforts, in 1672 these countries, along with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, invaded the Netherlands. Johan's brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversaw military operations as a representative of the States General, the Dutch government. As foreign troops advanced, more and more people demanded the return of a Stadtholder. Cornelis was arrested on suspicion of plotting against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged both brothers out and killed them. Even long after their deaths, the de Witt brothers are remembered as champions of what was called 'true freedom.'

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
persona3_adapted:
At the start of the 1800s, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783–1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first King and Queen of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together—one of them would later become Emperor Napoleon III—but their marriage was not a happy one.

Queen Hortense often appeared at celebrations and official events, and partly because she visited local businesses, she grew more and more popular with the people. However, her husband didn’t like how much the public admired her, so he pushed her out of the spotlight.

After Napoleon’s defeat, Hortense moved to Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive estate, Arenenberg, she hosted cultural gatherings that people talked about for a long time. Hortense was also a talented musician and composer herself.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors on July 13, 1885, after nearly ten years of construction. The building was designed by Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a well-known figure in the Catholic Church. On the outside of the museum, Cuypers included a portrait of himself, following a tradition from the Middle Ages when master builders would add their own images to the decorations of cathedrals.

Over time, many of the museum's original decorative details were lost. However, during the most recent renovation, Cuypers' original work was carefully restored to how it once looked. The Rijksmuseum wasn't Cuypers' only famous project. In 1889, Amsterdam's Central Station—also his design—was opened. Besides these well-known landmarks, Cuypers mainly worked on churches and monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948.
