It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.
persona3_adapted: Willem Drees (1886-1988) built his career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which later became the PvdA (the Dutch Labour Party). Over time, he worked his way up through a series of roles: city councilor, deputy mayor, and member of parliament. After World War II, he became the Minister of Social Affairs in the first elected government. There, he introduced the Drees Emergency Act, a law that provided a fixed income for elderly people, helping them out of financial hardship. Drees was very popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. His main job was to lead the rebuilding of the Netherlands after the war. He turned out to be exactly the right person for the task. With a strong sense of community and working together, the country was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, which lasted until 1958, the foundations of the modern Dutch welfare state were created. Even today, people remember and appreciate him for everything he did for the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.
persona3_adapted: Around the end of the 1500s, Italian artists favored a style called Mannerism. This style was known for being very complicated, with figures placed in twisted, unnatural poses. But around 1600, a painter named Caravaggio broke away from that tradition in a big way. Instead of showing gods and saints as perfect, idealized beings, he painted them as real, ordinary people—with wrinkled faces, messy hair, and dirty feet. He loved using strong contrasts between light and dark, creating dramatic scenes that were easy to follow and understand. His approach quickly became popular across Europe. In the Dutch city of Utrecht, around 1620, many artists became his followers. They are called the Caravaggists. Painters like Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen even traveled to Rome to study Caravaggio's style up close. And although Rembrandt never went to Italy, he was also inspired by Caravaggio's dramatic use of light and shadow.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona3_adapted: The 1400s and 1500s are known as the Renaissance, which means "rebirth." During this time, people in Europe became very interested in the art, writing, and ideas of ancient Greece and Rome. Artists and builders started looking for perfect shapes and designs. They studied old Roman buildings and used features like rounded arches, tall columns, and triangular tops (called pediments). In painting and sculpture, artists focused on making people and nature look realistic. They learned how to show depth (perspective) and studied the human body to get the details right. Besides religious stories, artists also began painting myths and everyday life. The Renaissance started in Italy and spread across Europe, helped by the invention of printing in the mid-1400s, which made it easier to share ideas and images.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona3_adapted: Between World War I and World War II, a style called Art Deco became very popular around the world. It mixed old-fashioned ideas with new, modern ones. Art Deco grew out of an earlier style called Art Nouveau. It used simple, flowing shapes from plants and flowers, but also straight lines and geometric patterns. Artists also got ideas from folk art and from art made in Africa, East Asia, and Central America. This interest was helped along by new discoveries from archaeology. The name "Art Deco" comes from a big world's fair held in Paris in 1925, called the *Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes*.

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona3_adapted:
From around 1150, churches in and around Paris began to be built taller and taller. The most famous example is Notre-Dame. This is seen as the start of Gothic architecture, a style that quickly spread across Europe and lasted until just after 1500.

In these churches, the walls became lighter and more open, thanks to tall, pointed windows filled with colorful stained glass. The ceilings were supported by slim columns and had visible ribs. On the outside, the stone walls were held up by slender supports called flying buttresses, often topped with pointed decorations.

Under the influence of Gothic style, sculptors also began to shape human, animal, and plant figures in a more stylized way. Their works featured elegant poses, flowing, almost calligraphic folds in clothing, and graceful gestures.

The Gothic style didn’t stay only in architecture. It also appeared in other art forms, like painting, decorated handwritten books (called illuminated manuscripts), metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum has many religious objects in this style, including painted altars, silver holders for the Eucharist, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The term "Impressionism" comes from the word "impression," meaning a quick snapshot or a brief look at something. It was first used in the early 1870s as a negative label—a way to criticize the work of a group of painters in Paris. Artists like Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot tried to show reality the way the artist personally saw it, not just how it looked in a photograph or a traditional painting.

These French painters were inspired by the realistic style of an earlier French group called the Barbizon School, as well as by the work of a slightly older Dutch artist named Johan Barthold Jongkind. The Impressionists used bright colors and painted with short, loose brushstrokes. They chose subjects from everyday modern life—like people in parks, train stations, or busy streets.

Because their painting style looked quick and sketchy, it often seemed as if they had finished their work in a hurry. This led many critics to say that their paintings looked unfinished or sloppy. However, from the late 1880s onward, people in the Netherlands also began to appreciate French Impressionism more and more.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new style of art appeared. It was created by artists who were tired of the old trend of copying styles from earlier centuries. This fresh approach was called Art Nouveau, which means "new art" in French. It focused on natural shapes like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The style is known for its smooth, flowing lines, simple geometric forms, and designs that don't feel perfectly balanced or symmetrical. Starting in cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau quickly became popular across Western Europe and the United States. This period lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, and Art Nouveau could be seen in everything from paintings and buildings to furniture and everyday objects.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona3_adapted: Rembrandt wasn't just a great painter—he was also an incredibly skilled drawer and printmaker. The Rijksmuseum has about 1,300 prints made by him, sometimes with several versions or different stages of the same image. His prints cover a wide range of subjects: stories from the Bible and ancient myths, everyday life, landscapes, and portraits of himself and others. Looking at these works, you can clearly see how his technique, style, and artistic choices evolved over time. What makes his work stand out is his loose, sketchy drawing style and the way he used strong contrasts between light and dark. He also had a knack for choosing the most emotional moments to capture, which was unusual for his time. This makes his art feel very human and easy to connect with. Because of all this, Rembrandt had a huge influence on other artists during his lifetime and for many years after.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona3_adapted:
For centuries, porcelain—often called "white gold"—was a luxury item imported from Asia. Wealthy European nobles and elites collected it eagerly. But no one in Europe knew how to make it themselves.

That changed in 1708. With the support of Augustus the Strong, the Elector of Saxony, an alchemist named Johann Friedrich Böttger became the first person in Europe to successfully produce porcelain. Soon after, the first European porcelain factory opened in Meissen, Germany.

This factory was key to Augustus the Strong’s grand dream: a Japanese Palace where he could display his collection of both Asian and Meissen porcelain. The fact that Europe could now make its own porcelain was a major turning point in the history of European decorative arts.

Today, the Rijksmuseum has one of the most important collections of early Meissen porcelain. Many of these pieces come from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has been collecting ceramics from Asia since it opened in 1885. Most of these pieces come from China (about 2,300 items) and Japan (about 600 items), with another 50 from other Asian countries. This makes sense because the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia ever since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. Starting in the 1600s, thin, hard-fired porcelain decorated with blue Chinese patterns became extremely popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became an important part of Dutch homes and interiors.

Since 1952, the museum has also housed the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes items that show the range of artistic production in Asia: Japanese tea ceramics, Chinese bowls from the Song dynasty and sculptures made for tombs during the Tang dynasty, as well as ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Starting in 1610, about 100,000 pieces of Chinese porcelain were shipped to the Netherlands every year. To compete with this popular Chinese product, Dutch potters began to improve their own ceramics. They made their pottery thinner, whiter, and shinier. They also painted blue designs on a white, shiny surface—a look that people strongly associated with Asian porcelain.

This type of pottery was called "plateel" or "faience," but in the 1600s and 1700s, it became known as "Hollants porceleyn" (Dutch porcelain). After 1650, the city of Delft became the main center for making this pottery, which is why it is now called "Delftware" or "Delft earthenware."

Around the same time, trade in Chinese porcelain nearly stopped because of a civil war in China (1644–1684). This gave Delft pottery a big chance to grow and become very popular among wealthy Europeans.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum's bronze collection features small sculptures from the Renaissance, mostly from Italy. Many of these were made in the late 1400s in the university city of Padua. These are small, everyday bronze objects inspired by ancient Greek and Roman designs: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with figures from mythology. Many small workshops produced these luxury items in large numbers, but unique bronze sculptures and objects were also created. A great example is the large candlestick that the artist Verrocchio designed in 1469 for the Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also thrived in other Italian cities, like Rome and Venice. In the 1500s and 1600s, these small statues found their way into the private studies of scholars, the art collections of wealthy citizens, and the "wonder rooms" of princes and nobles.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

At first, artists who painted the sea focused mainly on recording important battles and other historic events at sea. Their goal was to let future generations experience these heroic moments. One early example is Willem van de Velde I, who worked like a war reporter, painting famous Dutch naval battles from what he saw himself. His son, Willem van de Velde II, became known for his skill in showing either calm waters or wild, stormy seas.

In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to show dramatic scenes of ships in trouble. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study the weather up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the father-and-son team Schotel, Dutch art became almost a synonym for seascapes in other countries.

Later, the artists of the Hague School focused more on the effects of light and cloudy skies. They also paid greater attention to the difficult lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona3_adapted:
In the 1600s, members of local city militias—called "marksmen"—were often painted together in group portraits. Back then, powerful cities handled their own security. These militias were responsible for keeping order and safety. Every adult man up to age 60 was expected to join, but he had to be able to buy his own weapons.

These group portraits were expensive projects because each militiaman paid for his own likeness to be included. The earliest ones, from the 1500s, look quite stiff and formal. But as the 1600s went on, artists started making the compositions feel more relaxed and lively. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's *Banquet of the Marksmen*.

It wasn't just marksmen's groups who ordered these portraits. Other city, regional, and national organizations—and even individuals—also hired artists to paint them.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 paintings that are still lifes. This type of painting became popular in the 1500s, starting with artists from Flanders (now part of Belgium). In a still life, the artist paints a carefully arranged group of objects. These objects can be things like shells, books, food, drinks, plates, cups, a skull, or even dead animals. You might also see flowers, insects, or sometimes a person in the background. The name "still life" comes from the idea that everything in the painting is frozen in time—life has "stood still" for a moment.

Soon, artists began to specialize in different kinds of still lifes. For example, Rachel Ruysch focused on painting flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint scenes with food, glasses, and plates. Another type is called a "vanitas" still life. These paintings often include a skull and are meant to remind us that life is short and that material things don't last forever.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a huge collection of portraits, from the late Middle Ages all the way to today. These more than 1,700 portraits do more than just show artistic skill—they give a face to the past. They show us what people looked like: the clothes and jewelry they wore, their hairstyles, and sometimes even what the rooms in their homes looked like.

But portraits also change a lot over time, because each century focuses on different things. For example, during the Renaissance, portraits started to look more and more realistic. In contrast, artists from the 1900s and 2000s often moved away from that kind of lifelike style.

Throughout history, portraits were made for many different reasons. Governments, organizations, and individuals would pay artists to create them—to show off someone’s job or social position, to celebrate a wedding, or to remember a person after they died. The Rijksmuseum also has many self-portraits by famous painters like Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona3_adapted:
Genre paintings show scenes from everyday life. These are lively pictures that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, pubs, or markets. They capture people in their natural surroundings: children playing, a family gathered in the kitchen, or a merchant selling his goods.

The first genre paintings appeared in the 1500s in Flemish art, but in the Netherlands, this type of painting didn't become popular until a hundred years later. Artists like Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous for these works. Their paintings often hid a playful or cheeky meaning—or sometimes a moral lesson.

The term "genre painting" wasn't used until the 1800s, when this style reached its peak of popularity. The Rijksmuseum also has works by 19th-century painters like Jozef Israëls, who is known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona3_adapted:
A history painting tells a story from the past that people already know. The story usually comes from a written source, like the Bible, mythology, or real historical events. These paintings often show many people acting out the story.

For example, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert painted a scene from the European conquest of America. In 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman created *The Battle of Waterloo*, which is the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 1600s, Dutch painters sometimes used Roman history to comment on their own country's politics. For instance, Ferdinand Bol's painting *Claudius Civilis* shows a rebellion by the Batavians (an ancient Dutch tribe) against the Romans. This was a way of referring to the Dutch struggle against Spanish rule.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona3_adapted: On August 17, 1945, a nationalist leader named Sukarno declared Indonesia independent by saying, “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” However, the Netherlands, which had colonized Indonesia before, refused to accept this independence and tried to take back control. To protect its freedom, Indonesia fought a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the start, Indonesia asked for and received help from other countries around the world. Because of this international pressure, the Netherlands was forced to recognize Indonesia’s independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia became a member of the United Nations, which made its independence officially recognized worldwide.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona3_adapted: Water was essential to the Netherlands becoming a major trading nation. The country’s economy grew because raw materials arrived by rivers and oceans from around the world. For example, grain and timber came from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. The Dutch used these materials to make things like beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these finished products were then shipped out again, often by water. Cities played a key role in this international trade. In the 1400s and 1500s, Antwerp was the biggest port city in the Netherlands. But after the Spanish recaptured it during the Dutch Revolt, Holland—especially Amsterdam—took over as the main trading hub. This international trade led to the rise of a financial market, created many jobs, and caused cities to grow rapidly.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona3_adapted: What is money really worth? How can a small piece of metal that's only worth a few cents suddenly become much more valuable just because it has "2 euro" stamped on it? The person or group that has the right to make coins gets to decide what they look like, how much precious metal they contain, and what they're worth. For hundreds of years, gold and silver coins traveled around the world through trade. Coins that didn't have much gold or silver in them mostly stayed close to home, because when they went to other countries, people cared most about the actual metal they were made of. For a long time, the Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a major center for trading precious metals. Even during the Eighty Years' War, a lot of the silver that Spain got from its colonies in the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, large fleets of ships carrying silver sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled all over the world—for example, to Asia through the Dutch East India Company (the VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona3_adapted: William of Orange is known as the 'Father of the Country' because he led the fight for Dutch independence. The House of Orange gets its princely title from a small French city-state called Orange, located on the Rhône River. Even though this place was not very important, the family used its wealth and influence to become one of the major royal families in Europe. The wives of the stadtholders (a kind of leader or governor) played a key role in building these connections. However, by the end of the 1700s, it looked like the House of Orange was finished: Stadtholder William V fled to England, and the Batavian Republic was established. But in 1813, his oldest son returned and became King William I. From then on, the Netherlands became a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always been the monarch.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona3_adapted:
**Who owned this object before it came to the museum?**  
The lives, relationships, and backgrounds of its owners can tell us a lot about why the object mattered. By studying an object’s history—called “provenance”—we can uncover stories of people who owned it before World War II. This reminds us of those who were persecuted during the Holocaust.  

Even if an object was never stolen, knowing where it came from helps us understand its past. The Rijksmuseum’s collection mostly includes objects from collectors who lived between 1933 and 1945. So, researching the collection can’t give us a complete picture of everyone who was persecuted. Many everyday items never ended up in museums, or are harder to trace.  

Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us how diverse Europe was before the war. Provenance research shows that the people targeted by the Nazis were active contributors to Dutch culture and history. Today, objects in museums remain silent witnesses to that past.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
persona3_adapted: Before Europeans arrived, there were already trade routes around the Indian Ocean that had existed for centuries. The Dutch East India Company, known as the VOC, took control of these networks by force and used areas like the Moluccas and Sri Lanka to get spices. Cities like Batavia (now Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope became important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants traded goods like sugar and coffee, which led to the creation of the Dutch West India Company, or WIC. Over time, enslaved people were forced to work and were bought and sold at all the company's trading posts. Even craftsmen in the Netherlands could make money from colonial trade—for example, by supplying goods used in the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. Transporting products to Europe and processing them there also brought in money. In this way, colonial trade helped boost the Dutch economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona3_adapted: The Netherlands was a colonial power for over 350 years. In the 1600s, the country set up trading posts and colonies all over the world, including in places like Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Sometimes the Dutch settled through trade agreements, but they also used military force. The Rijksmuseum now holds many objects and artworks that came to the Netherlands during and after this time. Some were taken as war prizes, while others were acquired through trade. The museum believes it's important to be open about how these items came to be in its collection and to think carefully about the Netherlands' role in its colonial history. Through research and working with the countries where these objects originally came from, the Rijksmuseum hopes to tell a more complete and balanced story of this shared past.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona3_adapted: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and hums with activity. People from all over the world have shaped it into what it is today. The first residents were farmers and fishermen who lived along the Amstel River. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port town surrounded by peatlands. Thanks in large part to the founding of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city quickly transformed into a global hub with more than 200,000 residents during the 1600s. Nearly everyone in Amsterdam at that time was involved in colonial trade in some way—from rope makers and mapmakers to lawyers and candy makers. To handle the rapid population growth, city leaders created a plan to expand Amsterdam. This was the beginning of the famous canal ring.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona3_adapted: The tulip originally came from Turkey and was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. It grew especially well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coast. In the 1600s, traders who sold tulips and other rare flowers started making "Florilegia"—books filled with detailed pictures that worked like catalogs of what they had for sale. People who loved unusual plants could also hire painters or sketch artists. Paintings of flowers in vases became a popular type of art. Real flowers eventually die, but paintings could last much longer. And surprisingly, these flower paintings sometimes cost less than the real flowers shown in them.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona3_adapted:
Throughout history, the role of pets has changed a lot. At first, animals were mostly used for work, but over time they became beloved companions. It wasn't until between 500 and 1500 that wealthy people started keeping dogs just for their company. In paintings from the 1600s, dogs often appear in this new role.

For a long time, cats were mainly used to catch mice. They also had a bad reputation because, in the Middle Ages, people linked them to witchcraft. It wasn't until the Victorian era (the 1800s) that cats became widely accepted as pets. A beautiful purebred cat—like a British Shorthair or a Persian Longhair—became a status symbol for rich people.

Starting in the 1800s, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became common pets for people of all social classes. The artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) became famous for painting cats. Wealthy families often hired her to paint their beloved pets.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona3_adapted:
Here is the adapted text, written for a native English speaker without specialized art knowledge.

---

**About the Sculpture Collection**

This online catalog explores the Rijksmuseum's vast collection of European sculpture, from the Middle Ages to today. It covers sculptures of every size and shape, made from a wide range of materials.

The catalog includes every European sculpture owned by the museum. The last time a complete guide to the collection was published was in 1973. That book was very useful, but it's now out of date. Since then, art historians have learned a lot more, and the museum has added many new pieces. So, it was time for a thorough update.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions, the Royal Cabinet of Rarities and the Dutch Museum of History and Art) only started collecting sculptures in a planned way in the late 1800s. Before that, it owned very few. One early example is a marble bust of Andries de Graeff, bought by the Dutch government in 1817 and carved by the artist Artus Quellinus.

At first, the museum focused on buying sculptures that were important to Dutch history. It wasn't until the 1900s that this focus began to change. This shift was largely thanks to two people: Adriaan Pit (the museum's director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (the sculpture curator from 1948 to 1969, who also put together the 1973 catalog). They actively sought out sculptures from other countries. As a result, about half of the collection today comes from outside the Netherlands.

This international focus got another big boost in 1952. That year, the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of a group of Italian and German sculptures. These pieces had once belonged to a collector named Fritz Mannheimer and were recovered from Germany after World War II. This important addition encouraged the museum to keep buying art from around the world.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has an outstanding collection of Japanese art on paper, including woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Out of nearly 4,000 prints, more than 800 are special, privately made prints called surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono that were given to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The information in this catalog comes from Matthi Forrer's 2013 book *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Picture Together**

Surimono (which means "printed things") are a very delicate and refined type of Japanese printmaking. What makes them so interesting is that they combine poetry with images, and they also work like a visual puzzle. Looking at them gives you a special glimpse into the intellectual and literary world of late 1700s and early 1800s Edo (modern-day Tokyo). Famous artists like Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei created pictures to go along with the poems written by members of poetry clubs.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona3_adapted: Maria Trip (1619–1683) is shown standing, facing left, from the waist up. Her left arm rests on a railing as she holds a fan. She was the sister of Jacobus Trip and later married Balthasar Coymans.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona3_adapted:
He sits on a cloud that rests on a round base, his wings spread wide. With a playful smile, he holds his right index finger to his lips, as if telling us to keep a secret. His left hand reaches for an arrow in a quiver lying beside him. One leg is bent, and the other stretches out diagonally, with his foot hanging over the edge of the base.

Next to him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver, full of arrows, is decorated with carved grooves, a wreath of laurel leaves, and a pattern of curved ridges. The pedestal is a short column with a base that also has a laurel leaf wreath. The column has curved grooves that end in leaf-shaped decorations, then a smooth upper part with a raised band. On top is a flat, overhanging cover decorated with a repeating pattern of ovals and darts. This top piece can be turned using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona3_adapted: A mother sits with her child in a cozy indoor room, carefully removing lice from the child's hair—a scene once called "Mother's Duty." They are seated in front of a box bed on the right side of the room. Nearby, a high chair stands on the right. On the left, a dog rests on the tiled floor, looking toward an open window in the room behind. Above the door hangs a small painting of a landscape.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows Alida Assink dressed in her finest clothes, from head to toe. That’s unusual because the painter, Kruseman, usually painted full-length portraits like this only for kings and queens. Assink was 23 years old when her guardian—a wealthy landowner—asked for this painting to be made. The countryside setting, with a hunting dog and a garden vase, follows the English style of portraiture that Kruseman admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona3_adapted: Aert Schouman, an artist from the Dutch city of Dordrecht, wasn't a scientist, but he was very interested in painting birds as realistically as possible. You can see his sharp eye for detail in how this red-billed toucan holds onto the branch: it uses two toes, unlike most other birds, which use three. The background landscape, painted in light, see-through shades of green and blue, suggests the bird's natural home in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona3_adapted: A painting of roses, with a butterfly and two snails also in the scene.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona3_adapted: Here, the Duke of Wellington is being told that Prussian soldiers are on their way to help him. Wellington, who led the British and Dutch troops, is the main figure in this group portrait of the key people at the Battle of Waterloo. On the left, in the front of the painting, the Dutch Crown Prince (who later became King William II) is lying wounded on a stretcher. The painting was first meant for Wellington, but it ended up staying in the Netherlands because King William I bought it for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona3_adapted: William of Orange was one of the noblemen who rebelled against King Phillip II of Spain, who ruled the Netherlands at the time. Over time, William became the main leader of the Dutch Revolt. He fought for his own interests, pushed for more independence, and supported the idea of religious freedom. In 1580, Phillip II placed a bounty on William’s head, and four years later, he was assassinated.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona3_adapted: Take a close look at this still life painting. You'll notice all the tiny details—bright flowers, unusual shells, and even beautiful insects. It makes you wonder at the richness of the natural world. But look closer: decay is already setting in. Some fruit has started to rot, leaves are being eaten by caterpillars and snails, and ants are dragging away a dead fly. So this painting is also about how nothing lasts forever.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona3_adapted: Here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa, and a purple-naped lory from Indonesia (you can see it on a chain in the lower left corner). The artist, Hondecoeter, brought these animals together with several other beautiful birds in this painting. The artwork was made for Het Loo, the palace of King William III, and it hung above the door of his private rooms.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona3_adapted: This is a wide view of a sunny river landscape, with the distance looking a little hazy and the clouds drawn in a loose, quick way. Two Dutch soldiers stop by the river to let their horses drink, next to a herder and his resting cattle. Even though this peaceful scene looks like it could be in Italy, it actually takes place along the Waal River, between the cities of Nijmegen and Cleves. The artist, Cuyp, made drawings of this landscape and brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht to create this painting.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona3_adapted: On a frozen river near a village, people are enjoying winter fun. Some are skating, others are playing games or bowling on the ice. On the left side, two people have fallen through the ice into the cold water. In the background, you can see a drawbridge and a windmill.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona3_adapted: This dollhouse is incredibly lifelike. Everything inside it is made from real materials, and the sizes are perfectly to scale. The elegant cabinet, made of tortoiseshell and decorated with pewter inlay, was built by a French cabinetmaker who lived and worked in Amsterdam for several years. The dollhouse belonged to Petronella Oortman, who was married to Johannes Brandt, a merchant from Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona3_adapted: This scene was painted on a copper panel. Because copper is very smooth, it allowed the artist to paint with great care and fine detail. Van Kessel created many small paintings like this one, showing insects and other animals. He would then arrange them together in a larger artwork. These little pieces might also have been used as decorations for a small cabinet of curiosities—a kind of personal collection of interesting objects.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

On June 18, 1648, a banquet was held at Amsterdam's crossbowmen's guild. The event celebrated the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which ended the war with Spain. The captains of the local militia shake hands as a symbol of peace, and a drinking horn is passed around. A poem written on the drum expresses the joy of Amsterdam's armed forces that their weapons can now be put away for good.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona3_adapted:
In this painting, *Fishing for Souls*, the artist uses a river scene to tell a story about religious conflict during a period of peace called the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621), when the Dutch Republic and Spain had stopped fighting. 

On the left side of the river are the Protestants and their allies. You can see important leaders like the Princes of Orange (Maurice and Frederick Henry), Frederick V of the Palatinate, King James I of England, and the young King Louis XIII of France with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat closest to us, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are labeled with the Latin words *Fides* (Faith), *Spes* (Hope), and *Charitas* (Charity).

On the right side of the river are the Catholics and their supporters. This group includes Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is being carried by cardinals. In a small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

The rainbow on the horizon adds a hopeful note, but the main message of the painting is about the jealousy and competition between different religious groups during this uneasy peace.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, who were married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated on a raised platform beneath a tree. To the left, you can see a thistle plant; to the right, ivy grows. In the background on the right, well-dressed couples stroll through a garden next to a country house, where a fountain adds to the peaceful scene.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona3_adapted: This small porcelain figure shows a parrot—specifically a macaw—perched on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue, scale-like pattern on its head and neck, while its body is white. The tips of its wings and its tail are painted in blue, yellow, and white. Under its right leg, there is a small branch with cherries. The piece has no maker's mark or signature.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona3_adapted: This kimono was worn by a young unmarried woman on formal occasions. It is covered with over two hundred flying cranes, a symbol of good luck and long life. The kimono is made from black silk crepe, a fabric with a slightly crinkled texture. The design was created using a special dyeing technique called yuzen, which allows for detailed patterns. The colors are white, red, and grey, with small accents of gold foil for extra shine. The inside lining is red silk. On the back, there is a single bamboo crest, which may represent the woman’s family.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting of flowers. A bouquet of roses, irises, poppies, tulips, and other blooms sits in a metal vase placed inside a small alcove. A wheat stalk rises among the flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl across the flowers and the wall of the alcove. This painting is the companion piece to another work, SK-A-337.

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona3_adapted: Mary Magdalene is shown sitting beneath a tree, with a jar of ointment resting on her lap. She is placed in a natural outdoor setting, surrounded by mountains and rough, rocky landscapes.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona3_adapted: This young man is Marten Soolmans, shown at age twenty. He stands from head to toe on a floor made of checkered tiles, in a room that is otherwise plain and undefined. He is wearing a very fine black suit, complete with a lace collar, decorative bows, fancy cuffs, and garters. On his head is a black hat, and his shoes are adorned with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a single glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona3_adapted: This woman is Oopjen Coppit, shown here at age 23. She stands in a simple, empty space on a tiled floor. Her outfit tells us she was wealthy: a fine black dress, a lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She also wears valuable jewelry and holds a fan made of black ostrich feathers in her right hand.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting showing a golden ceremonial cup, a silver stand holding a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a bread roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, dressed in a Turkish costume. She is shown from head to toe, sitting on a couch. Her head rests on her right hand. On the couch beside her are a book and a basket holding a mirror, a comb, and a bonnet. On the floor, there is an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona3_adapted: A happy violinist holds up a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window that is covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he lifts a birch flask full of wine; in his left hand, he holds a violin and a bow. On his head, he wears a hat with a feather.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona3_adapted: On the left side of this painting, you can see a hill with a small group of people standing near the ruins of a church. In the front of the scene, there are tropical plants and fruits, along with several animals you might not see every day: an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona3_adapted: A winter scene shows a wide, snow-covered road stretching past a forest. In the front, three boys are standing with sleds. Further down the road, a horse-drawn wagon waits in front of a farm.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona3_adapted: A peaceful Italian countryside scene. A road winds through the landscape, crossing a bridge over a mountain stream. Along the road, a man walks with a mule. In the front of the picture, a few people are sitting down.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona3_adapted: A table is covered with a white linen cloth. On it are pewter plates holding a pie, oysters, a lemon, salt and pepper, and what may be olives. Also on the table are a pewter pitcher, a wine glass, a knife, nuts, bread rolls, a fancy cup made from a seashell, and a Chinese bowl filled with fruit. In the background, there is a large pie with a whole turkey sitting on top.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona3_adapted: A stone bridge crosses a river or canal in this landscape scene. Two small boats with people float on the water. Along the road, several people are walking, and a wagon is visible on the left. In the middle of the scene, a hay barn sits between trees, while a church tower can be seen far off on the right.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona3_adapted: A carved stone portrait of the goddess Flora sits inside a decorative frame. Around her, a garland weaves together many different flowers, such as tulips, roses, delphiniums, foxgloves, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona3_adapted: A cat playing with a mousetrap knocks over a vase of flowers. The bouquet includes tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails are scattered among the blossoms.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona3_adapted: Adam and Eve stand in front of the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Around them are many different animals, including a monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, God warns Adam and Eve not to eat fruit from the tree.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
