persona3_adapted: A view of an orchard filled with blooming plum trees. In the foreground, you can see the branches of a plum tree up close, with its blossoms in full view. In the background, visitors are having a picnic. On the left side of the foreground, part of the entrance gate is visible. The sky above is a bright red.

CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona3_adapted: The artist painted this picture using many tiny dots of color, a style that makes the image look almost shimmering. In the scene, a woman sits on the right side of the painting, facing us as she reads a book by the light of an oil lamp. She wears a loose, comfortable pink dress—a style that was considered modern and practical for women at the time—and her hair is simply pinned up. On the left, we can see part of the oil lamp on a small table covered with a floral cloth. Next to the lamp is a tiny vase holding roses. In the background, a larger vase of flowers blends into the pattern of the wallpaper, making it hard to tell where the flowers end and the decoration begins.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
persona3_adapted: A small cat is playing with dominoes on a table. Next to the cat, there is an ashtray with a cigar in it.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona3_adapted: In this cozy indoor scene, a family is celebrating the Feast of Saint Nicholas, a Dutch holiday similar to Christmas. At the center, a young girl holds a doll of John the Baptist and a bucket of candy. On the left, a boy is crying because he found a rod—a punishment—in his shoe instead of a treat. Next to their mother, who has her arms stretched out, a laughing boy holds a club. On the floor, there’s a basket of cakes and rolls. By the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman look on.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona3_adapted: Jeremiah mourns as he watches Jerusalem being destroyed. The old prophet sits on a rocky mountainside, resting his head in his left hand. Next to him on a stone are a golden bowl, a pitcher, and some books—items saved from the ruined temple. Down below on the left, you can see the city's buildings on fire and soldiers in the streets.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting shows a stone table with a silver plate of oysters and fruit, a porcelain bowl, an upside-down wine glass, a small glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. There are also snails and insects in the scene.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona3_adapted: A painting called *View at the Geestbrug* shows a river scene near Rijswijk. In the distance on the left you can see De Binckhorst Castle, and on the right is the Laakmolen windmill. A woman and some children are standing on the road leading to a farm, while boats sail on the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona3_adapted: In a busy kitchen, two maids are getting food ready. One is plucking a chicken, and the other is putting a chicken on a skewer. On the left, a few more people are sitting near the fireplace. In the front of the scene, there is a big display of different kinds of hunted animals, birds, vegetables, and fruits. You can also see many glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates. In the background, there is a small scene showing Jesus with Martha and Mary.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona3_adapted: On a stone pedestal, a vase holds a colorful mix of flowers, fruit, and vines. Next to it sits a glass, while a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a single rose rest nearby. Behind them, a painting on the wall shows a woman with a flower basket, reaching down to give a flower wreath to a child.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona3_adapted: Six warships rest on a wide, calm sea. The crew is raising the sails and lifting the anchors. On the front right, the back of one ship is decorated with Amsterdam's coat of arms, held up by two standing lions. This scene likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat carrying important-looking people—probably from the Admiralty—sails past the ships, with trumpets playing and cannons firing in salute. A bit farther away, the last supplies and crew members are still being loaded on board. What stands out is the incredible attention to detail in the ropes, sails, and cannons.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting shows a bouquet of flowers in a hammered bronze vase. The flowers include tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the table next to the vase are a dead frog, a small bunch of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona3_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
Giuliano da Sangallo was an architect, and the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. In this portrait, we see him from the chest up, facing to the right. Behind him is a landscape scene. At the bottom of the painting, on a small ledge, there is a compass and a pen—tools that hint at his work as a designer and builder. This piece is part of a two-panel artwork (called a diptych), along with another painting labeled SK-C-1367.

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona3_adapted: An elderly woman, likely Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (who died in 1640), is shown here as if she were the prophetess Anna from the Bible. She sits reading a large book that lies open on her lap.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a group of people making music together, but it's also meant to tell a deeper story. On the left, a man wearing a turban and foreign-style clothing plays a stringed instrument called a viola da gamba, which is an early version of the cello. Next to him, a young man holds a harp. A young woman sits nearby, singing from an open songbook on her lap. Behind her, an older woman with a cloth covering her head watches the scene. In the front of the painting, you can see more musical instruments, like a lute and a violin, along with stacks of books. On the wall behind them hangs a painting that shows a famous Bible story: Lot's Flight from Sodom.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona3_adapted: Venus and Adonis. Venus, who is partly undressed, and Cupid are trying to stop Adonis from going hunting. In the bottom right corner, there are two white doves. Adonis’s arrow holder is hanging from a tree, and he has two hunting dogs with him.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona3_adapted: A warship sits still on a calm sea, its sails hanging loose. Suddenly, it fires a cannon. Two small rowboats float nearby, one on each side of the ship. In the distance, another warship can be seen with its sails rolled up. This painting is a companion piece to another work in the museum, numbered SK-A-1848.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona3_adapted: The word "Sufi" often brings to mind images of Turkish whirling dervishes, spinning gracefully in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, most people know little about the beliefs and practices behind these ceremonies, or about the communities that follow them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona3_adapted:
This display looks at Sufi life and art through objects from Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement that began in the Islamic world around the 700s AD. It is often described as a personal journey to connect with God.

The objects on display come from the British Museum’s collection. They include Islamic items that have special meaning for Sufis. Some, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes (a type of Sufi follower). Others, such as calligraphy, miniature paintings, and wall tiles, have a more indirect connection.

Sufis come from many different backgrounds—across class, ethnicity, and region. The display explores themes like mystical love, poverty, and self-denial, as well as how Sufism has influenced the wider societies around it.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This exhibition, *Hawaiʻi: A Kingdom Crossing Oceans*, is a stunning look at the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together incredible objects—from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved statues of gods, to powerful weapons made with shark teeth and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would change the course of their kingdom’s history. The exhibition marks over 200 years since that fateful royal visit, and it explores Hawaiʻi’s history and culture by looking at the deep and lasting connection between the Hawaiian and British nations.

Built on years of working with Native Hawaiian artists, experts, and scholars, the show puts indigenous knowledge at the center. It shines new light on extraordinary objects and remarkable stories. Created together with Hawaiian knowledge-keepers, the exhibition features rare items on loan from around the world, alongside the British Museum’s own impressive collection—one of the largest outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This exhibition features prints by Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006), a Scottish poet, writer, artist, and gardener. The works explore the idea of revolution and how it connects to modern life.

Finlay first became known in the late 1950s for his poetry. But by the 1960s, he had expanded his creative work to include prints and three-dimensional pieces made from materials like stone, metal, neon, and glass. He often made these pieces in partnership with skilled craftspeople.

Finlay was also a dedicated gardener. He created a conceptual garden at his remote home, located on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. This garden included many of his artworks. He named it Little Sparta, after the ancient Greek city that was a military rival to Athens. The name reflects his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona3_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay started focusing more and more on the French Revolution in his work. He was drawn to the idea of revolutions and sudden changes, and how even the best intentions can sometimes lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, created between 1984 and 1992, are part of Finlay's exploration of the French Revolution. They come from a set of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written a lot about Finlay and exchanged letters with him for many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona3_adapted: Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) lived through some of the most dramatic events in French history during his long career, which lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s. He was a student of the famous artist Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805). Wille made a name for himself by copying the kinds of everyday-life scenes that had made Greuze popular at art exhibitions in the 1760s. His work showed a preference for gentle, refined details that appealed to the tastes of the French royal court and aristocracy before the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona3_adapted:
Johann Wille was a man who fully supported the French Revolution—he even joined the republican army and spent his time recording the famous people of the day. But in the end, the Revolution turned out to be more of a problem than an opportunity for him. In his younger years, Wille tried out many different artistic styles, but as he got older, he found it hard to adjust to the simpler, more serious taste that became popular in France under Napoleon. By the time he died, he had been living in poverty for a long time.

During his life and after, Wille was better known for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been collecting his work since the 1800s, and its collection recently doubled in size thanks to a gift from the art dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of Wille’s finest finished drawings. One of the most powerful is a portrait that is said to show Charlotte Corday, the woman known as the "Angel of Assassination." The drawing was made in her prison cell just days—or even hours—before she was executed.

This exhibition, celebrating Clark’s gift, shows Wille’s world through his drawings and those of his fellow artists. It reveals him as a highly skilled artist whose works give us a fascinating look at French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona3_adapted:
This jug, called the Asante Ewer, was made in England between 1340 and 1405. Later, it traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, it was photographed in the courtyard of the royal palace of the Asante king in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, British soldiers took it as loot, and it was later bought by the British Museum. A second jug, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photo, was given to a British army regiment. This display brings these two jugs back together.

A new research project is looking into the Asante Ewer—one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting—to learn more about its journey to Ghana and what it meant to people there. In the palace courtyard, it was placed in what seems to be a shrine, surrounded by the roots of sacred trees. This suggests it was an important cultural or ritual object. It's remarkable that this jug survived the long trip from Europe to West Africa and back. Over time, it has had very different roles: a luxury English jug, a sacred West African vessel, and finally, colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona3_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*. The project is funded by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. It looks at the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period. The display was created in close partnership with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many other institutions in Ghana, the US, and the UK.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona3_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the region we now call the Netherlands, Belgium, and Luxembourg was a major center for new ideas in art. During this time, drawings in this area changed in an important way. At first, artists mainly used drawings to plan their paintings or other works. But over time, people began to see drawings as finished pieces of art that were valuable on their own.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition brings together about 120 drawings by artists from the Low Countries—the region we now know as Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands. The works date from before 1600 and include pieces by famous names like Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius.

The British Museum has an unusually large and high-quality collection of drawings from this area, which allows us to tell the full story of how drawing developed there. That’s rare, because early drawings from the Low Countries are very hard to find. Back then, artists used drawings mainly as working tools in their workshops—like sketches or plans—and they weren’t usually kept after the final artwork was made. This was different from Italy and German-speaking regions, where people started collecting drawings as early as the 1500s, so many more of those have survived.

Drawings were essential for creating all kinds of art, from paintings and tapestries to stained glass and printed images. The ones that have survived give us a valuable glimpse into how Netherlandish artists worked and collaborated.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona3_adapted: Based on a research project and funded by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, this exhibition presents new discoveries made by combining curatorial and conservation knowledge with scientific analysis. For example, new research into drawings that were previously only linked to Rogier van der Weyden and his group shows that these drawings actually come directly from his workshop. They were likely created by his students as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona3_adapted: Be one of the first to see the proposed designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II, to be built in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona3_adapted:
In St James' Park, a new memorial will honor the late Queen Elizabeth and her lifelong dedication to public service. It will feature a 10-foot-tall bronze statue of her as a young queen, standing on a 14-foot base. The statue is expected to be finished in 2028.

The statue was designed by well-known sculptor Martin Jennings and will be made of bronze. It shows the Queen early in her reign, wearing the robes of the Order of the Garter. The design was inspired by a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Mr. Jennings will also create a statue of Prince Philip, the longest-serving royal spouse in British history. The statue will show him at a similar age to the Queen's statue, wearing the uniform of an Admiral of the Fleet. It will be placed close to Queen Elizabeth's statue, to honor the support he gave her during her 70-year reign.

Other parts of the memorial have been designed by the architecture firm Foster + Partners, working together with landscape designer Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display includes small models of the statues and a scale model of the entire memorial park.

The King and Queen, other members of the Royal Family, and the Prime Minister saw the designs at the Museum on April 21, to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth.

The permanent memorial will also include:
- A new bridge made of cast glass, inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara (the tiara Queen Elizabeth wore on her wedding day)
- A bust (a sculpture of the head and upper chest) of the Queen in her later years, by sculptor Karen Newman
- *The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture*, a new abstract artwork by Yinka Shonibare

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona3_adapted: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, offering quiet spaces where people can relax and think. The memorial was created with input from the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee, following a design competition held last year. In addition to the memorial park, the committee has suggested setting up a new charitable trust to help improve community spaces across the UK, as well as an online Digital Memorial where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona3_adapted:
**Discover Life and Death in Ancient Egypt**

Step into the world of ancient Egypt and find out how people lived, died, and what they hoped for after death. This tour explores everything from human remains and painted coffins to small statues, models of daily life, and stunning wall paintings and writings on papyrus (an early form of paper).

You’ll see what tombs, bodies, and burial objects can teach us about the lives and beliefs of ancient Egyptians. We’ll look at how they prepared the dead for burial, including the process of mummification, how they filled tombs with items for a peaceful eternal rest, and how they used magic to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife.

The tour also shows how these burial remains reveal details about everyday life in ancient Egypt.

**Please note:** This tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is dedicated to treating these remains with care, respect, and dignity. To learn more about our principles for handling, displaying, and studying human remains, please ask a staff member or visit our website.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Step into the Museum before it opens to the public and discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt. You'll explore one of the Museum's most famous spaces: the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4).

Here, you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, large stone structures from ancient tombs, and carved wall panels that tell stories from over 3,000 years of Egyptian history. Highlights include the giant stone head of Ramesses II and the famous statue of the Gayer-Anderson Cat.

On this special guided tour, you'll learn why ancient Egyptians created such large-scale stone artworks for their temples and tombs, and what these works meant to the people who made them.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona3_adapted:
The British Museum first opened its doors to visitors in 1759, just six years after it was founded in 1753. Its mission is to tell the story of human achievement across cultures and time, using a vast collection of objects gathered from all over the world.

This room highlights some of the collectors who, in different ways, have helped shape the Museum over the past 400 years. It also looks at the people and organizations who continue to influence what the Museum will become in the future. The rooms next door explore different aspects of collecting as well.

In Room 1, called "Enlightenment," you can learn how the Museum's earliest staff gathered and organized objects and knowledge. In Room 2a, called "The Waddesdon Bequest," you'll find a collection of masterpieces from the Renaissance and Baroque periods. These were left to the Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild, a member of Parliament, when he passed away in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona3_adapted: The British Museum has raised £3.5 million, so the Tudor Heart Pendant will stay on public display for many years to come. In December 2019, someone using a metal detector in Warwickshire discovered a stunning Tudor heart-shaped jewel buried in the ground, where it had rested for centuries. This pendant is linked to King Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon. There is no other object like it in the British Museum or anywhere else in the UK. The pendant is a highly skilled piece of work, decorated with enamel designs. It hangs from a 75-link gold chain by a clasp shaped like a hand coming out of a cloud. Very few objects survive that tell the story of Henry's life with Katherine, who was a devout Catholic. That's because their marriage was annulled (officially ended) in 1533. Still, their union was the longest of all Henry's marriages—lasting 24 years—and it deeply shaped the first three decades of his rule.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona3_adapted:
In the 1700s, the museum's collections were split into two types: 'Natural and Artificial Rarities.' Natural rarities were things found in nature, like plants and animals, while artificial rarities were objects made by people. Back then, only a few rooms in the early museum contained manmade objects. Most of the space was filled with natural specimens from Sir Hans Sloane's collection, along with his library, which included 265 volumes of dried plant samples (his herbarium).

In 1735, a Swedish botanist named Carl Linnaeus changed how scientists name and organize plants, animals, and other natural objects. One of his students, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator at the museum. Solander used Linnaeus's system to organize Sloane's collections, as well as the natural history specimens he himself gathered with Sir Joseph Banks on Captain Cook's first voyage. Many of these original specimens, along with several fossils, are on display in this exhibition.

By 1880, the museum had so many natural history specimens that they needed their own separate building. That's why the Natural History Museum was built in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona3_adapted:
In the past, people called "antiquaries" were fascinated by history. They collected old books, handwritten documents, drawings, and objects from earlier times. They also traveled around the country to study ancient ruins, hoping to learn more about Britain's Roman past and even earlier history.

It was fairly easy to learn about the Middle Ages because many things from that time had survived, such as religious items, armor, books, and buildings. But antiquaries didn't stop there. They began to carefully survey and map Roman and even older sites, like Stonehenge, using a more scientific and organized approach. This helped them learn more about the earliest people who lived in Britain.

This new knowledge, combined with the emerging study of rock layers (called "strata"), led archaeologists to question a long-held belief. At that time, many people thought the world was created in 4004 BC, a date calculated from the Bible. But the evidence from these studies suggested the world might be much older.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona3_adapted:
In 1824, a famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures arrived at the British Museum. These works had been gathered by Charles Townley during his travels in Italy. They joined other collections of prints, drawings, gems, coins, bronze objects, vases, and ancient artifacts from other enthusiasts, like Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight.

These collectors studied each other's collections to understand how they believed art had "progressed." They thought art started out simple and "primitive" in early civilizations, and reached its peak in the sculpture and architecture of ancient Greece. For them, Greek art became the standard by which all other art was judged. This idea had a big impact in Britain during the late 1700s, sparking a revival of classical styles in buildings and decorative objects.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona3_adapted:
In the 1700s, people believed that to truly understand the world, you had to sort and label everything you collected. King George III had a huge personal library, which his son George IV later gave to the nation. It was kept in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (though the books themselves are now at the British Library). Among the first items from that library to arrive at the Museum were coins and medals. They were organized by country and ruler, so visitors could picture history coming to life.

George III was also fascinated by ships, navigation, and new scientific discoveries. He collected tools and working models to study how things worked. Meanwhile, Sir William Hamilton gathered ancient vases found near Naples, Italy. He grouped them by their painting styles, trying to figure out which ones were older or newer. Later, he published beautiful books with detailed pictures of his collection.

Earlier in the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious objects from around the world—things people had made by hand. Some of these items came from enslaved people in Jamaica. Sloane’s collection was cataloged and displayed in many different ways: by what the object was, what it was made of, how old it was, or what it was used for.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona3_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked a huge curiosity about ancient writing systems—like Egyptian hieroglyphs, the wedge-shaped cuneiform from biblical lands, and early Sanskrit in India. In the 1800s, scholars managed to decode these scripts, unlocking thousands of years of history. Around the same time, early historians also figured out runes and other old forms of writing, which helped reveal more about our own past. During this period, the British Museum received many ancient objects, collected from private collectors, diplomats, and archaeological digs.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona3_adapted: Christian suspicion of superstition, idols, rituals, and magic meant that people often knew very little about other religions. During the Enlightenment, scholars tried to change this by studying both ancient religions and the ones they saw around the world. They looked at ceremonies and worship practices in different cultures, collected religious objects, sorted gods into groups, and studied their traits and roles. Their goal was to find common patterns that could help explain where the world's religions came from. Much of this work happened as part of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona3_adapted:
During the age of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats gathered objects from different cultures. These included both everyday items and ceremonial pieces, which they believed showed the customs and ways of life of people around the world. They collected these objects and published illustrated books describing the lives and cultures of people from a European point of view.

Along with Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also holds objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to places like Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. Many of these objects were obtained through employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company. The wealth of these companies came from the use of enslaved labor and the exploitation of people and natural resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Alexander the Great was a famous ancient leader. From 336 to 323 BC, he conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities. His empire stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.

After Alexander died in 323 BC, a new period began, called the Hellenistic period. This period lasted until 31 BC, when Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony were defeated at Actium. After Alexander's death, his empire was split into smaller kingdoms. These were ruled by his former generals. The new royal families built large buildings and supported art and culture.

In Room 22, you can see many objects from this time. One is a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos. This temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. You can also see a statue called the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey, and several Greek bronze objects. Smaller items show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and gem cutters. There are also small clay figures, which were made by artists called coroplasts.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the rich artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It features a stunning collection of objects, including feathered cloaks once worn by chiefs, finely carved figures of gods, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean on a journey that would become a turning point for their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition marks over 200 years since that fateful royal visit. It explores Hawaiʻi’s history and culture by looking at the deep and lasting connection between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the rich artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. You'll see a wide range of objects, from feathered cloaks once worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons made with shark teeth and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean on a journey that would become a turning point for their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition marks over 200 years since that tragic royal visit. It explores Hawaiʻi's history and culture by looking at the deep and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona3_adapted:
This fashion show features contemporary clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa, which is the great ocean of Kiwa, also known as the Pacific Ocean. The show was organized in the UK by the Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia, a Māori designer. Lyall created the collection in partnership with respected experts from across the Pacific. These experts, called *tohunga*, are people who are building and sharing Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides and aim to be 'good ancestors' through their daily actions, strong intentions, and vision. Their goal is to create a strong future for *mokopuna* (grandchildren) and generations to come.

The show will serve as a gathering point for the Pacific diaspora community, as they open a VĀ (a kind of portal) to connect with their ancestors. It is an offering to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum, represented through Pacific collections. The show aims to 'warm' them as they rest. During the event, the group will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona3_adapted: One side of the gallery shows the story of China from 5000 BC to today. You'll see famous blue-and-white porcelain from the Ming dynasty, delicate hand-painted scrolls, impressive tomb figures from the Tang dynasty, and modern artworks. The displays highlight the variety of art and everyday objects from China, including paintings, prints, jade, bronze, lacquerware, and pottery.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona3_adapted: The other side of the gallery shows the many stories of South Asia, arranged by time period and region, from the earliest human settlements to today. Some standout items include carved seals from the Indus civilization, beautiful sculptures of the god Shiva from southern India, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. You can also see highly detailed paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors, displayed alongside 20th-century paintings, including works by Nobel Prize winner Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona3_adapted: China is one of the world's oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a huge area about the size of Europe and is home to about a quarter of all people on Earth. From very early times, China developed a unique culture and made beautifully crafted objects in large numbers. Room 33 looks at 7,000 years of China's history, exploring topics like writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, and international trade. The room also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Themes include trade, exchange, migration, and political power. South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. The region is home to many ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Religions like Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam have inspired amazing architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona3_adapted:
Africa’s rich history and cultural traditions are on display at the Museum in several places—including the Africa galleries (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and throughout other exhibition spaces. You can join free tours led by our volunteers, which were created with help from community members. On these tours, you’ll explore objects that each guide has personally chosen, spanning from ancient times to the present day. Each guide offers their own unique tour, focusing on one of these themes:

- **Out of Africa:** Stories about migration, materials, and mythology  
- **The Igbo worldview:** How artifacts from the past connect to Igbo culture, shown at the British Museum  
- **African metal artifacts:** The traditions, techniques, and trade behind metal objects from Africa  
- **Ancient objects from African kingdoms:** Items from powerful kingdoms in Africa’s past

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona3_adapted:
**Step into spring and the month of May with a fun, hands-on play session for children ages five and under.**

In this relaxed, child-led activity, you and your little one can meet characters like May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures. Take time to smell floral scents, draw and scribble with different art supplies, and make crowns and masks to wear for spring celebrations. We'll go at your child's pace as we explore textures, colors, and scents together using a variety of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona3_adapted: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704 to 681 BC, and he was well known for his large-scale building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Southwest Palace in Nineveh were decorated with detailed stone carvings. Many of these carvings are now on display in Room 9. They show a variety of scenes, including the movement of enormous stone statues of human-headed winged bulls, called *lamassu*. These statues weighed up to 30 tons and were made for the main entrances of the palace. The carvings give us a look into how the ancient Assyrians quarried and moved heavy stone, and they also show how interested Sennacherib was in his building work. Other carvings in the room show the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona3_adapted: Spread across three rooms, these galleries show what life was like in Assyrian culture during this time. You'll see stone carvings of lion hunts, the attack on the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. In Room 10a, you're greeted by two enormous winged bulls with human heads. These giant statues were meant to be supernatural protectors, guarding the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad and keeping all kinds of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona3_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was seen as a sport fit for kings. It was a way to show the ruler's role as a protector and fighter for his people. The stone carvings on display in Room 10a tell the story of the hunting adventures of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (who ruled from 668 to 631 BC). These carvings were made for his palace in Nineveh, which is in what is now northern Iraq. The hunting scenes are full of excitement and look very realistic. They are considered some of the best examples of Assyrian art. The carvings show the lions being let out, the chase that follows, and finally the killing.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona3_adapted:
Lachish was one of the most important cities in the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Near East (modern-day Israel/Palestine). In 701 BC, it was conquered by the Assyrian king Sennacherib, who ruled from 704 to 681 BC. The attack happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute—essentially a forced payment—to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is also mentioned in the Bible.

In Room 10b, you can see many stone carvings (called reliefs) that show the city being captured, along with some of the weapons and tools used during the siege. There is also a special clay object shaped like a prism, which has an Assyrian account of the campaign written on it.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona3_adapted:
In the city of Khorsabad, located in what is now northern Iraq, a palace was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC. At the main entrances of the palace, there were huge statues of winged bulls with human heads. These statues were meant to guard the palace, along with other protective spirits that were believed to have magical powers.

Today, two of these impressive statues can be seen in Room 10c. Also in that room are carvings that show the king, the crown prince, royal officials, and scenes of hunting. Nearby, there are inscriptions on display that came from a similar winged bull statue. That statue was originally from the palace of King Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC) in the nearby city of Nineveh. These inscriptions were badly burned when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona3_adapted:
Rooms 57–59 show objects from daily life and culture in this region. The time period covered stretches from the first farmers of the Neolithic period, around 8000 BC, all the way to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. These objects are presented alongside the major historical events of the time.

The items on display help us see how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant continued over many centuries. They also show the local roots of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. In addition, the exhibition compares this culture with the peoples of central inland Syria—the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona3_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu were key regions connecting Europe and Asia. Today, this area covers parts of modern Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. In the Early Bronze Age, skilled artisans created objects like a silver bull and a cup, which are on display here. Records from Middle Bronze Age merchants show how trade linked central Anatolia with Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). From the Hittite period, you can see delicate gold jewelry and small statues. Objects from the Iron Age Urartu culture include winged bulls and griffins that were used to decorate furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition looked back more than 2,000 years to explore how Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist sacred art first began. It showed how these traditions grew out of ancient Indian beliefs in powerful spirits of nature, and how this art later spread beyond India.

It was one of the first major exhibitions worldwide to examine early religious art in India from a modern, multi-faith, and global viewpoint. The exhibition highlighted what inspired the now-familiar images of gods, goddesses, and enlightened teachers in these world religions. It also showed how these images traveled across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition is full of color, sound, and feeling. It was created with help from a group of advisors who are practicing Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains. These are living religious traditions, and their sacred art is part of the daily lives of nearly two billion people around the world, including here in the UK. Important objects borrowed from our community partners helped us tell this modern story.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition features over 180 objects from the British Museum’s South Asian collection, along with generous loans from museums and partners around the world. The items include sculptures, paintings, drawings, and manuscripts. The show focuses on the story behind each object—tracing its journey from when it was first made to how it ended up in a museum.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona3_adapted: From early symbols like footprints that stood for the Buddha before people showed him as a human, to the giant snakes in Hindu art that represent the universe, and the nature spirits that accompany Jain holy teachers, this fascinating exhibition revealed the ancient stories behind these still-practiced traditions.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition was the first major show of Hiroshige's work at the British Museum, and the first in London in over 25 years. It offered a beautiful look at a country on the verge of great change.

Hiroshige was born in Japan during a time of unrest (1797–1858). He went on to become one of the country's most talented, productive, and beloved artists. As Japan faced growing contact with the outside world, Hiroshige's calm and peaceful art connected with people from all walks of life—and gave them a sense of comfort.

His work shows many sides of life in Japan at the time: fashionable people, lively city scenes, quiet countryside, and views of nature. His bird-and-flower prints reveal a poetic love of the natural world, while his landscapes reflect a growing interest in travel across Japan.
