Hiroshige sometimes showed his world exactly as it was, but often he painted it as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona3_adapted: Hiroshige was an incredibly skilled artist, especially when it came to using color and drawing. He also had a genuine, caring interest in people from every level of society. Unlike most other print artists of his time, he was born into a samurai family—a high social class—but he chose to cross those social lines and focus on showing everyday life and customs. His art was also affordable. In addition to his famous landscape prints, he created hundreds of simple, hand-held fans that anyone could buy and use.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition brings together prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum’s collection. It also includes a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a leading American collector of Hiroshige’s work, along with other important loans.

Beyond showcasing Hiroshige’s remarkable career, the show looks at his lasting influence around the world—from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and contemporary creators such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona3_adapted: A stone carving shows the Hindu god Shiva seated with his wife Parvati on his knee. This type of statue is also called "Umamahesvaramurti." A lion and a bull (named Nandi) are nearby. Around them are many attendants, some flying and some standing—they include musicians and people carrying flower garlands. Below them is a row of ritual objects: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona3_adapted: These are a pair of bride's slippers, called *tarkasin*. They are made from red-dyed leather, embroidered with silk thread, and decorated with metal studs. The tongues of the slippers are cut in the shape of the *khamsa* (meaning "five" in Arabic), also known as the "Hand of Fatima."

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona3_adapted:
This is a large statue of the head and upper body of the Egyptian pharaoh Ramses II. It is made of pink and gray granite. The statue was originally one of a pair placed in front of a temple called the Ramesseum.

The king wears a striped headdress (called a *nemes*) and a crown of cobras (called *uraei*) around his forehead. About half of the cobras are now missing. The sculptor used the two colors of the stone to clearly separate the body from the face.

On the back, there is a stone pillar carved with rows of hieroglyphs. These inscriptions list the king’s names and titles, and include part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, people noted that there were still traces of color on the statue. This means it was likely painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona3_adapted: A large head, carved from red granite, once part of a statue of the pharaoh Amenhotep III (possibly).

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona3_adapted:
This is the inner coffin of Hornedjitef, a priest who held many important religious roles. The coffin is made of wood and shaped like a human body. Its face, wig, collar, and chest piece are painted in bright colors and covered with gold. Across the chest is a winged scarab beetle, an ancient Egyptian symbol of rebirth. The body of the coffin is covered with rows of painted hieroglyphs, with gods and goddesses on either side. The rest of the surface is left plain.

Inside the lid is a painting of Nut, the sky goddess, surrounded by stars and constellations. The inside of the base is also decorated.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona3_adapted:
This brass head was made using a method called "lost wax casting." First, a model of the head was made in wax. Then, it was covered in clay and heated, which melted the wax and left a hollow mold. Finally, molten brass was poured into the mold to create the metal head.

The head is slightly smaller than a real human head and is made in a realistic style. It wears a complex headdress that looks like a crown. The crown has three main layers:

- **Top layer:** A band with four flat, disc-shaped beads. Above each bead is a red-painted tube-shaped bead and a tassel.
- **Middle layer:** A row of vertical rectangles that represent tube-shaped beads with tassels.
- **Bottom layer:** A row of red-painted rosettes (flower-like designs).

Around the forehead, the crown has a curved section made of small tube-shaped beads, edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. Its center has eighteen vertical lines that look like braided hair, with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides of the neck cover have a row of red-painted rosettes.

On top of the crown, at the front, there is a crest. It has a cone-shaped roundel (a circular decoration) with a raised center, surrounded by seven rings that probably represent beads. Behind this roundel is a braid-like element that ends in a pointed oval tip. Both parts have traces of black paint.

The face is slightly long and has vertical lines (like scratches) carved into it. There are two lines of small holes between the ears: one goes through the side of the neck and jaw, and the other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The eyes are almond-shaped and small compared to the head. The eyebrow ridge is sharply defined. The lips do not have any carved lines. There are grooves around the front of the neck that look like skin creases. There are holes pierced through the front and both sides of the neck. There is also a large, irregular hole on the right side of the jaw.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona3_adapted:
This statue shows a female goddess, known as Tārā. She is made of solid bronze and covered in gold. Her hair is styled high on her head, like a tube, and held in place by a decorated band. On either side of the band are mythical water creatures called *makara*s. The center of the band was probably once filled with precious stones, and the eyes of the statue were likely decorated with stones too.

Her earlobes are stretched long, and she wears a tight cloth wrapped around her lower body, tied at the hips. Her upper body is bare. In her right hand, she makes a gesture of giving. In her left hand, her thumb and fingertips touch to form a ring, as if she is holding a flower. Two of the fingers on her right hand are missing, and some toes are also gone from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona3_adapted:
This drawing shows the Virgin Mary and the baby Jesus. Mary is shown from the waist up, and Jesus stands with his arms around her.

The artist used black chalk (a soft drawing material) and possibly charcoal to create the image, with a few touches of white chalk for highlights. The drawing was made on two sheets of paper joined together. Tiny holes were pricked along the outlines, and the lines were pressed into the paper underneath—this was a technique used to transfer the design onto another surface, like a painting or a fresco.

The artwork is framed.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona3_adapted: A marble sculpture of the upper body of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory. She was originally shown with wings, and is dressed in a sleeveless garment called a chiton. This piece was likely part of an akroterion—a decorative ornament placed at the top or corners of a temple roof.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a small piece of a marble decoration shaped like a palm leaf, with a bird's foot carved into it.

The fragment was once part of a larger decorative element that stood on the corner of a building. It came from a rectangular, upright palm-leaf design that sat above a spiral-shaped ornament called a volute. On both sides of the center leaf—which sticks out from the corner—you can see three gently curved, hollowed-out leaves. Each leaf has a raised line running along its edge. The rounded tips of the leaves are emphasized on the outside by small bumps. Between the leaves, there are arrow-shaped decorations called darts.

On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved, egg-shaped molding from the volute is still visible. The top and back of the piece show a small, five-sided block with a disk-like tunnel that sticks out at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a rounded back seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels, you can see two claws, each with bird legs along the edges.

On the darts on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost dart and the third one down. There are also traces of red and blue paint elsewhere.

The marble is light and crystalline. The surface is fairly smooth, but the bottom is broken and rough.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona3_adapted:
This chess piece is carved from walrus ivory. It shows a king with a beard, seated on a high-backed chair. He wears a crown decorated with leaf-like shapes, a tunic, a cloak, and a brooch. In his hand, he holds a sword still in its sheath.

The back of the chair has two horizontal sections separated by a zigzag band. The top section is filled with a pattern of connected, rounded arches. The bottom section has matching leaf-shaped decorations. The sides of the chair are decorated with woven, interlocking patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona3_adapted: This small object is a fragment from a miniature altar, likely used in ancient Egyptian religious practices. It was made by pressing clay into a mold, probably made of two or more pieces. The upper corner, which broke off, is shaped like a half-palm leaf design, known as a palmette. You can still see faint traces of a dull red coating on both the inside and outside, and there are signs that something was burned inside it. The clay itself is brown Nile silt with a lighter brown center, and it contains tiny flecks of gold-colored mica and some white specks.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona3_adapted: This cloth was made using a traditional Nigerian technique called *adire eleko*, where a starchy paste is painted onto the fabric to resist the dye. The cloth is made of two panels of factory-made cotton that have been sewn together by machine. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows and seven squares. The patterns were painted by hand. In two of the squares, you can see the pillars of Mapo Hall, a famous building in the city of Ibadan. Between these pillars are four shapes that look like pestles (used for grinding) or spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona3_adapted: This ticket was sent to A. W. Franks, inviting him to see Falero's painting *Nightmare* at the Gainsborough Gallery. The ticket itself features a creepy creature with a bat's wing, a claw, and a ribcage made of bone. It was made in 1888 using wood-engraving and letterpress printing.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a ticket from 1865. It was made for people to see a painting called *The Railway Station* by an artist named Frith. The main picture on the ticket shows a steam train pulling into a station, with people standing around. There's a blank space at the top where someone could write their name. The ticket was printed using a method called lithography, which is a way of making copies from a design drawn on stone.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a paper ticket for entry. On the left and right sides, there are photographs of two paintings: one by the artist Klimt and one by the artist Schiele. In the middle is a photo of the Leopold Museum. Above these images, there is a line of black text printed in multiple colors on a white background. Below that, there is more black text printed on white.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona3_adapted:
Here’s the adapted version for a native English speaker without art expertise:

---

**Paper admission ticket**  
A photograph of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul, showing its columns and arched ceiling. On the right side, there is black text printed on a pink and white background.

**Unidentified historical painting**  
A colorful print on a white background. It shows a group of soldiers or forces gathered together, with a tree on the left side.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This image shows the inside of a tent where visitors are gathered in front of a very large history painting by Copley (now in the Guildhall Art Gallery). The painting has an elaborate frame decorated with columns and hanging fabric. On the left side, two young men are flirting with young women. On the right side, two older men sit with papers in their hands. Behind them on the left, there are figures at a desk. The image also includes a copy of the admission ticket for the exhibition, based on an engraving by Bartolozzi. This reproduction was made around the 1850s or 1860s using a photomechanical process.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
This ticket was for a show put on by a group called the Guild of Literature and Art. The picture on the ticket tells two sad stories about artists and writers struggling to make a living.

On the left, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He is carrying a painting to a pawn shop, probably to get some quick cash. On the right is the writer Daniel Defoe, author of *Robinson Crusoe*. He has a copy of his famous manuscript in his pocket, but he looks unhappy as he leaves a publisher’s office. His worried wife and daughter are waiting for him.

In the front of the scene, there are books and a painter’s palette with brushes lying on the ground. These objects remind us of the creative work that didn’t bring these men enough money.

This print was made in 1851, using a mix of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a special thin paper attached to a thicker backing sheet.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
This ticket was for a show put on by a group called the Guild of Literature and Art. The picture tells two stories about struggling artists and writers.

On the left, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He is carrying a painting to a pawn shop, probably hoping to get some money for it.

On the right, you see the writer Daniel Defoe, who wrote *Robinson Crusoe*. He has the manuscript (the handwritten pages of his book) in his pocket. He is leaving the office of his publisher, Edmund Curll, and looks sad. His worried wife and daughter are waiting for him.

In the front of the picture, there are books and a painter's palette with brushes lying on the ground.

This print was made in 1851, using a mix of different printing techniques (etching, aquatint, and engraving) on a special type of paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
This ticket was for a special performance put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. The picture on the ticket tells a story about two famous artists who struggled to make a living.

On the left, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He is carrying a painting to a pawn shop, where he will sell it for quick cash. On the right is the writer Daniel Defoe, author of *Robinson Crusoe*. He has a copy of his famous manuscript in his pocket, but he looks sad as he leaves the office of his publisher, Edmund Curll. Defoe’s worried wife and daughter are waiting for him. In the foreground (the front of the scene), you can see books and a painter’s palette with brushes lying on the ground.

This print was made in 1851 using a mix of printing techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a special thin paper called *chine collé*, which is glued onto a stronger backing sheet.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, with signature by James Beck; am emblem on the top left with the letters VR and a crown above. 1862
Woodcut and letterpress
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a ticket for a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum (now known as the Victoria and Albert Museum in London). It was made in 1862 and features a signature by James Beck. In the top left corner, there is an emblem with the letters "VR" (which stand for Victoria Regina, meaning Queen Victoria) and a crown above them. The ticket was created using a woodcut design and printed with a letterpress.

CuratorialText:
Advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel"; Princess Alexandra, full length, seated to right, painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel; illustration to "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" (3 December, 1887).
Wood engraving
persona3_adapted: This advertisement shows Princess Alexandra sitting in a chair, facing to the right. She is painting a stool using "Aspinall's Enamel." The image was printed in a newspaper called *The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper* on December 3, 1887. It is a wood engraving, meaning the picture was carved into a block of wood and then used for printing.

CuratorialText: Large glazed and framed oil painting on canvas depicting a swirling abstract composition dominated by crimson on a dark background. The central bright design has areas of thickly applied paint while in the black background the contracting of the paint surface has produced a textured effect.
persona3_adapted: A large oil painting on canvas, framed and covered in glass. It shows a swirling, abstract design, with bright red as the main color against a dark background. In the center, the paint is thickly applied, creating a raised, textured look. On the black parts of the painting, the paint has shrunk as it dried, which also gives the surface an interesting, bumpy texture.

CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.
persona3_adapted: In the center of this oil painting on canvas, a scene of worship is shown. A sacred stone called a Śālagrāma is being honored. On the left side, you can see Balakṛṣṇa—the child form of the god Krishna—holding a ball of butter. On the right, a woman holds a parasol over a central throne. This throne represents the god Viṣṇu without showing his image, a way of showing respect that is sometimes called "aniconic." A large conch shell placed in the front of the painting also points to Viṣṇu. Other objects used in prayer (known as "puja") are visible too, including a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.
persona3_adapted: Lady Layard, the wife of Henry Layard, is shown sitting on the balcony of Ca' Capello, the Layards' home in Venice, which looks out over the Grand Canal. She wears a fitted red jacket with a belt and a black skirt with a very long train. In her right hand, she holds a fan, and she leans on the balcony railing. Behind her, open French doors with glass panels lead into a room with potted plants. In the distance, you can see a view across the Grand Canal. The painting is done in oil on canvas and is set in a carved and gilded wooden frame.

CuratorialText: Painting in mixed media (?oil pencil) of a bearded head and shoulders of a male figure. This is one from the series 'Telingana Faces'. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. Signed and dated by the artist. Mounted
persona3_adapted: This is a mixed-media painting—likely done with oil pencil—showing a bearded man from the chest up. It’s part of a series called *Telingana Faces*. The man is wearing a square amulet around his neck. The artist signed and dated the work. It is mounted.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Voltaire (1694-1778), T.Q.L., standing, body nearly in profile to r., head turned half r., and eyes looking full, wearing dark green coat trimmed with gold lace, and holding book in his l. hand.
persona3_adapted: This is a portrait of Voltaire (1694–1778), a famous French writer and philosopher. He is shown standing, with his body turned mostly to the right. His head is turned partway in the same direction, and he looks directly at the viewer. He wears a dark green coat decorated with gold trim, and holds a book in his left hand.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk a Dakota Sans Arc Chief, sitting. Wearing three feathers in his hair, ear and neck -ornaments, decorated shirt with feathers on upper sleeves and blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk.
Oil on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows a man named Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also called Yellow Hawk. He was a chief of the Dakota Sans Arc people. He is sitting down and looking straight ahead. He wears three feathers in his hair, along with earrings and a necklace. His shirt is decorated, with feathers on the upper sleeves, and he is wrapped in a blanket. In his hands, he holds a weapon that is both a pipe and a tomahawk.

The artist used oil paint on canvas to create this portrait.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468), H.L., turning half-r., the eyes looking full, wearing a cap, holding a wood block with the letters of the alphabet A-I.
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows Johann Gutenberg, the inventor of the printing press, who lived from about 1398 to 1468. He is shown from the waist up, his body turned slightly to the right, but his eyes look directly at us. He wears a cap and holds a wooden block carved with the first nine letters of the alphabet (A through I). The artwork is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), Infanta of Spain, half-length turned slightly to left, wearing white dress trimmed with red rosettes etc., large ruff, and jewelled headdress; after Frans Pourbus II
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria, who lived from 1566 to 1633. She was a Spanish princess, known as an "Infanta." In the portrait, she is shown from the waist up, with her body turned slightly to the left. She wears a white dress decorated with red rosettes, a large ruff (a stiff, frilly collar popular at the time), and a jeweled headdress. This artwork is a copy of an original painting by Frans Pourbus II, a Flemish artist. It was made using oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670-1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, three-quarter length, standing, body turned half-right, head half-left, wearing armour and ermine-trimmed cloak, and holding sceptre.
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of Augustus II, also known as "The Strong." He was the King of Poland and the Elector of Saxony, and he lived from 1670 to 1733. In the portrait, we see him from about the knees up. He is standing with his body turned slightly to the right, but his face is looking a little to the left. He is wearing armor and a cloak trimmed with ermine fur, and he is holding a royal scepter. The artwork is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait said to be of Mary Tudor (1516-58) as Princess; Head and shoulders, 3/4 face, eyes looking full. Wearing ruff, earring, embroidered dress, ?chain, headdress.
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted: This is a portrait believed to show Mary Tudor (1516–1558) when she was a princess. The painting shows her from the chest up, with her body turned slightly to the side but her eyes looking straight at the viewer. She is wearing a ruff (a stiff, pleated collar), an earring, an embroidered dress, what appears to be a chain, and a headdress. It is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Jacques (aka Jean, John, James) Rousseau (1630-93), Oval, head and shoulders turned half-r., eyes looking full, wearing dark coat and lace cravat. Oil on canvas.
persona3_adapted: This is a portrait of a man named Jacques Rousseau, who lived from 1630 to 1693. (He was also known by other versions of his first name, like Jean, John, or James.) In the painting, we see his head and shoulders, with his body turned slightly to the right, but his eyes looking straight at us. He is wearing a dark coat and a lace necktie. The painting is done in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Dr Robert G W Anderson. Head, profile, facing to sitter's right
Oil painting on panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio at Talfourd Road, Peckham. Superimposed on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe. The tie is a version of the Great Court ties sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to mark the opening of the Great Court.
persona3_adapted:
This is a portrait of Dr. Robert G. W. Anderson. We see his head from the side, facing to the right.

It is an oil painting on a wooden panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio on Talfourd Road in Peckham, London. Overlaid on the window is a section of an old scientific instrument called the Sloane astrolabe. The tie he is wearing is a version of the special "Great Court" ties that were sold at the British Museum around the end of the year 2000 to celebrate the opening of the museum's Great Court.

CuratorialText: Oil painting on canvas stretched and glued to back of stained wooden frame. Painting shows scene of an imaginary beach side village with huts and trees in the foreground, ocean and sky with full moon behind. Painting is signed.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a beachside village that exists only in the artist's imagination. In the front of the scene, you can see small huts and trees. Behind them, the ocean stretches out, and a full moon glows in the sky. The painting is made with oil paint on canvas, which has been stretched and attached to the back of a wooden frame. The frame is stained a dark color. The artist has signed the work.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; commercial sign-board made of plywood, used to advertise barbers' shop hairstyles. Six individual heads are depicted in profile, each numbered and displaying a different haircut. The heads face in different directions and are painted in bold black outlines on a white background; each head has a blue collar.
persona3_adapted: This signboard was made of plywood and painted with oil paint. It was used by a barber shop to show different hairstyles to customers. The board shows six heads, each seen from the side. Every head has a number and a different haircut. The heads face in different directions. They are drawn with thick black lines on a white background, and each head has a blue collar.

CuratorialText: A large oil painting on canvas depicting the slaying of the dragon by St George who is the central figure of the painting, mounted on a white horse and carrying a circular shield in one hand, a spear in the other. The dragon, with striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves, together with a little blue vanquished devil, his face in profile. To the right of the painting Biruwit is depicted in the branches of a tree; to the left stand a group of soldiers armed with spears and shields.
persona3_adapted: In this large oil painting, Saint George takes center stage as he slays a dragon. He sits on a white horse, holding a round shield in one hand and a spear in the other. Beneath the horse’s hooves lies the dragon, with striped skin and small, spotted wings. Next to it is a small blue devil, defeated and shown from the side. On the right side of the painting, a figure named Biruwit can be seen among the branches of a tree. On the left, a group of soldiers stand ready, holding spears and shields.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; wooden frame painted brown; matte of white mesh; at centre is miniature oil painting on white background with brown matte around it; painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. Written on back: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985"
persona3_adapted:
This small painting shows a bullfight scene. A matador stands in front of a bull, waving a red cape. The painting is done in oil paint on a white background. It is set inside a brown wooden frame. In front of the painting, there is a white mesh cover that looks like a matte sheet.

On the back of the frame, there is handwritten text. It gives the title, "El Paso del Desden," and notes that the painting is very small—only about 1.8 by 2 centimeters. It also includes the artist's name, Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro, and the date, October 1985.

CuratorialText:
Painting; pouches made from the skin of small animals; used for carrying tobacco, smoking barks and weeds. plate 13.
Oil on cardboard
persona3_adapted: This small painting shows pouches made from the skin of small animals. People used these pouches to carry tobacco, as well as smoking barks and weeds. The painting is done in oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Painting; three Javanese men; wearing head-dress, shirt and sarong. Man at the front has a kris or sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. House and trees are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This painting shows three Javanese men. They are wearing traditional clothing: head coverings, shirts, and sarongs. The man in the front has a kris—a type of sword—secured to his back with a cloth wrapped around his waist. In the background, you can see a house and some trees. The artwork is made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait a Javanese woman; wearing head, neck, arm ornament and designed costume. People, trees and two thatched buildings are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This woman from Java is shown from head to toe, wearing a decorated outfit with ornaments on her head, neck, and arms. Behind her, you can see people, trees, and two buildings with thatched roofs. The artist used oil paint on canvas to create this portrait.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear; wearing a helmet, striped shirt and leggings and sarong with waist wrap. He carries a kris either side of his waist wrap and a sword hanging on his left side. Trees and buildings, one with a large flag are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This man from Java stands tall in a full-length portrait. He holds a spear and wears a helmet, a striped shirt, leggings, and a sarong wrapped at his waist. Tucked into both sides of his waist wrap are traditional Javanese daggers called *kris*, and a sword hangs on his left side. In the background, you can see trees and buildings—one of the buildings has a large flag flying from it. The painting is made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr; was principal Chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation; wearing headdress with feathers, ear-rings, necklaces, one made of bear claws, shirt and a decorated blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk and fan made of feathers.
Oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr. He was the principal chief of the Sac and Fox Nation. In the portrait, he wears a feathered headdress, earrings, and several necklaces—one made from bear claws. He is dressed in a shirt and a decorated blanket wrapped around him. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk and a feather fan. The artwork is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; from a collection of seventeen. An Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria; wearing head-band and possum skin cloak.
Oil on canvas.
persona3_adapted: A painting from a set of seventeen. It shows an Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, in Victoria. She is wearing a headband and a cloak made from possum skin. The artwork is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of a Native North American Chief, name unknown; wearing two feathers in his hair, neck ornament, decorated frill shirt, blanket wrap and holding a Pipe-tomahawk and tobacco pouch.
Oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of a Native North American chief. We don't know his name. He wears two feathers in his hair, a necklace, a shirt with decorative frills, and a wrapped blanket. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk and a tobacco pouch. The artwork is oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; side view of a Javanese soldier holding a kris and circular shield; wearing head-dress, top garment and serong, with a kris-sheath secure to his back with his waist wrap. People, trees and the top of a building are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: A painting showing a Javanese soldier from the side. He holds a traditional dagger called a kris and a round shield. He wears a headdress, a top garment, and a cloth wrap called a serong. A sheath for the kris is tucked into the cloth around his waist, resting against his back. In the background, you can see people, trees, and the top of a building. This artwork was made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; five decorated pipe bowls featuring men, bears, and a woman; below is a decorated tomahawk pipe. plate 8.
Oil on cardboard
persona3_adapted: This painting shows five decorated pipe bowls. The carvings on the bowls include images of men, bears, and a woman. Below them is a decorated tomahawk pipe. This artwork is labeled as "plate 8." It was painted with oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Framed painting; portrait of Sina Kavaliku, seated on the ground, wearing a western-style dress, her hair in plaits and a flower in her hair. Tonga. 1951.
Oil on canvas.
persona3_adapted: This is a painted portrait of a woman named Sina Kavaliku. She is sitting on the ground and wearing a Western-style dress. Her hair is in braids, and she has a flower tucked into it. The painting was made in Tonga in 1951. It is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona3_adapted:
This small, rounded pottery bottle, called an alabastron, was made in ancient Greece around the 7th century BCE. It was used to hold perfumed oil. The vase was shaped on a potter's wheel and has a small mouth with a sloping rim, a rounded base, and a small handle on the side.

The decoration is painted in black and purple on a pale clay background. Some details of the animals are scratched into the surface. The main scene shows a lion and two griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head and wings of an eagle. They are separated by a simple, tree-like design. Small decorative shapes fill the empty spaces around them.

Above this scene is a band of flower-like patterns. Below are simpler designs made of dots and S-shaped lines. The bottom of the vase has simple lines, and the rim is decorated with small tongue-like shapes.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona3_adapted: IWM has a collection of about 11 million photographs showing conflicts from the First World War up to the present day.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona3_adapted: The collection shows what British and Commonwealth forces did during wartime, along with views from around the world. You'll find photos taken by official military photographers, news reporters, and everyday people—like soldiers, nurses, and civilians who were there or saw what happened. These images help us understand how war affects people's lives. The collection also features work by well-known photographers, including Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.
persona3_adapted: The photographs are displayed with original documents that show us how and why these pictures were taken.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.
persona3_adapted:
**Military Uniforms, Flags, and Insignia**  
This collection brings together uniforms, flags, badges, and insignia from around the world. It includes pieces from all branches of the armed forces and military institutions—not just from Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also from Allied nations and even former enemies. Each item tells a story about the people and countries involved.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona3_adapted:
**Personal Treasures and Everyday Objects**  
This collection brings together a wide range of items that held deep personal meaning for the people who owned them. You'll find objects used to help people escape or avoid capture, belongings from prisoners of war and civilians held in camps, and "trench art" (decorative items made by soldiers from war materials). There are also souvenirs picked up on battlefields, objects made to remember important events, food and money, smoking-related items, toys and games, and things connected to the Kindertransport (the rescue of children from Nazi Europe) and the Holocaust. The collection also includes items from life on the home front during wartime and from peacetime.

CuratorialText:
Orders, Medals and Decorations
An important collection comprising orders, medal and decorations issued to service personnel and civilians.
persona3_adapted:
**Medals and Awards for Service**

This collection features medals and awards given to both military members and civilians for their service and achievements.

CuratorialText:
Equipment
An extensive collection of equipment used in twentieth and twenty-first-century warfare, including radios, cipher machines, radars, cameras, medical equipment and even pigeon baskets.
persona3_adapted:
**Equipment**  
A large collection of tools and devices used in wars from the 1900s to today. This includes radios, code-making and code-breaking machines, radar systems, cameras, medical gear, and even baskets used to carry pigeons.

CuratorialText:
Weapons and Ammunition
A major reference collection that includes firearms, edged weapons, trench clubs or concussive weapons, ordnance and munitions.
persona3_adapted:
**Weapons and Ammunition**

This is a large and important collection of military equipment. It includes guns, knives and bladed weapons, clubs used in trench warfare, and large weapons like cannons, along with their ammunition.

CuratorialText:
Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships
An internationally renowned collection including models, battle tanks, maritime equipment and aircraft components. Most of the collection of artillery and large military equipment is on show at IWM London or IWM Duxford.
persona3_adapted:
**Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships**  
This world-famous collection includes model vehicles, battle tanks, ship equipment, and aircraft parts. Most of the large military equipment and artillery is displayed at IWM London or IWM Duxford.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona3_adapted: IWM’s Sound Collection holds more than 35,000 recordings about conflicts from 1914 to today. It’s the largest collection of its kind in the world, with over 60,000 hours of material and interviews in more than 15 languages. Alongside interviews, you’ll find speeches, sound effects, radio shows, and music. The recordings capture the wartime experiences of everyday people—soldiers, activists, factory workers, and journalists. It also includes famous figures like Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and singer Vera Lynn. This unique set of oral history interviews has been used in important academic research and has appeared in bestselling books, movies, TV shows, radio programs, and podcasts. You can listen to thousands of these recordings on the IWM website, which adds new material regularly.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona3_adapted:
The IWM has been running a Film Archive for over a hundred years. Right from the start, the museum recognized that film is a valuable primary source—a direct record of events. Its huge collection shows the causes, events, and aftermath of conflicts, from World War I to today. It's a fantastic resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection is always growing and now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide range of material: public information films, service films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two major collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing project to digitize the films means you can watch many of them online for free, with newly-digitized material added as soon as it's ready.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona3_adapted:
**Private Papers**  
The IWM keeps more than 20,000 personal collections of private papers. These are mostly unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs written mainly by British and Commonwealth soldiers and nurses since 1914, as well as by civilians during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from people who suffered or survived the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona3_adapted: We also take care of a large collection of documents from other countries. This includes the official British records of the major war crimes trials held in Nuremberg and Tokyo, as well as related papers from World War II that mostly deal with Germany and Japan's war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona3_adapted: This collection is one of the world's most important displays of British art from the 1900s. It includes many famous works created for British government programs during the First and Second World Wars. These programs hired the best artists of the time, including leading figures of the modern art movement. Among them were Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which shows how the war art programs operated day to day and offers fascinating insights into the artists' experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona3_adapted: Our Library holds more than 150,000 items that focus on wars and conflicts from the 1900s to today, especially those involving Britain and Commonwealth countries. In addition to information about military groups and the history of specific battles, you'll find personal stories, instruction guides, and materials that explore the economic, social, and cultural sides of war. We also have magazines, newspapers, short-lived printed items (like posters and pamphlets), propaganda, and online resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona3_adapted:
**The Sinking of HMS *Laurentic***  
*Painted by Worsley*  

In this painting, you can see the British ship *HMS Laurentic* sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its front end (the bow) is sticking up into the air. In the front of the picture, a lifeboat is filled with sailors. They are lit up against the dark night by a small light. Another lifeboat appears on the right side, and tiny dots of light in the distance show there are more lifeboats. On the left side, you can just make out the top of a German submarine (a U-boat).  

*HMS Laurentic* was originally a passenger ship for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with large guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a warship that could also carry cargo. This was the first ship that the artist, Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Foreland, in Ulster, by a German U-boat captain named Otto Kretschmer (who commanded U-99) on the night of November 3, 1940.  

Worsley painted the original version of this scene right after the attack, while he was on leave as a survivor. The War Artists' Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought that painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a traveling exhibition of war art. But the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed on the way, and the original was lost. The WAAC then asked Worsley to paint this replacement, which is the picture you see here.  

*(Based on information from the 1995 exhibition catalog, Burstow Gallery, Brighton)*

CuratorialText:
image: a canopy of camouflage nets suspended over a road that leads to a cluster of industrial buildings painted with a camouflage patters. A group of figures gather in the background.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee purchase
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This photograph shows a road leading to a group of factory buildings. The buildings are painted with a camouflage pattern, and a canopy of camouflage nets hangs overhead. In the background, a small group of people can be seen gathered together.

**History note:** This image was bought by the War Artists Advisory Committee, a British government group that hired artists to document World War II.

image: a coastal radar station with the radar equipment and masts in the centre background. In the foreground are several buildings with personnel in dark uniforms milling around outside. The sea is visible in the background to the left.

War Artists Advisory Committee purchase
persona3_adapted:
A coastal radar station is shown, with the radar equipment and masts standing in the center background. In the foreground, several buildings can be seen, with personnel in dark uniforms gathered outside. The sea is visible in the background to the left.

CuratorialText:

CuratorialText:

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Two warships moored at Plymouth dock with hundreds of people standing on the dockside in the foreground. There are two cranes on either side of the composition and other vessels are visible on the water in the background.

War Artists Advisory Committee commission
persona3_adapted:
Two warships are tied up at the dock in Plymouth. In the front of the scene, hundreds of people stand on the dockside. Two cranes appear on each side of the picture, and other ships can be seen on the water in the background.

This artwork was created as part of a government program during wartime. The War Artists Advisory Committee asked artists to record scenes like this one.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: A view looking down at men sitting in rows, listening to the proceedings at the Nuremberg trials through headphones. Behind them is a row of soldiers.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee commission
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This photograph shows a courtroom scene from the Nuremberg trials, which took place after World War II. We are looking down at rows of men sitting and listening to the proceedings through headphones. Behind them, a line of soldiers stands guard.

This image was created as part of a special program during the war. The British government set up a committee called the War Artists Advisory Committee. Their job was to hire artists to record and document the war and its aftermath. This photograph was one of the works they commissioned (paid for and requested) for that purpose.

CuratorialText:
Object description
Image: A view of a pond from a boat. On the pond are floating supports, suspending a large camouflage net over the water. To the right is another boat containing three men inspecting the nets.
Physical description
Framed and mounted watercolour painting.
persona3_adapted: A watercolor painting, framed and mounted, shows a pond seen from a boat. Floating supports hold up a large camouflage net above the water. On the right, another boat carries three men who are examining the nets.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: A reconstruction of the sinking of a number of merchant ships by German U-boats in the North Atlantic. Merchant sailors and debris from a destroyed ship are in the water in the foreground. To the right a merchant ship is in the process of sinking, with the stern end submerged and the bow end pointing skywards. In the background, other merchant vessels are being hit by enemy fire.
persona3_adapted: A scene shows the aftermath of a German submarine attack on merchant ships in the North Atlantic. In the foreground, sailors from the sunken ships are struggling in the water, surrounded by wreckage. To the right, one ship is going down, with its back end underwater and its front end pointing up toward the sky. In the distance, more merchant ships are being struck by enemy fire.

CuratorialText: A rough for a furniture mural. whole: text is split between left, middle and right side, with separate images associated with each. image: (left) blueprint illustrating furniture with aircraft, tanks and ships in background. (middle) various types of furniture arranged over a blueprint of a house with factories in the background. (right) birds eye view of a port, mainly in yellow, with modern buildings and furniture floating above the buildings. text: FROM WAR PRODUCTION THROUGH AUSTERITY TO BETTER TIMES
persona3_adapted:
This is a sketch for a large wall painting about furniture. The design is divided into three parts, each with its own picture and text.

On the left: A blueprint shows furniture, with war machines like planes, tanks, and ships in the background.

In the middle: Different types of furniture are arranged over a house blueprint, with factories behind them.

On the right: A bird's-eye view of a port, mostly in yellow, with modern buildings and furniture floating above them.

The text across the whole piece reads: "FROM WAR PRODUCTION THROUGH AUSTERITY TO BETTER TIMES."

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This drawing shows a view across a road lined with trees. On the other side of the road is a high wall that borders a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti on it that says "Nuclear state = Police state," followed by an anarchy symbol and then "One World." Behind the wall on the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right, there is a junction where another road leads off to the left.

The artist, Eric Rimmington, made this drawing in the summer of 1983. He had discovered an old train station in Mildrove Grove and began working on a series of ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While he was working on the second drawing, he realized that the train line he was looking at was used several times a week to carry nuclear waste from Sizewell nuclear power station to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel reprocessing plant. Over the next six weeks, he found it impossible to ignore this fact, and it became deeply connected to every mark he made in his drawings.

Rimmington created this series during a time of strong anti-nuclear protests. People were protesting both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. In 1979, the British government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be placed in the UK. This decision sparked a series of marches and protests. One of the most famous was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981 and was at its peak in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. Also in 1979, an accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people even more worried about the safety of nuclear power.

Even today, nuclear waste—and how it is transported and stored—continues to be a source of protest and concern. In fact, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly commissioned a report on the safety of nuclear waste trains for this very reason.

persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This photograph shows the inside of a reporting room. Two large circular maps on the wall show the East Anglia region. Around these maps, women wearing dark blue uniforms are standing. In the background, you can see information boards on the wall. In the front of the picture, more women are working at their desks.

**How this object came to be here:**
This work was bought by the War Artists Advisory Committee.

War Artists Advisory Committee purchase

**Object Description**

In the front of the image, you can see tall metal towers used for sending signals. Behind them are wooden towers for receiving signals. Closer to the viewer, there are piles of tires, some plants and bushes, and a line of barbed wire fencing.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona3_adapted:
**The Image**
In this photograph, five children are gathered around a table in a room lit by a large window. An older woman sits at the center, holding the youngest child on her lap. On the table sits a large "Spotted-Dick" pudding (a traditional British dessert), along with several bowls and utensils. The woman is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for his portion.

**The Story Behind the Image**
This picture shows Mrs. Norris of Cookham, along with five of the seven children from London who were staying with her during World War II. By January 1941, the children had already been living with her for sixteen months. The original caption describes Mrs. Norris as an ideal foster parent, and notes that many other families like hers were doing the same thing.

During the war, over a million children were moved from cities in organized school groups to safer areas. Because there were so many children, placing them in private homes was the main way to find them shelter. Thanks to the kindness and care of people like Mrs. Norris, city children were able to adjust well to their new surroundings.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona3_adapted:
This painting shows a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a large machine called a lathe in a factory. She was only 21 years old and had never worked in a factory before, but she was so good at her job that she was noticed by a government committee as an "outstanding factory worker."

The artist, Laura Knight, originally planned to paint Loftus in a studio. But the Ministry of Supply asked her to paint Loftus at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport, Wales. There, Loftus was making a part for a Bofors gun—a job that was considered the most difficult in the factory. Normally, it took eight or nine years of training to learn how to do it.

At the time, Loftus’s quick success was a big win for the government’s public image. However, some people have suggested that she was placed at that particular machine just to make a good impression for the war effort.

For Laura Knight, painting industrial machinery was completely new. But critics at the time praised her for getting the technical details right. In a way, Knight was also proving herself in a world that was usually run by men—just like Loftus was.

Knight was known for painting circus performers and dancers, and you can see that influence here. She focuses on Loftus’s balance and posture as she works, making the scene feel graceful and powerful at the same time.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona3_adapted: In this scene, we are looking down a train platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left corner, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers placed on the ground. A nurse is caring for them, while people in uniforms from the Order of St John stand on the platform, lifting stretchers off the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats stand together, some with bandaged arms or legs. The top half of the picture shows the station roof, filled with steam and smoke from the train below.

image: A line of Indian lancers charge at Turkish infantry across a valley, with high ground visible in the background. In the foreground two Turkish infantrymen lie on the ground as they are speared by two Indian lancers.

Framed and glazed oil painting.
persona3_adapted:

In the front of the picture, two Turkish soldiers lie on the ground as they are speared by two Indian lancers.

CuratorialText:
image: a troop of Indian Lancers on horseback advance towards the viewer along a dusty road, passing the derelict inn. The road runs through a valley and there are further hills in the background.

Wellington House commission, transferred to Dept and Ministry of Information and then to the Imperial War Museum
persona3_adapted:
A group of Indian soldiers on horseback, known as Lancers, ride toward the viewer along a dusty road. They pass by an old, abandoned inn. The road winds through a valley, with more hills visible in the distance.

CuratorialText:
image: Indian troops, wearing distinctive Sikh turbans, move timber and provisions from two military trucks to a waiting train in the background.
