Imperial War Museum commission
persona3_adapted:
Indian soldiers, easily recognized by their traditional Sikh turbans, are unloading timber and supplies from two military trucks. They are loading these materials onto a train waiting in the background. In the foreground, three soldiers carry wooden planks toward the viewer. This scene takes place near Pozières on the Somme in northern France, just behind the front lines of the battlefield.

This photograph was created as part of a commission for the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText:
image: a group of Indian infantrymen, all wearing distinctive Sikh turbans, dismantle a strongpoint near Pozières on the Western Front. The strongpoint consists of earth, brick, sandbags and corrugated iron and is the prominent feature in a flat, largely desolate and battle scarred landscape.
History note
Imperial War Museum commission
persona3_adapted:
A group of Indian soldiers, each wearing the distinctive turban of the Sikh faith, are shown taking apart a fortified position near Pozières on the Western Front. The strongpoint is built from earth, bricks, sandbags, and corrugated iron. It stands out in a flat, empty landscape that has been heavily scarred by battle.

This image was created as part of a commission by the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona3_adapted: In this scene, we are looking along a train platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left corner, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers placed on the ground. A nurse is caring for them, and nearby, uniformed members of the Order of St John are standing on the platform, lifting more stretchers off the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats stand together, several with bandaged arms or legs. The top half of the image shows the station roof, filled with steam and smoke rising from the train below.

CuratorialText: image: A half length portrait of a British officer wearing a red dress uniform jacket with seven medals on his chest, including the DSO and OBE. He also wears the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire around his neck.
persona3_adapted: A half-length portrait shows a British officer in a red dress uniform jacket. He wears seven medals on his chest, including the Distinguished Service Order (DSO) and the Order of the British Empire (OBE). Around his neck, he also wears the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire.

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.
persona3_adapted:
**Label**

In this imagined scene, a skeleton representing Death drives a bulldozer through a war-torn landscape. The ground is covered with rubble—bricks, metal beams, and tires—while a burning object glows on the right. Flags are still standing, but they cross over each other, adding to the chaos rather than bringing order. Almost nothing is left of the buildings except bricks and metal supports.

The artist, Michael Sandle, is mainly a sculptor who has created several public monuments. War is a major theme in his work, shaped by his childhood memories of air raids in Plymouth and his later time in the military. This print is one of six in a series called *The Driver*.

CuratorialText: image: a view through a few trees across the large field of crosses of the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. The coastline is bordered by trees and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.
persona3_adapted: A view through a few trees across a large field of crosses in the British military cemetery at Étaples, on the coast of France. The shoreline is lined with trees, and a sailboat can be seen in the English Channel.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This photograph shows the British Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship looks like it has snapped in two, with only the front and back ends sticking out of the water. The front section is on fire, sending a bright cloud of flames and smoke into the night sky. Another ship can be seen in the background, and two Greek islands are visible on the left and right.

**What Happened:**

On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers, HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard, were sailing along the coast of Turkey. They were heading to the Greek island of Leros, which was held by British forces at the time. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking military officer who was on his way to inspect the island's defenses. The ships were also carrying soldiers, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 PM, the Eclipse hit a mine in the sea between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion caused the ship to sink in just two minutes. Besides its normal crew of about 200 men, the ship was also carrying another 200 soldiers. Only about 100 men survived. They were rescued after spending more than three hours in the water, picked up by a small rescue boat sent from Leros and by boats from the Petard.

General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He wrote down his memories of this terrible event 24 years after it happened.

CuratorialText: image: two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent with their heads resting on khaki bags. Both are wearing dessert gear. The soldier furthest away is reading, but the man nearest the artist has fallen asleep with his book resting on his chest.
persona3_adapted: Two soldiers are lying side by side on the floor of a tent. Their heads rest on khaki bags, and both are wearing desert uniforms. The soldier farther away is reading, but the one closer to us has fallen asleep, with his book resting on his chest.

CuratorialText:
image: A view across the river Spree with the sun rising over the city in the background. There are fortifications and an observation post on the opposite bank with a vessel moored below, and a British Military policeman with a vehicle on the near side.
History note
Artistic Records Committee Commission, December 1980
persona3_adapted:
A view across the River Spree shows the sun rising over the city in the background. On the far bank, there are fortifications and an observation post, with a boat tied up below. On the near side, a British military police officer stands with a vehicle.

**History note:** Commissioned by the Artistic Records Committee in December 1980.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona3_adapted:
This drawing shows a tangle of twisted metal beams, all different sizes. It was inspired by the damage bombs caused to buildings. You can feel the force of the explosion in the way the beams are bent and arranged.

The artist, Paule Vézelay, was born in Bristol. She had lived in Paris for over ten years as an artist, but returned to Bristol during the Second World War. She was never officially hired as a war artist, partly because of her left-wing political views and because her art was abstract. However, with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got permission to sketch the bombed-out ruins of the city.

CuratorialText: image: In the foreground two sailors sit with their backs to the viewer on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun protrudes out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead of them and clouds of smoke rising from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons. The artist has also made several other sketches of ships and sailors in the upper right corner of the image.
persona3_adapted: Two sailors sit on the deck of a ship with their backs to us, while a third stands behind them, leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun is visible above them. They are watching two ships anchored in the distance, where clouds of smoke rise from the hills. The sky is filled with small, floating barrage balloons. In the upper right corner of the image, the artist added several quick sketches of ships and sailors.

CuratorialText: image: British soldiers are in action on a German street, crouching and firing their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers are lying on a Nazi flag bearing a swastika, the left of the two in the process of firing a Bren Gun. Four other infantrymen stand directly behind them to the left, two armed with rifles and one with a Bren Gun. Other British soldiers are visible in the middle distance.
persona3_adapted: British soldiers are shown fighting on a German street. They crouch and fire their guns among the ruins of bombed buildings. Two soldiers lie on a Nazi flag with a swastika. The soldier on the left is firing a Bren Gun, a type of light machine gun. Four other infantrymen stand just behind them to the left. Two carry rifles, and one also has a Bren Gun. More British soldiers can be seen farther away in the background.

CuratorialText: image: two British Royal Army Medical Corps stretcher-bearers stand with their stretchers in a shallow trench in the foreground. The trench leads up to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, marked with an ADS sign, which is housed in the bomb damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. Other heavily bomb damaged buildings are visible in the background.
persona3_adapted: Two British Army medical workers stand in a shallow trench, holding stretchers. The trench leads to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, marked with an "ADS" sign. This station is set up inside the bombed-out remains of a large house in a destroyed village. In the background, you can see other buildings that have also been heavily damaged by bombs.

CuratorialText:
image: the interior of the designers' room in the Old Vic Theatre, Waterloo, London, after suffering the effects of an air raid. The windows have been blown out and internal walls damaged, with the room littered with disturbed papers, designs and a fallen mannequin. The window looks out roughly south towards Lambeth, with the roofs of buildings visible.
Label
Short text of working notes. Inscription verso reads: 'Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941'.
persona3_adapted:
The image shows the inside of a designers' room at the Old Vic Theatre in Waterloo, London, after it was hit by an air raid. The windows have been blown out, and the interior walls are damaged. The floor is covered with scattered papers, designs, and a fallen mannequin. Through the window, you can see roughly south toward Lambeth, with the rooftops of nearby buildings visible.

The label includes a short note written during the work. On the back of the image, there is an inscription that reads: "Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941."

CuratorialText: image: Against a gloomy sunset and upon a grassy mound stands a smoking but unmanned French 75mm field gun, facing left. Below running the length of the composition lies the detritus of battle: a broken limber, four German corpses and two dead mules.
persona3_adapted: Against a dark sunset, a French 75mm field gun sits on a grassy hill. The gun is smoking but has no one operating it, and it faces to the left. Stretching across the bottom of the image is the wreckage of battle: a broken cart used to carry ammunition, four dead German soldiers, and two dead mules.

CuratorialText: image: A convoy at sea being bombarded by enemy aircraft. In the centre of the composition is a large tanker, which has black smoke emanating from it. Other ships can be seen to the left and right with aircraft visible above. Large splashes of water in the sea show several near-misses.
persona3_adapted: A convoy of ships is under attack at sea. Enemy planes are bombing them from above. In the middle of the scene, a large tanker is hit and black smoke pours out of it. Other ships are on both sides, and aircraft can be seen overhead. Big splashes in the water show that several bombs barely missed their targets.

CuratorialText: Powder paint painting, hinged in corners onto a single sheet of white board. Bottom left hinge has previously been cut to allow access to object number. Neatly torn along top and right edges, rather than cut. Previously folded in half vertically.
persona3_adapted: This painting was made with powder paint. It is attached to a single sheet of white board at the corners with hinges. The hinge at the bottom left was cut at some point so the object number could be reached. The top and right edges are neatly torn, not cut. The piece was once folded in half vertically.

CuratorialText: image: eight internees stand behind a barbed wire fence at Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. The word 'Parachutists' is on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses and all but one of them appear to be wearing kippot, indicating their Jewish faith. The other man wears a bowler-style hat.
persona3_adapted: Behind a barbed wire fence at the Hutchinson camp on the Isle of Man, eight men stand together. The word "Parachutists" is painted on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses, and all but one appear to be wearing small round caps called kippot, which show they are Jewish. The other man wears a bowler-style hat.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona3_adapted:
J.M.W. Turner (1775–1851) is widely considered one of the greatest landscape painters of all time. He knew Oxford very well. From the time he was a child, he made many drawings and watercolors of the city's streets, colleges, and the beautiful scenery around it. His famous oil painting of Oxford's High Street is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery.

To celebrate the 250th anniversary of Turner's birth on April 23, 1775, take a look at this selection of his Oxford scenes from the Ashmolean's collections. The museum's Western Art Print Room holds just over 100 watercolors and drawings by Turner, along with many landscape prints. You'll find lively sketches of animals, birds, and fish, as well as atmospheric views of places he visited on his travels across Britain and Europe—from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona3_adapted:
In the second millennium BCE (that's about 4,000 years ago), the island of Crete was home to a complex society that existed before the ancient Greeks. We call these people "the Minoans." 

In 1900, an archaeologist named Sir Arthur Evans began digging at a site on Crete called Knossos. As he worked, he often linked the objects he uncovered to old Cretan myths. He named the building he found the "Palace of Minos," after the legendary king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. 

According to the myth, King Minos ordered the construction of a clever maze called the Labyrinth. Its purpose was to trap the Minotaur—a terrifying creature with a bull's head and a human body. The hero Theseus, who was half-god, killed the Minotaur. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was said to have founded the city of Athens.

These Cretan myths originally came from stories that the Greek and Roman people living in Knossos would have told, long after the Palace had been destroyed.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona3_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. But in Japan, it wasn’t until the early 1800s that landscapes became a major subject in woodblock prints. Around that time, more Japanese people were going on outdoor trips—visiting temples, taking boat rides—and they wanted souvenirs to remember these experiences. In the competitive world of publishing, companies were always looking for something new to sell, and landscape prints turned out to be a fresh and popular idea.

Technical changes also helped make landscape prints possible. One key development was the arrival of a new blue pigment imported from Europe, called Prussian Blue. Unlike older Japanese blues that faded quickly, this bright, stable color gave artists more freedom, especially when painting skies and water.

In this section, we’ve chosen some of the best works by Utagawa Hiroshige, a master of landscape prints, along with selected pieces by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona3_adapted:
Our collection of ancient objects includes world-famous archaeological finds from many different times and places. You'll see items from prehistoric Europe, the ancient Near East, Egypt and Sudan, the Minoan and Mycenaean cultures of Greece, classical Greece, ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, and medieval and later Europe. We also have the museum's original Founding Collection and its historical archives.

In addition, we have one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of plaster casts of Greek and Roman statues in the UK.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.
persona3_adapted: In our Eastern Art collections, you can explore sculptures, fabrics, pottery, and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India, and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.
persona3_adapted: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the world’s top collections of coins. It is especially strong in coins from ancient Greece, Rome, the Celtic world, the Byzantine Empire, medieval Europe, the Islamic world, and China. The collection also includes paper money, tokens, and commemorative medals.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
persona3_adapted: The Western Art collections feature remarkable European paintings, sculptures, and decorative objects from the Renaissance to today. You'll find works by famous artists from earlier centuries, as well as more recent paintings, drawings, watercolors, and prints. The collection also includes English and European ceramics, sculptures, silver items, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's outstanding print collection holds about 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints, many of which can be viewed in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona3_adapted:
This painting started with an early, important job: Turner was asked to create two watercolors that would be turned into engravings for the *Oxford Almanack*, a yearly calendar published by the University Press. The watercolors were so well received that Turner ended up making ten of them between 1798 and 1804. Later, he turned this work and one other into oil paintings for a local print seller and frame maker named James Wyatt.

Turner asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings shown in the scene. He also added people to the painting—university members, clergy, and some women—explaining that the women were included “for the sake of colour,” meaning to add visual interest. This painting is one of the best-documented works Turner ever made, because Wyatt kept the letters Turner wrote to him about it. Those letters were later sold after Wyatt’s death.

The original plan was to make an engraving from the design. But Wyatt decided to commission an oil painting instead, half the size of Turner’s usual canvases, for 100 guineas (a large sum at the time). The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Years later, in the 1830s, when Turner was choosing scenes of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolors, he decided not to paint the High Street again. He felt that in this painting, he had already achieved a level of technical skill that he could not match a second time.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona3_adapted: These four paintings were originally part of a larger religious artwork, called an altarpiece, that has since been lost. They were created around 1520 by the Italian artist Granacci. Two other panels from the same set are now in museums in Oxford and Wales. It's possible that these four pieces were placed on the vertical side sections, or pilasters, of the altarpiece. Granacci was a student of the artist Ghirlandaio, and he was especially inspired by the work of two other artists, Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona3_adapted: These tall, pointed panels were once part of the same religious artwork, but we don't know who made the original piece. It may have had a central panel showing Christ on the Cross at the top. The artist was from Florence, Italy, and worked in a style that was popular in the late Middle Ages. Recently, experts have suggested that the artist might be Bartolomeo di Fruosino. He was a painter and a maker of small, detailed illustrations for books, and he learned his craft from a teacher named Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona3_adapted:
In *The Hunt in the Forest*, the artist Paolo di Dono created a night-time hunting scene that is both unusual and carefully planned. Hunting was a popular activity for wealthy people, with its own traditions, but showing it at night is more playful and may have a hidden meaning rather than being realistic.

To prepare this painting, di Dono first drew a grid on the wooden panel. He placed a single point in the center of the painting to pull your eye deep into the scene. He added details like the hunters' spears, cut branches, logs, and water in the front to help focus attention on that space and on the people and animals that get smaller as they go further back.

Tiny gold specks in the trees would have made the bright colors of the figures stand out against the dark forest. When lit by candles, those gold flecks would have sparkled and seemed to move.

Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was famous in his time for his skill in showing depth, animals, and nature. His nickname, Uccello (which means "bird" in Italian), refers to his paintings of the natural world. He was a versatile artist who also worked on mosaics and stained glass, and this painting combines all those talents into one masterpiece.

*The Hunt* is a type of painting called a *spalliera*, which means it was meant to be placed at shoulder height—either as the back of a decorated chest or set into the wooden wall panels of a room. We don't know who first bought it, but it was clearly made for a grand Renaissance palace, possibly in Urbino (where Uccello worked around 1465) or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona3_adapted: This portrait is similar to several paintings and prints that show King Louis XIII of France riding a horse that is rearing up. Based on the style and the way the paint was applied, it was likely painted by a Flemish artist, or possibly copied from one. The way the king looks in the painting suggests it was made around the year 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona3_adapted:
This small painting shows Mary Magdalene. You can recognize her by the jar of ointment she holds. This refers to a story in the Bible (Luke 7:36-50) where a woman anoints Jesus’ feet with perfume and dries them with her hair. Traditionally, that woman is identified as Mary Magdalene.

The painting may have originally been part of a larger artwork. It was later cut down and then fitted into another panel, with the figure extended to fill the new space. The artist likely worked in Parma, Italy, in the early 1600s, and was part of the group of artists around Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona3_adapted:
This landscape looks like a scene from Éragny, a village in France where the artist lived. It was probably painted in the summer of either 1893 or 1894, just before Félix Pissarro moved away from Éragny to go to Belgium.

Source: Jon Whiteley, *Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum* (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum, 2022)

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona3_adapted:
This is a freehand copy of a painting that was made in the late 1600s or early 1700s. The original was part of a larger artwork on the ceiling of a room called the Loggia di Psiche, located in the Villa Farnesina in Rome. That room was decorated in 1518–19 by the famous artist Raphael and his team of assistants. The scenes they painted there tell the story of Cupid and Psyche, a myth from ancient times.

The specific part that this copy is based on was likely painted by Raphael’s senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The person who made this copy changed the shape of the image from its original curved form into a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona3_adapted: In this still life painting, three objects also show up in other works from Renoir’s later years. The silver cup appears with a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted in Cagnes in 1910, and again in a similar version without the knife, also from 1910. The bronze-colored child statue on the column was included in a small study dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles. The two-part ceramic salt holder—a type of pottery mass-produced in the 1800s in Desvres (in northern France) and Quimper (in Brittany)—appears in at least four other sketches. A separate image of the same child figure, seen from behind, floats in the upper right corner, likely a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked at a pottery factory early in his career, seems to have owned several pieces of this popular kind of pottery and used many different colored and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona3_adapted: Although this portrait of an unknown woman is damaged, it is still a high-quality painting. It shares similarities with the work of the artist Andrea in the 1520s. Andrea spent most of his career in the Italian cities of Siena and Florence, except for a short visit to Rome around 1516. He often worked together with his brother, Raffaello Piccinelli.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona3_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch focused on painting small bouquets and flower arrangements, often writing her age on the finished work, as she did here. The way the stems curve, the full, round roses, and the mix of smaller flowers are inspired by the style of another artist, Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the museum in March 1949 and has never been found.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona3_adapted:
This panel was originally part of a larger religious artwork, called an altarpiece, created between 1433 and 1435 for a church in Florence, Italy. The altarpiece was later taken apart, and its pieces are now spread across different museums and collections.

The main section of the altarpiece showed the Virgin Mary and baby Jesus with four angels. On either side were pairs of saints: Saint Benedict and Saint Nicholas of Bari on one side, and Saint John the Baptist and an Evangelist (likely Saint Matthew) on the other.

This panel comes from the bottom part of the altarpiece, known as the predella. The predella showed scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas. Five of these scenes are still known today: his birth, pilgrims visiting his shrine, him bringing three young people back to life, him providing money for three sisters to get married (which is why he is often shown with three gold balls), and him calming a storm. The panels probably originally had a four-lobed frame shape.

The artist, Bicci, based the design of this panel on an earlier altarpiece by Gentile da Fabriano, created in 1425 for another church in Florence.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona3_adapted: This is a portrait of a professional sculptor. He is shown in his studio, with some of his work on the shelf behind him. In his hands, he holds a small clay figure of a saint, possibly the Virgin Mary. Experts think this painting may have been created by someone in the circle of the de Critz family—a group of artists connected to that well-known family of painters.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona3_adapted: These pages of calligraphy were all written by the same person. Most of them feature *kanshi*—Chinese-style poems. The artist signed them using different versions of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems are dated 1878 and 1880, and a few mention where the poem came from or where it was written. If the calligraphy was created around the same time as the paintings, then this album would be an early work by Sobun, made before he started showing his art in national and international exhibitions.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona3_adapted:
This painting started out in 1857 with a different title: *The Mother's Grave*. At first, it showed only a young boy grieving alone at his mother's grave. We know this because an early drawing of the scene still exists at the Ashmolean Museum. The landscape background was painted in the summer of 1856, based on a real old churchyard in Chingford, Essex.

Around 1862, the artist changed the painting. He added a new background and included the boy's sister, using his own wife, Tryphena, as the model. Every small detail in the picture is meant to make us feel the sadness of the scene. For example, spiderwebs, dewdrops, wild roses, and dandelion seeds all remind us that life is brief and fragile. The boy's loss is also shown through a lamb that is separated from its mother by a tombstone.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona3_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608–1662) took over his father's job as the head gardener for the King's gardens at Oatlands Palace. He added many more interesting natural and man-made objects to the family's collection. In this painting, he is shown with his friend Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth. (The Greek word for brewing is "Zythepsa.") It's possible that Friend donated the shells you see in Tradescant's museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona3_adapted: Lanyon was a key artist in the group that worked in St Ives after World War II. His main focus was on how to show the landscape in his art. This painting captures the push and pull between abstract shapes and recognizable objects, even though the work is otherwise very detailed and realistic.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona3_adapted: A young noble couple sleeps in a close embrace on a palace terrace, attended by a drowsy maid. They are sheltered by a decorative canopy called a *shamiana*. The prince’s sword and arrows lie nearby, ready at hand. This painting was made in Bundi, Rajasthan, around 1760–70, using gouache (a type of opaque watercolor) and gold on paper.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona3_adapted: This painting was once called *A Nun Playing a Piano* when it was sold at auction in 1919 and again in 1936—probably because the woman in the picture is wearing a long veil. But there’s no real reason to think she’s a nun. In fact, it’s almost certainly a portrait of the artist’s daughter, Desirée-Marie Ribot (known as Louise), who often modeled for him. She was born in 1857, and her young appearance matches the style of the painting, which suggests it was made in the late 1860s or early 1870s. After that, the artist’s brushwork became thicker and more noticeable. The dark background and the simple, everyday scene remind us of the work of François Bonvin, an early supporter of the artist, and of Alphonse Legros, who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona3_adapted: The inscription on this painting says: "A wind from the west blows through the layered curtains on the doors. The proud chrysanthemums are falling, but their beauty remains. In the fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers as they cooled, writing poetry through this cool autumn evening."

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona3_adapted: Sinnott is known for creating detailed paintings of people in action. In this work, two men are wrestling. You can see the same smooth, flowing brushstrokes in both the background and the figures. However, the wrestlers stand out because the artist used a lighter touch and slightly different paint application on them. In some spots, the first layer of paint shows through the top layers, which makes the painting feel like it was done quickly and with energy.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona3_adapted: This painting is the only one we can be sure was made by the artist Francesco. It was created in 1447 and is now in a museum in Padua, Italy. Francesco was influenced by another artist, Michele Giambono, and may have worked with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. This panel, along with another one (WA1850.10), was once part of a larger religious artwork, possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. The woman in the painting is Saint Catherine. She was a princess, so she wears a crown and holds a palm branch, which is a symbol of her martyrdom. The wheel next to her represents an earlier attempt to kill her.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona3_adapted:
This small panel shows the Virgin Mary and St John the Evangelist overcome with grief. Their sorrowful figures make it a powerful image for private prayer. It may have been the right half of a small, two-panel painting that could be folded and carried, though no signs of hinges remain today.

The artist is unknown, but their style is very close to that of Jacopo di Cione (who is also thought to have painted another work in this collection). This artist would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading painter in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona3_adapted: Veronese painted a famous version of *The Last Supper* in 1573 for a monastery dining hall in Venice. It got him in trouble with the Inquisition, a religious court, because they thought it was disrespectful. To fix this, he simply changed the title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*—a Bible story that allowed for all the lively, colorful details he had already included. Many copies of this large painting exist. The one in the Ashmolean Museum is a smaller, high-quality copy made by a Venetian artist from the mid-1700s, probably Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona3_adapted: This portrait is a companion piece to Opie's painting of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764–1815), which is also in the collection (WA1934.422). Both works were shown at the Royal Academy in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona3_adapted:
This painting was created in 1886, a time when both Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were using a technique called pointillism, which involves applying small dots of color to build up an image.

The scene shows a view from a bend in a path that leads from a place called Pré du Chêne, across the Epte River, from the village of Éragny to Bazincourt. In the center of the painting, you can see the spire of the Saint-Martin church in Éragny, and on the right, a brick manor house built in the 1500s. The Epte River runs along the line of trees that cuts across the composition.

In 1887, Lucien painted a very similar view using watercolor and bodycolor (a type of opaque paint). Today, the buildings seen from this part of the path are mostly hidden by trees. However, it's clear that in both of Lucien's works, he made the church and manor much larger than they really were, and he left out the farm buildings that actually stand in front of the manor.

Lucien probably knew that his father had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using a looser, more spontaneous style typical of his earlier work.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona3_adapted: This painting offers a distant view of the village of Lauris in southern France. On the left, you can see its distinctive 18th-century castle and garden terrace, with the mountains of the Petit Luberon rising in the background. The artist, Guigou, likely sat or stood on one of the many sandbanks that appear in the dry season along the Durance River, between the towns of Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron. Like most of Guigou's landscapes of Provence, the scene is marked by clear air and strong sunlight. He may have been inspired by other artists who favored wide, panoramic views, but his painting style is unique. Notice how the sky, sand, and river are painted with thin layers of color, brought to life with small, thick dabs of paint and quick, short brushstrokes—a signature technique of the artist.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona3_adapted: This painting is a landscape that feels like a view of the universe, with subtle hints of Daoist philosophy. It was created in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919–2003), who asked for a piece that was mostly blue in color. The writing on the painting says, “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona3_adapted: One of Lucien's most lively and expressive works, painted in June 1925, shows the broom plant in full bloom at Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona3_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722–1800) studied at Oriel College, Oxford, before becoming a clergyman. He served as Headmaster of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and was also a skilled poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777, the same year Warton was chosen to join a famous group called 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who was the Poet Laureate at the time. Thomas Warton’s portrait was also painted by the same artist, Reynolds (that portrait is now at Trinity College, Oxford).

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona3_adapted: Vuillard's style clearly influenced this work. On the back of the painting, there is a rougher, less detailed version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona3_adapted: Bollongier was one of only a few artists in Haarlem during the early 1600s who focused on painting flowers. His style appears to have been influenced by the Bosschaert group of painters, but he arranged the flowers in a more natural-looking way. The glass vase in this painting was one of his favorite items to use. It first shows up in a painting from 1627 and appears for the last time in a flower painting from 1639 (now in the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona3_adapted:
When the Pharaoh ordered that all newborn Hebrew boys be killed, Moses’s mother placed him in a basket made of reeds and set it in the Nile River. His sister stayed nearby to watch over him. Then Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her servants, found the baby, and decided to save him (Exodus 2:2-4).

This painting, known as WA1950.169, is striking because of its strong emotional focus—not on the joyful discovery of Moses, but on his grieving parents. In contrast, the happier scene of Moses being found was a popular subject for artists, while this more sorrowful one is much rarer. (The French painter Nicolas Poussin had treated this same subject earlier, around 1627.)

Painted in 1654 for a patron named Jacques Stella, this work was highly admired by people at the time. They praised the artist’s thoughtful and learned approach to the story, his skill in painting reflections, and the beauty of the landscape. The city shown in the background is based on an artist’s reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx, along with a river-god and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped basket overflowing with fruit and grain), identifies the Nile River.

There are different ideas about what the pipes of Pan (a set of musical pipes) hanging from a tree behind this group might mean. These interpretations are based on the artist’s interest in ancient history and his use of many different visual references. Several copies of this painting were made in the 1600s, and other versions exist, including a tapestry that shows only the right side. Three engraved versions were also created, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona3_adapted: Masaccio’s brother, known as Lo Scheggia, was a versatile artist who created many paintings for people’s homes. This kind of decorative, ceremonial scene was commonly painted on the front panels of wedding chests, called *cassoni*. Even though this painting is quite worn and faded, it still shows enough similarities to Lo Scheggia’s later, more developed work to make art experts confident that he painted it.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona3_adapted: Lemon studied art in Rome and Paris with a teacher named Carolus-Duran, but he grew up in California. He began showing his work at the Royal Academy in London in 1880. In Italy, he met Giovanni Costa and his group of artists called the Etruscans. This small, simple drawing from real life was probably made in the late 1890s.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a person, or "subject," with a flat, simple style and large, statue-like figures. These features reveal how much the artist, Shannon, was influenced by another painter named Puvis de Chavannes. The artwork was likely made around 1910 and is one of several versions of a theme Shannon liked to explore.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a view across the sand dunes near the village of Berck in northern France. The artist painted scenes like this many times, from 1870 until he died in 1898. We know the exact date this particular view was painted. The horizon stretches across the whole scene without any breaks, and there is only one person standing in the front of the picture. These details make the place feel empty and swept by the wind.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona3_adapted: This painting was long thought to be a portrait of the famous violin maker Antonio Stradivari, but that is now known to be incorrect. Instead, it shows a musician or instrument maker from the late 1500s. Some experts have suggested it might be the composer Claudio Monteverdi or the violin maker Gasparo da Salò, but no one knows for sure.

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona3_adapted: In his later years, Calvert created many small paintings on paper using thin oil paints. The colors are soft and quiet, giving the works a dreamlike feel. Most of the pictures show figures from Greek myths. He painted them on a pink background.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona3_adapted:
This painting was long thought to be by the famous artist Guido Reni and was called *Head of a Sibyl*. However, experts have recently discovered that it is actually a copy of a work by another artist, Giovanni Domenico Cerrini.

The same expressive face appears in one of Cerrini’s religious paintings, *The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes*. He originally made that painting for a church in Rome called San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, which was consecrated (officially blessed and opened) in 1646. Today, that painting is kept in the convent attached to the church.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona3_adapted: One of John Linnell's most significant religious works, this painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1835. In 1838, it was sold to the Art Union so that it could be turned into an engraved print. The painting is based on sketches Linnell made in 1829, when he was closely connected with a group of artists called the 'Ancients' in Shoreham. The disciple on the right side of the painting was modeled after Samuel Palmer, another artist. The scene comes from a Bible story in Luke 24: 13-35.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona3_adapted:
Design is something that connects people across different times and cultures. This exhibit brings together two areas that are usually shown separately: fashion and textiles, along with decorative and industrial arts. By doing this, we can better understand what drives us to create and design things.

You’ll see outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection, which show the bold and playful way one of the world’s most famous fashion icons mixed and matched styles. Around them, you’ll find groups of unique and important works of design, fashion, and textiles that highlight creative and clever ways of expressing ideas.

A recent highlight of the gallery is a display of 38 bright and flashy outfits from the collection of Yolanda Cellucci, a business owner and fashion icon from Boston. Her shop, Yolanda’s, in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a popular place for fancy evening wear and wedding dresses. It became a well-known name in the area and a symbol of glamour and sparkle in the late 1900s. In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to the museum, along with old photos and materials that tell the story of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.

"That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished." Her lively, lifelike stone sculptures show abolitionists and social reformers, explore Indigenous identity, and breathe new life into religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona3_adapted: The boreal forest is one of the last truly wild places on Earth. It stretches across the northern part of the world, just below the Arctic Circle, and is home to 3.7 million people, 85 kinds of animals, 32,000 types of insects, and 2 billion migrating birds. This exhibit invites you to explore this amazing region through personal stories, specially made objects, hands-on activities, and stunning photos and videos. You’ll learn why the boreal forest matters to the whole planet: it helps control the Earth’s climate, is the largest land-based ecosystem in the world, stores huge amounts of fresh water, and holds two to three times more carbon than tropical forests do.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona3_adapted:
Here is the adapted text, written for a native English speaker without specialized art knowledge.

---

Did you know that Salem played a part in the story of the Declaration of Independence? The connection comes through a local printer named Ezekiel Russell. In 1776, he helped print and spread the Declaration, making sure people could read it for themselves.

This exhibition is timed to mark two big anniversaries: 250 years since the American Revolution, and 400 years since European settlers first arrived in Salem. The show is located in the small, cozy James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery.

Inside, you’ll find two of the earliest printed versions of the Declaration of Independence, known as broadsides (single large sheets of paper meant to be posted in public). Alongside them are letters, newspapers, pamphlets, and other broadsides from the Revolutionary era. Together, these items highlight the core ideas the nation was built on: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.

They were made over more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera, and many other filmmakers for the Salem Film Fest. The films cover a wide range of topics—from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which marks 400 years since the city was settled, while also honoring the Indigenous people who lived here long before. The goal is to bring the Salem community together and help build a lively, sustainable city for future generations.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B.
