The exhibition starts with **"A is for Always Indigenous"** to honor the Native communities who have lived on this land for thousands of years—long before the museum was built here. It ends with **"Z is for Zoology,"** which marks the return of a leatherback turtle specimen caught in 1885, a favorite among longtime visitors.

Salem is a city full of stories that matter locally, nationally, and even globally. For example, in 1877, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful long-distance phone call from Salem. Parker Brothers created the board game Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed a law recognizing Salem as the birthplace of the United States National Guard.

Using items from PEM’s huge collection, *Salem Stories* brings together more than 100 objects. These include paintings, sculptures, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, firsthand accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z format makes it easy and fun to explore Salem’s history, from the past to the present day. This is a story that keeps growing and changing throughout the exhibition.

**Location:** Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This gallery tells the story of India’s journey to build a new national identity after gaining independence from British rule in 1947. The artworks here were created by some of India’s most famous 20th-century artists. They wanted to find a way of making art that felt truly Indian—art that could express their experiences, struggles, hopes, and dreams.

The second part of the gallery focuses on the 1800s, a time when India was under foreign control. It looks at how that long and difficult history affected the way Indian people were shown in art. Some of the oldest objects in the museum’s collection are on display here, including sculptures made from unfired clay, paintings on mica (a shiny mineral), and Kalighat paintings (a style from Kolkata). These pieces help us think about the lasting images and ideas of India that are still common today—and whether they are accurate or fair.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.
persona3_adapted: This collection features a wide variety of modern glass artworks created by over 40 of the most important glass artists from the 1900s to today. The pieces were given to PEM in 2022 by New York collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer. What makes this collection special is the incredible range of shapes, sizes, and methods used to work with glass. The Pforzheimers' gift greatly adds to PEM's already well-known historic glass collection, while also highlighting the amazing beauty that can come from mixing sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them
persona3_adapted:
**The Pod: A Place to Explore Nature and Art**

The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, a space that helps us think about our relationship with nature through art, interesting objects, and hands-on activities. Designed for visitors of all ages, this family-friendly center includes art displays, changing exhibits, things you can touch and play with, and areas where you can make your own art.

Here, you can grow your awareness of nature, try new ways of experiencing the world, and tap into your own creativity. You'll find a life-size wire tree made by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline, which was modeled after a real tree on PEM's campus. You can watch dancer Laura Kathrein move like different animals and show what it's like to live in local habitats. And you can create your own imaginary creature, inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the world.

What creative spark will you feel during your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a "pod"? A pea pod, an iPod, or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the director of the Art & Nature Center and the curator of The Pod, shares her idea of a pod and what you'll find when you visit this new, multi-sensory exhibit space.

There is something for everyone here, from amazing nature-inspired artworks—both new and historical—to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will love the Wonder Zone, which has activity bins, a light table, and picture books just for them.

CuratorialText: This extraordinary series of events between June 1692 and March 1693 led to the deaths of 25 innocent women, men and children who were wrongfully convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and grievous wrongs that occurred still provoke us to reflect and reckon with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy as told through the voices and with the possessions of those directly involved. This ongoing installation tells this story through court documents and authentic historic objects presented as tangible fragments directly tied to people in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick — each illuminates an aspect of individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these harrowing events. The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.
persona3_adapted:
Between June 1692 and March 1693, a series of shocking events led to the deaths of 25 innocent people—women, men, and children—who were wrongly convicted of crimes they didn't commit. More than 300 years later, these personal tragedies and serious injustices still make us stop and think about what those involved went through.

This ongoing exhibit tells the true story of the Salem witch trials, using the voices and belongings of the people who lived through it. You'll see court documents and real historical objects—like a handwritten petition, a carved loom, and a walking stick—each one a small, powerful piece of the past. These items help show what life was like for individuals in Salem and nearby towns in the late 1600s, and remind us that real people were deeply affected by these terrible events.

*The Salem Witch Trials 1692* is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We also thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

CuratorialText: The artist researched the museum’s vast and varied holdings, diving deep to find unexpected connections across time, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned her findings into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with X-Acto blades. After applying the stencils to the walls, she and her assistant used a variety of spray-paint and graffiti techniques, including drop shadows, high contrast, and layering, to apply color to the walls and give dimensionality and life to her forms. Organic forms and curvilinear lines emerged as unifying design motifs that speak to the natural world. As you move deeper into these galleries, floral forms give way to enchanting fauna and larger-than-life presentations of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’ labor-intensive contemporary approach to painting connects to the artistry and skill embedded within the historical objects in PEM’s collection, creating a fascinating interplay between artistic practice then and now. What discoveries await? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
persona3_adapted: The artist spent a long time exploring the museum’s huge and diverse collection, searching for surprising links between different time periods, cultures, and materials. She turned what she found into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand using X-Acto knives. After placing the stencils on the walls, she and her assistant used various spray-paint and graffiti techniques, like adding shadows, strong contrasts, and layers, to bring color and depth to her shapes. Organic, flowing lines became the main design theme, reflecting the natural world. As you walk further into these rooms, flower shapes give way to charming animals and oversized versions of both familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’s careful, hands-on approach to painting highlights the artistry and skill found in the historical objects at PEM, creating an interesting conversation between art made long ago and art made today. What will you discover? This exhibition is in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.

CuratorialText: As one of the 20th century’s most critically acclaimed and genre-blurring artists, Noguchi created a remarkable design legacy encompassing sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics and designs for public spaces such as gardens, playgrounds and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer" shines a light on the artist’s limitless creativity and social consciousness. Discover the evolution of Noguchi’s design work, particularly the way he created environments through theatrical set design, furniture and inclusive public spaces — all of which were intentionally designed to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain incredibly popular, such as the celebrated coffee table and Akari light sculptures that you may recognize in stores today. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also excelled at working collaboratively and forming close relationships with other creative professionals, particularly with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful creative relationships are also highlighted within the exhibition.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Isamu Noguchi was one of the most celebrated and innovative artists of the 1900s. He worked in many different areas, including sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics, and public spaces like gardens, playgrounds, and plazas. This exhibition, organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, is called *Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer."* It highlights his endless creativity and his concern for society.

In the show, you can see how Noguchi’s design work changed over time. He created environments through theater sets, furniture, and public spaces that were open to everyone. All of these were made with the goal of improving how people lived. Many of his designs are still very popular today. For example, you might recognize his famous coffee table or his Akari light sculptures, which are still sold in stores.

Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” This means he saw art in everything around him.

Noguchi also worked well with others and built close friendships with other creative people. Some of his most important collaborations were with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller, and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. The exhibition also highlights these creative partnerships.

CuratorialText: In the nearly two decades since PEM featured the crowd-pleasing exhibition Origami Now!, contemporary origami has continued to expand into exciting new styles of expression and invention. This multigenerational exhibition in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center highlights origami as not just an exciting art form, but also an important influence on new innovations in science, engineering, math and more. See works from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. In addition to showcasing masterworks of origami, PEM will highlight what is emerging in contemporary origami and design, ranging from incredibly complex artistic models and folding nano structures, to origami-inspired emergency shelters. Investigate origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to investigate and participate in the artistic process of origami.
persona3_adapted:
It’s been almost 20 years since PEM hosted the popular exhibition *Origami Now!*, and since then, the art of paper folding has grown in exciting new directions. This new exhibition, located in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, brings together artists from different generations to show that origami is more than just a beautiful craft—it’s also inspiring breakthroughs in science, engineering, math, and more.

You’ll see work from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top paper-folding masters and a Massachusetts local. Alongside stunning examples of origami as an art form, the exhibition also looks at what’s happening in origami today—from incredibly complex paper sculptures and tiny folded structures, to real-world inventions like emergency shelters designed using origami principles.

Explore how origami is inspiring new ideas in robotics, medicine, and sustainability. With hands-on displays and video tutorials, visitors of all ages can try folding for themselves and see how the creative process works.

CuratorialText: View iconic contemporary artworks that reach back into millennia-old art practices. The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, representing the incredible breadth and depth of Contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition charts watershed moments in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vivid history of creativity that predates the arrival of Europeans by many thousands of years. The exhibition features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, among them several undisputed masterworks from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these significant works have never before left the country. The Stars We Do Not See is curated especially to introduce North American audiences to customary forms and styles, such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes colloquially referred to as “dot paintings”), ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video and photography that acknowledge and innovate upon tradition.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This exhibition brings together powerful modern artworks that are rooted in traditions stretching back thousands of years. *The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art* is the largest show of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever held in North America. It showcases the amazing range and depth of contemporary Indigenous Australian art.

The exhibition highlights key turning points in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a long and vibrant creative history that began many thousands of years before Europeans arrived. It features nearly 200 works by more than 130 artists, including several masterpieces from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these important pieces have never been shown outside Australia before.

*The Stars We Do Not See* is designed especially to introduce North American audiences to traditional forms and styles. These include the famous map-like paintings from the Central and Western Desert regions (sometimes called “dot paintings”), bark paintings using natural ochre pigments from far northern Australia, and bold, experimental woven works. The exhibition also includes striking pieces made with neon, video, and photography—works that both honor and build on tradition.

CuratorialText: Come and experience the liveliness of a drinking party, the opulence of a royal wedding and poetic evocation of spring on a delicate dish. Discover plants and animals, myths and symbols and decipher the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." With more than 30 highlights from the museum's wide-ranging Chinese collection spanning 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements crystallized in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies and celebrations. Located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.
persona3_adapted: Come and see the energy of a drinking party, the luxury of a royal wedding, and a poetic image of spring—all painted on a delicate dish. Explore plants, animals, myths, and symbols, and learn what the Chinese character for "Double Happiness" means. This exhibition features over 30 standout pieces from the museum's large Chinese collection, which spans 3,000 years. It celebrates China's artistic achievements as shown in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies, and celebrations. The exhibition is in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

CuratorialText:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only Philadelphians painted by John Singleton Copley, the greatest artist in the American colonies prior to the Revolution. Mifflin was an ardent patriot and by the time this portrait was made, had established himself as a successful merchant; later he rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army, and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States achieved independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a portable loom, which may symbolize the couple's endorsement of the colonists' boycott of English goods.
Famous as one of Copley's finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the height of his powers. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley was self-taught. He developed a highly finished style that rendered the features, costumes, and settings of his subjects with remarkable accuracy. Copley always kept a keen eye on his competition, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale, who had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family that was admired for its portrayal of family unity and affection. Here, Copley depicts not only the features and costumes of his sitters with his famed skill, but creates an image of marriage as an affectionate, equal partnership--an innovative concept in American portraiture at the time.
persona3_adapted:
**Thomas Mifflin and His Wife, Sarah: A Portrait of Partnership**

This painting shows Thomas Mifflin (1744–1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (around 1747–1790). They were the only people from Philadelphia ever painted by John Singleton Copley, who was widely considered the best artist in the American colonies before the Revolutionary War.

Thomas Mifflin was a strong supporter of American independence. By the time this portrait was made, he had already become a successful businessman. Later, he would serve as a major general in the Continental Army and, after the United States won its freedom, he was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania. Sarah Morris married Thomas in 1767. She was described as talented, clever, and a supportive partner.

In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a small, portable loom. This detail may have been a way to show that the couple supported the colonists' boycott of British goods—a political statement woven into the picture.

This painting was famous as one of Copley’s finest works soon after it was finished. It shows the artist at the peak of his skill. Copley was born in Boston in 1738 to a poor immigrant family and taught himself to paint. He developed a very polished style that captured people’s faces, clothing, and surroundings with amazing accuracy.

Copley always kept an eye on what other artists were doing. He may have been inspired to paint both Thomas and Sarah together in one canvas by a fellow artist named Charles Willson Peale. Peale had recently painted a group portrait of the Cadwalader family of Philadelphia, which was praised for showing family closeness and affection. In this painting, Copley does more than just show his subjects’ faces and clothes with his famous skill. He also creates an image of marriage as a loving, equal partnership—a fresh and unusual idea for American portraits at the time.

CuratorialText: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most prolific and accomplished silversmith in late 1700s Boston. His shop produced domestic and ecclesiastical objects for clients belonging to the same civic, military, political, and religious organizations as Revere. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved ornament of C-scrolls, foliage, and shells epitomizes the Rococo style of his early career. Equally renowned as an engraver of bookplates, trade cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, Revere probably executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) depicts the silversmith with engraving tools, holding a teapot with the same low, domed cover and slightly pointed, pinecone finial as this example. Could they be one and the same?
persona3_adapted:
Paul Revere is best known as a patriot from the American Revolution, but he was also the most skilled and productive silversmith in Boston during the late 1700s. His workshop made objects for both homes and churches, for customers who belonged to the same community groups—civic, military, political, and religious—that Revere himself was part of.

This teapot has an upside-down pear shape, with carved decorations of curving lines, leaves, and shells. These features are typical of the Rococo style, which Revere used early in his career. He was also famous for engraving bookplates, business cards, magazine pictures, and political cartoons, so he probably did the decoration on this teapot himself.

A well-known 1768 portrait of Revere by John Singleton Copley (now at the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) shows the silversmith holding engraving tools and a teapot. That teapot has the same low, rounded lid and slightly pointed, pinecone-shaped knob on top as this one. Could they be the same teapot?

CuratorialText: Human rights and social issues often figure in Pippin’s work, but this painting is particularly overt in its treatment of racism. Clouds hover over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a burly white man holding a noose. The two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, here painted brown instead of green. At the center, Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, segregating white and black machinists and servicemen. Pippin includes himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.
persona3_adapted:
In this painting, Pippin addresses racism in a very direct way. On one side, we see a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a large white man holding a noose. Dark clouds hang over them. Across from these threatening figures stands the Statue of Liberty, but here it is painted brown instead of its usual green.

In the center, a character named Mr. Prejudice is driving a wedge into a V-shaped symbol that stands for "victory." This wedge separates white and Black workers and soldiers. Pippin painted himself as one of the soldiers, wearing his World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.

CuratorialText: While the bed chambers of chic early Philadelphia houses often featured a high chest (for storing textiles; 1957-129-1) and dressing table (for cosmetics and hair accessories; 2012-59-1), this pairing represents the pinnacle of design, cabinetmaking, and carved ornament. The raised space underneath the high chest distinguishes it as a confection of North America since the British had abandoned that design by the 1730s. While most high chests and dressing tables fit within well-defined groups, the proportions and shaping of the rails and the legs of this pairing relate to only one other dressing table, making it difficult to identify the maker. The choice mahogany, with an even surface and swirling figure, signifies the status of the commission. And a highly skilled carver ornamented it profusely—most conspicuously with a narrative from Aesop’s fable of “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moralistic tales were the only fiction Quakers were allowed to read.
persona3_adapted:
In the bedrooms of stylish early Philadelphia homes, you'd often find a tall chest for storing linens and a dressing table for cosmetics and hair accessories. The set described here represents the very best of design, furniture-making, and decorative carving.

The raised space under the tall chest is a feature that makes it distinctly North American—by the 1730s, British furniture makers had stopped using this design. While most tall chests and dressing tables fit into clear categories, the shape and proportions of the rails and legs on this set match only one other known dressing table, which makes it hard to identify who made it.

The furniture is made from high-quality mahogany, chosen for its smooth surface and swirling grain pattern, which shows that the person who ordered it was wealthy. A highly skilled carver decorated it richly, most noticeably with a scene from Aesop's fable "The Fox and the Grapes" on the bottom center drawers. Aesop's moral stories were the only fiction that Quakers were allowed to read.

CuratorialText: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (clothes presses) functioned, as did the earlier models in northern Europe, to hold hanging clothes and store cherished textiles. This schrank is notable for its extraordinary decoration—bold architectural paneling, carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the date it was made, 1779, only a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Rather than a lightwood inlay, the lettering and floral designs were made by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved in the walnut. Swiss and German designs inspired the decorative motifs, many of which are commonly found on other Pennsylvania German furniture as well as ceramics and fraktur, and helped to connect the owners and makers to their cultural roots. The wardrobe’s monumental size, bold composition, and profuse ornament demonstrate not only Huber’s aesthetic taste and wealth but also the skills and sensibilities of the Germanic artisans who made it.
persona3_adapted:
This wardrobe, known as a *Kleiderschrank* (a German word for "clothes press"), was made in Pennsylvania and used for storing hanging clothes and treasured fabrics, just like earlier versions from northern Europe. What makes this piece special is its striking decoration—bold wood panels, detailed carvings, and lettering that tells us the owner's name, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the year it was made, 1779. That was just one year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer.

Instead of using light-colored wood for the lettering and flower designs, the maker poured hot liquid sulfur into grooves carved into the walnut wood. The patterns were inspired by Swiss and German styles, and many of them also appear on other furniture, pottery, and decorative documents made by Pennsylvania Germans. These designs helped the owners and makers feel connected to their European heritage.

The wardrobe's huge size, strong design, and rich decoration show not only Georg Huber's good taste and wealth but also the skill and craftsmanship of the German artisans who built it.

CuratorialText: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania maintained the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often retaining art forms and styles over several generations. This dish features decoration made using the centuries-old sgraffito technique, achieved by cutting through a slip coating to expose the red clay underneath. The verse in German around the border, extolling the skill of the potter, also shows an adherence to tradition: language provided an immediate means of holding onto ethnic identity. With such rich ornamentation, this dish was clearly not intended for use.
persona3_adapted:
This dish was made in 1786 by a German-speaking potter living in Pennsylvania. The potter, G.H., created it for a woman named Cadarina Raeder.

German immigrants to Pennsylvania kept up the artistic traditions they had brought from Europe. Often, they passed down these art forms and styles through many generations.

The decoration on this dish was made using a very old technique. First, the potter covered the red clay dish with a thin layer of liquid clay, called slip. Then, he scratched a design through the slip to show the red clay underneath. This technique is called sgraffito (pronounced sgra-FEE-toe).

The words around the border are a verse in German that praises the potter's skill. Using the German language was a way for these immigrants to hold onto their cultural identity.

Because of its rich decoration, this dish was clearly not meant to be used for eating or serving food. It was made to be admired.

CuratorialText: In 1795 the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this illusionistic portrait to showcase his artistic skills. At the same time, he wished to represent the roles played by two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who crafted natural science displays, gestures at the top of the stairs as his older brother, Raphaelle, an artist with his palette in hand, strides upward just above a real step attached to the base of the canvas. The life-size portrait is known to have startled and delighted visitors, reputedly even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was dismantled and finally sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture continues to engage and welcome visitors.
persona3_adapted:
In 1795, Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this portrait to show off his skills. The painting looks so real that it tricks the eye—a style meant to impress viewers. Peale also wanted to highlight the different jobs his two sons had at his museum, which was the first in America created to both teach and entertain the public.

In the painting, one son, Titian Ramsay Peale, stands at the top of the stairs and gestures. He was the one who built displays about nature and science. His older brother, Raphaelle, is shown climbing the stairs just above a real step attached to the bottom of the canvas. He holds a painter's palette, showing he was an artist.

This life-sized portrait reportedly amazed and delighted visitors—even George Washington, it's said—before the Peale Museum's collection was broken up and sold in 1854. The Philadelphia Museum of Art bought the painting in 1945, and it still welcomes and charms visitors today.

CuratorialText: A rare early representation of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, and an outstanding example of Peale’s late naturalistic style, the picture is distinguished by the direct and sympathetic encounter between the artist and his subject and a skilled rendering of details. Yarrow’s knit cap suggests a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to assert their religion or African identity, but Peale artfully employed its yellow band to highlight his sitter’s steady gaze with its glint of humor and wisdom. Seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was seeking to record the traits that he believed supported a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive attitude. He perceived Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, industriousness, and sobriety.
persona3_adapted:
This painting is a rare early look at the ethnic and religious diversity in the United States. It’s also a great example of Charles Willson Peale’s later style, which aimed to show people in a natural, lifelike way. What makes the picture stand out is the honest, warm connection between the artist and his subject, along with Peale’s skill at painting small details.

Notice the knit cap worn by the man, named Yarrow. It looks like a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to show their faith or African heritage. Peale cleverly used the yellow band on the cap to draw attention to Yarrow’s steady gaze, which has a hint of both humor and wisdom.

Peale was seventy-seven years old when he painted this portrait. At that point in his life, he was interested in recording the qualities he believed helped people live a long life. In his writings and museum exhibits, Peale often praised making smart choices to stay healthy and keep a positive outlook. He saw Yarrow’s strength and determination in the face of racism and slavery as a model of cleverness, hard work, and self-control.

CuratorialText: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. Decorated in rich red, yellow, and gilt on a black ground and incorporating classical motifs and symbols, the set of drawing room furniture consisted of a pier table, Grecian sofas, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Its legs that curved inwards make the chair the earliest American example of a Klismos chair, a forerunner of the classical style that enjoyed widespread popularity in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum's collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.
persona3_adapted:
This chair is part of a set of beautifully painted furniture that Latrobe created for the Waln family’s living room. The set was decorated in rich red, yellow, and gold on a black background, with designs inspired by ancient Greek and Roman styles. It included a large table meant to stand against a wall, sofas in a Greek style, two card tables, window seats, and several chairs.

The chair’s legs curve inward, making it the earliest known American example of a Klismos chair—a style that became very popular in the late 1810s and 1820s. Besides this chair, the museum also owns six more chairs from the same set, a Greek-style sofa, a card table, and a large table with a mirrored back.

CuratorialText:
The Gross Clinic is recognized as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known Eakins created it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, intending to showcase his talents as an artist and to honor the scientific achievements of his native Philadelphia.
Choosing the city’s world-famous surgeon and teacher Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject, Eakins sets the scene in Jefferson Medical College’s surgical amphitheater. Dr. Gross is shown leading a clinic of five doctors operating on the left thigh of a patient. At the same time Gross is demonstrating to students the relatively new surgical procedure he had developed to treat bone infections. In contrast to the recoiling woman to the left—traditionally identified as the patient’s mother—Gross embodies the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Casting himself as a witness, Eakins can be seen seated to the right of the tunnel railing, sketching or writing.
persona3_adapted:
**The Gross Clinic** is widely considered one of the greatest American paintings ever created. The artist, Thomas Eakins, was young and not yet famous when he made this work. He painted it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition—a huge world’s fair celebrating the nation’s 100th birthday. Eakins had two goals: to show off his own artistic skill, and to honor the scientific achievements of his hometown, Philadelphia.

For his subject, Eakins chose Dr. Samuel Gross, a world-famous surgeon and teacher from the city. The scene takes place in a surgical amphitheater at Jefferson Medical College—a room shaped like a theater, where students could watch operations from raised seats. Dr. Gross is shown leading a team of five doctors as they operate on a patient’s left thigh. At the same time, he is teaching students about a new surgical method he had developed to treat bone infections.

Notice the woman on the left, who is shrinking back in horror. She is traditionally thought to be the patient’s mother. In contrast, Dr. Gross stands calm and confident—the kind of confidence that comes from deep knowledge and experience. Eakins even painted himself into the scene. You can see him sitting on the right side of the railing, near the tunnel-like area, sketching or writing. By doing this, he places himself as a witness to the operation.

CuratorialText: The dramatic rescue from a foundering ship shown here was made possible by a recent innovation in lifesaving technology, the breeches buoy. Secured firmly to ship and shore, the device permitted the transfer of stranded passengers to safety by means of a pulley that was hauled back and forth by crews at either end. Cropped down to its essentials, Homer's composition thrusts us into the midst of the action with massive waves rolling past, drenching the semiconscious woman and her anonymous savior. The Life Line was immediately recognized by critics as a major contribution to American art, portraying a heroic, contemporary subject with both painterly virtuosity and detailed observation.
persona3_adapted:
A dramatic rescue from a sinking ship is shown here. It was made possible by a new lifesaving invention called the breeches buoy. This device was securely tied to both the ship and the shore. It allowed stranded passengers to be moved to safety using a pulley system that crews pulled back and forth from each end.

The artist, Homer, cut the scene down to its most important parts. This pulls us right into the action. Huge waves roll past, soaking a half-conscious woman and her unknown rescuer. Critics immediately recognized *The Life Line* as a major work in American art. It shows a heroic, modern subject with both impressive painting skill and careful attention to detail.

CuratorialText: Mary Cassatt’s flair for digging into an ordinary moment and distilling its emotions stands out in this portrayal of three individuals lulled by a carriage ride into silent reverie. Once titled simply Driving, the painting depicts an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, sister of the artist, and the girl seated primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is unidentified. Usually associated with domestic interiors and the private lives of women and children, here Cassatt explored an outdoor setting and a degree of autonomy enjoyed by upper-class women. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters enjoyed, particularly after 1879, when the family acquired a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly depicted here
persona3_adapted:
In this painting, Mary Cassatt captures a quiet, everyday moment and brings out the feelings hidden within it. Three people are shown riding in a carriage, lost in their own thoughts. The painting was once simply called *Driving*, and it shows a trip through the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris.

The driver is Lydia Cassatt, the artist’s sister. Sitting neatly beside her is Odile Fèvre, the niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom (the person taking care of the horse) is not identified. Cassatt is best known for painting scenes inside homes and focusing on the private lives of women and children. But here, she explores an outdoor setting and shows the freedom that upper-class women could enjoy. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters loved to do, especially after 1879, when their family bought a small carriage and a horse named Bichette, who is partly shown in this painting.

CuratorialText: Tanner painted The Annunciation soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner specialized in religious subjects, and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God. Mary is shown as an adolescent dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other holy attributes. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Tanner painted *The Annunciation* shortly after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. His father was a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, and Tanner often focused on religious subjects in his art. He wanted to see the people, culture, buildings, and light of the Holy Land for himself.

Inspired by what he saw, Tanner created an unusual version of the biblical scene where the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will give birth to the Son of God. In this painting, Mary is shown as a young teenager, wearing simple, wrinkled Middle Eastern peasant clothes. She has no halo or other traditional signs of holiness. Gabriel appears only as a beam of light.

Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon, a major art exhibition. The next year, it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art, making it the first of his works to enter an American museum.

CuratorialText: A father’s love radiates from this iridescent painting of sunlight and youth. Shaped by the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, Daniel Garber adopted Impressionist color and handling during a two-year sojourn in Europe. Returning in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he harmoniously merged nature, art, and domestic life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio renders her smock translucent, while the composition’s serene geometry captures the shimmer of an endless summer afternoon.
persona3_adapted: A father’s love shines through this glowing painting of sunlight and youth. Daniel Garber learned to paint in the realistic style of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts. But after spending two years in Europe, he started using the brighter colors and looser brushwork of the Impressionists. When he returned to the United States in 1907, he moved to a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. There, he blended nature, art, and family life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio makes her smock look almost see-through. The calm, balanced arrangement of the scene captures the feeling of a long, lazy summer afternoon.

CuratorialText: Georgia O’Keeffe once remarked, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is an extraordinary example of her floral compositions, made of sweeping, broad waves of subtly blended hues. The white petals, highlighted in green, are arranged against a pink backdrop, and from each one emerges a bright yellow pistil. Many have interpreted O’Keeffe’s depictions of floral anatomy in relation to sexuality and gender, but the artist always resisted these interpretations, considering them too specific and limiting.
persona3_adapted: Georgia O’Keeffe once said, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is a great example of her flower artwork. It uses wide, flowing brushstrokes with soft, blended colors. The white petals have green highlights and sit against a pink background. From each flower comes a bright yellow center. Many people have seen O’Keeffe’s flower paintings as having to do with sexuality and gender, but the artist always disagreed with that idea. She felt those interpretations were too narrow and didn’t capture what she was trying to express.

CuratorialText: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are among his most important early sculptural work. Inspired by the angular shapes and intersecting lines of the Cubist movement, the artist incorporated the natural patterns and grains of assorted woods into his pieces to emphasize their strong geometric and organic forms. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908–10, and discovered wood as a medium for artistic expression when he began to make carved frames for his canvases. By the mid-1920s he was fully committed to sculpture and extended his definition of the medium to the carving of furniture and architectural interiors. His work is often seen as a forerunner to the contemporary studio furniture movement.
persona3_adapted:
Wharton Esherick created some of his most important early sculptures by carving directly into the walls and woodwork of rooms. He was inspired by the sharp angles and crisscrossing lines of Cubism, an art style that breaks objects into geometric shapes. To make his pieces stand out, he used the natural patterns and grain lines found in different types of wood, which added both structure and a sense of nature to his work.

Esherick was born in Philadelphia in 1887. He first studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908 to 1910. He discovered wood as a way to make art when he started carving frames for his own paintings. By the mid-1920s, he had fully turned to sculpture, and he expanded what sculpture could mean by carving furniture and even entire rooms. Today, his work is often seen as an early influence on the modern studio furniture movement, where artists make one-of-a-kind wooden pieces by hand.

CuratorialText: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-twentieth-century painters of the American scene, although his process of simplification and synthesis often led to haunting, surrealist effects. His picture of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s light-filled kitchen holds an air of something missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log outside the window, like the knife on the table, connected in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experience, his violent dog, and his strong hand on the entire farm. Said Wyeth, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”
persona3_adapted:
Andrew Wyeth was part of a group of mid-1900s American painters who focused on realistic scenes of everyday life. But Wyeth’s way of simplifying and combining details often gave his work a strange, dreamlike quality.

This painting shows the bright, sunlit kitchen of his neighbor, Karl Kuerner. Yet the room feels oddly empty, as if something is missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was really about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” 

Look at the snarling log outside the window and the knife on the table. For Wyeth, these objects were connected to Kuerner’s experiences in war, his aggressive dog, and the tight control he kept over his farm. As Wyeth put it, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”

CuratorialText: In November 1943 this painting was included in Jackson Pollock's stunning solo exhibition at Art of This Century, the innovative New York art gallery run by legendary art patron Peggy Guggenheim. As its title suggests, the work depicts a male figure, embodied in the black columnar form at right, with its mysterious arithmetic graffiti, and a curvy female figure at left, with marvelous catlike eyelashes. Standing on tiny triangular feet, both figures are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint that enhances the sense of energy inherent in the two vertical forms, while also foreshadowing the famous "drip" technique that Pollock would develop later that decade.
persona3_adapted: In November 1943, this painting was part of a major solo show by Jackson Pollock at Art of This Century, a cutting-edge New York gallery run by the famous art supporter Peggy Guggenheim. As the title suggests, the painting shows a male figure—the dark, column-like shape on the right, covered in strange, number-like marks—and a curvy female figure on the left, with striking cat-like eyelashes. Both figures stand on tiny, triangular feet. Around them, the oil paint is splattered and smeared, which makes the two upright shapes feel full of movement and energy. This style also hints at the famous "drip" technique Pollock would become known for later in the 1940s.

CuratorialText: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo riffs on the traditional representations of Franklin found throughout the city—ones that celebrate him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one such stately portrayal of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—ones that shaped his childhood and adult life. The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined dialogue between himself and Franklin around the themes of money, history, violence, and success.
persona3_adapted:
Here you will see Benjamin Franklin like you’ve never seen him before. The artist, Roberto Lugo, was born in Philadelphia. He takes the usual, serious images of Franklin that you find all over the city—the ones that honor him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States—and gives them a completely new twist. (You can see one of those traditional, dignified portraits of Franklin right behind you, in gallery 384.)

Instead of just showing Franklin as a famous historical figure, Lugo surrounds him with songs, symbols, and the names of rappers—things that shaped the artist’s own life, from childhood to adulthood. On the other side of the vase, Lugo also includes a self-portrait. This creates an imagined conversation between himself and Franklin about big topics like money, history, violence, and success.

CuratorialText: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage, framed by a grand red staircase at left and a long red drape mimicking a theater curtain at right. The depicted customers of Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, lack the decorum and manners expected of women in this period. Instead, they frantically dive for clearance items, change clothes, inspect garments, and examine their mirrored reflections. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to observe these women during a moment of unabashed liberation. The action-packed canvas contains an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs, including the Pekingese at the forefront who serves as a surrogate for the artist, as indicated by the "FS"-monogrammed sweater. Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left foreground. Opposite is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture—align with how white gay men often presented themselves at the time.
persona3_adapted:
Florine Stettheimer designed this painting like a theater scene. On the left, a grand red staircase frames the picture, and on the right, a long red curtain looks like the one you'd see on a stage. The women shown here are shopping at Bendel’s, a famous department store in New York City. But they aren't acting polite or proper, as women were expected to at the time. Instead, they're frantically grabbing sale items, changing clothes, checking out fabrics, and looking at themselves in mirrors. Stettheimer lets us peek "behind the curtain" to see these women enjoying a moment of total freedom.

This busy painting includes an amazing thirty-two people and two dogs. One of the dogs, a Pekingese in the front, stands in for the artist herself—you can tell because it's wearing a sweater with her initials, "FS." The store's owner, Henri Bendel, who was gay, stands in the left front of the scene. On the opposite side, there's a salesperson who looks androgynous (neither clearly male nor female). Their slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture were common ways that white gay men presented themselves at that time.

CuratorialText: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
persona3_adapted: Three immortal women wear cloaks and skirts made of grass and leaves. Their hairy bodies mark them as the "Feathered Women" from Daoist stories—mystical beings who live deep in the mountains, searching for healing plants. In this scene, they are showing each other what they have found. The woman on the far left holds a lingzhi fungus, a special mushroom believed to give long life if eaten, and a basket of plants hangs from her arm. The main focus of the painting is a large ginseng root, known for its health benefits, held by the woman in the center. Other plants shown include banana and palm leaves, carried in baskets on the women's backs. This image of Feathered Women displaying precious medicinal items was thought to act as a lucky charm, protecting the owner from sickness.

CuratorialText:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
persona3_adapted:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light that it feels almost weightless when you hold it. If a candle were placed inside, the light would glow and flicker through the tiny, detailed cut-out patterns. Making these patterns required great skill because the clay had to be extremely thin. Artists didn't develop this technique until the early 1700s.

Auspicious Decoration

On each of the six sides, there is a central medallion with one of four flowers: camellia, rose, peony, and lily. These flowers are symbols of peace, unity, and long life.

CuratorialText:
Various deva, or guardian deities, are depicted on this fragment from the outer section of a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Murals representing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although there is some loss of color, we can identify several of the deities:
(Far left) The guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit), wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.
(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and stupa/pagoda.
(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one frontal and two in profile—and multiple arms.
persona3_adapted:
This fragment comes from a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. It shows several **deva**, or guardian deities—beings who protect the temple and the faith. These figures are richly dressed in costumes and jewelry, which was typical of northern Chinese temples built between the late Yuan (1271–1368) and early Ming (1368–1644) dynasties. During this time, the ruling class followed a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism.

Although some of the colors have faded, we can still identify several of the deities:

- **Far left:** Weituo (called Skanda in Sanskrit) is the guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith. He wears a winged helmet and carries a diamond sword, which he uses to destroy demons and enemies of Buddhism.

- **Center:** The Heavenly King of the West holds a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North carries an umbrella and a small pagoda (a type of tower).

- **Right:** The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one facing forward and two in profile—and multiple arms.

CuratorialText: The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple compounds in the Chinese capital of Beijing. Conceived on a grand scale, the compound was built in the early 1400s by the eunuch Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls on the monastery's central axis—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To give visitors a sense of the original context, the Museum installed the ceiling with architectural elements reconstructed from measured drawings made at the original site. In the center is a writhing imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, bracketing, and eight canted panels, each bearing a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved in the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is tianjing, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is well preserved, much of the rich overlay of gold leaf has been lost. A series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets encompass the central element; within these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas. The remainder of the ceiling consists of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers that show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (Lamaism). Each bears a character from a North Indian script (called Lantsha by the Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).
persona3_adapted:
The Zhihua Monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple complexes in Beijing, China. Built on a grand scale in the early 1400s, it was created by a eunuch named Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls along the monastery's central path—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To help visitors imagine how it originally looked, the Museum installed the ceiling with architectural pieces rebuilt from detailed drawings made at the original site.

At the center of the ceiling is a twisting imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, brackets, and eight angled panels, each with a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved into the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is *tianjing*, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is still in good condition, much of the rich gold leaf overlay has worn away.

Around the central element is a series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets. Inside these tiny structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas (enlightened beings). The rest of the ceiling is made up of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers, showing the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (also called Lamaism). Each panel has a character from a North Indian script (known as Lantsha to Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).

CuratorialText: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa installed this monumental painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it held pride of place. The artist’s highly naturalistic rendering of intertwined dragons reflects a fusion of East and West. While the subject of dragons dates back to ancient China, the three-dimensionality and foreshortening of the animals’ forms reflect Hōgai’s familiarity with the Western use of perspective.
persona3_adapted: Ernest Fenollosa, a teacher and art collector, hung this large, impressive painting by Kano Hōgai in his Tokyo home, where it was the centerpiece. The artist’s very lifelike depiction of two dragons twisting together shows a blend of Eastern and Western styles. Dragons have been a subject in art since ancient China, but the way these animals look three-dimensional and seem to recede into space shows that Hōgai was familiar with how Western artists use perspective.

CuratorialText: Fifty-eight sixteenth-century granite architectural elements are presented within the museum gallery in an arrangement designed to resemble a South Indian temple hall (mandapam). In 1913 these pieces lay discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them while on their two-year-long honeymoon. But recent research supports that the majority were created for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There they had been part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, wife of the Hindu deity Vishnu. This shrine had become dilapidated, and the temple authorities dismantled it by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923. Stone slabs between the lion brackets depict several scenes from the Ramayana, the epic tale of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. They once formed parts of a carved frieze showing the entire story. The life-size figures projecting from pillars are deities and mythological characters, including from the Ramayana. The many small images on the pillars include gods and heroes as well as humans (like an architect with his measuring stick).
persona3_adapted:
In this museum gallery, fifty-eight granite pieces from the 1500s are arranged to look like a South Indian temple hall, called a *mandapam*. In 1913, these pieces were found lying unused at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai. A couple from Philadelphia bought them while on their two-year honeymoon. However, recent research shows that most of these pieces were originally made for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There, they were part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, the wife of the Hindu god Vishnu. That shrine had fallen into disrepair, and the temple authorities took it apart by 1907, finishing its replacement by 1923.

Stone slabs between the lion-shaped brackets show scenes from the *Ramayana*, an ancient epic story about one of Vishnu's earthly forms. These slabs were once part of a carved band that told the entire story. The life-sized figures sticking out from the pillars are gods, goddesses, and characters from myths, including some from the *Ramayana*. The many small images on the pillars include gods, heroes, and even everyday people, like an architect holding his measuring stick.

CuratorialText: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape.  Over the past thirty years researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6500 craftsmen who enslaved people.  In fact, this fact is among the most common things we know about an identified craftsman.  Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen were often at work on building sites, at the potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and at dozens of other trades.  Their skills are revealed in the objects that they helped to fashion.  Their identities are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman – but careful reading of period documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3000 enslaved craftsmen by name.  The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at icons of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years.  It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

In the American South, enslaved Black workers and free white craftsmen often worked side by side. Over the past thirty years, researchers at MESDA have found nearly 6,500 craftsmen who owned enslaved people. In fact, this is one of the most common facts we know about these craftsmen.

The people they enslaved are often unnamed in history. But they were hard at work on building sites, at potters' wheels, in furniture workshops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths' shops, and in many other trades. Their skills are visible in the objects they helped create, but their identities are usually lost to time. Sometimes all we know about an enslaved craftsman is a single line in a legal document or a brief mention in an advertisement.

Still, by carefully reading old documents, MESDA researchers have identified nearly 3,000 enslaved craftsmen by name. The exhibition *“Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen* explores famous examples of southern decorative art through the findings of MESDA’s thirty years of research. It brings to light the stories of the many named and unnamed individuals whose hidden work shaped the southern landscape.

CuratorialText: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is known around the world for the manufacture of fine furniture.  However, long before the factories of Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point came into existence, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of cabinetmakers who crafted distinctive regional furniture. By the middle of the eighteenth century, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began pouring into the North Carolina Piedmont in search of inexpensive yet fertile land.  By 1800 about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers called the region home.  Cabinetmakers immediately utilized the abundant walnut, cherry, pine and poplar trees to produce well-made, stylish furniture for their homes.  In most cases, these craftsmen combined national trends with local idiosyncratic preferences to produce regional furniture with a distinctive style and appearance. This exhibit is drawn from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.
persona3_adapted:
North Carolina’s Piedmont region is famous around the world for making high-quality furniture. But long before the big furniture factories in towns like Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point were built, the Piedmont was home to many different kinds of furniture makers. These craftspeople created furniture that was unique to the area.

In the mid-1700s, people started moving to the North Carolina Piedmont from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia. They came looking for land that was both affordable and good for farming. By the year 1800, about 250,000 settlers—mostly of Scotch-Irish, English, and German background—lived in the region.

Right away, these furniture makers used the local trees that were plentiful, like walnut, cherry, pine, and poplar. They built sturdy, attractive furniture for their own homes. Often, they mixed popular styles from across the country with local tastes and habits. This blend created furniture that had its own special look and feel.

This exhibit features pieces from MESDA’s large collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture, all made between 1780 and 1860.

CuratorialText: Masterfully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience are but part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work an the context in which it was created.
persona3_adapted:
The pottery made in early North Carolina was both beautiful and useful. Skilled craftspeople created decorated clay pots, sculpted bottles, fine tableware, and colorful glazed ceramics. These pieces are part of the rich artistic tradition started by the state’s first earthenware potters.

In the late 1700s, European settlers brought pottery-making techniques from the Old World to the Carolina backcountry. Their work was often better—both in artistry and skill—than pottery made in the Middle Atlantic and New England regions at the same time.

These North Carolina potters turned simple materials like clay into everyday objects that were practical, stunning to look at, and full of cultural meaning. This exhibit is the first major show to explore their work and the world in which it was created.

CuratorialText: Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities and from the moment of arrival the earliest settlers documented their landscape with a variety of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town with the Church overseeing all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all land and leased it to individuals who owned only the improvements. Salem was a bustling trade center with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes emphasize the centrality of the Church. They depict the town’s conscious design around a central square, the heart of the community. The square was home to the Gemein Haus (and later Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal dwellings for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. Gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most prominent buildings in early views, suggesting the town’s growing success both agriculturally and commercially. As the nineteenth century progressed, the residents lobbied for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing enterprises grew, and the railroad’s arrival in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely intertwined with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.
persona3_adapted:
Salem was the largest of North Carolina's Moravian communities. From the very beginning, the first settlers recorded their surroundings in detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Founded in 1766, Salem was designed as a congregation town, meaning the Church oversaw everything—money, social life, and spiritual matters. The Church owned all the land and leased it to individuals, who only owned the buildings and other improvements they made.

Salem was a busy trade center with a mill, pottery workshop, tannery, brewery, and store. However, the earliest pictures of the town focus on the Church's central role. They show that the town was carefully planned around a main square, which was the heart of the community. This square held the Gemein Haus (and later the Home Church), where religious services took place; the large shared homes for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. In these early views, gardens and fields appear tucked in and around the most important buildings, suggesting the town was becoming successful both in farming and business.

As the 1800s went on, residents pushed for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston. Then, in 1856, it ended the lease system and allowed people to own land privately. Manufacturing grew, and when the railroad arrived in 1873, it set the stage for huge growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely linked with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries boomed, and by 1900, the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.

CuratorialText:
James River Valley pottery has been the subject of archaeological and collector interest for over half a century.  The James River and its tributaries were home to more than a dozen potteries before the Civil War.  Potters hailing from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland and England, created a rich  hybrid tradition with forms and decorations adapted from their regional styles and wrought in local clays dug from Kaolin to Claymount.

Transcending their status as utilitarian objects, these pots, many exhibited here for the first time, provide a remarkable record and a unique glimpse into a significant and long-lived concentration of pottery manufacturing in eastern Virginia.  Viewed collectively, they present a bold decorative aesthetic rivaling long-recognized and celebrated schools of production seen elsewhere in the commonwealth and the Nation.
Today, many of these important historic sites are threatened by development.  Fortunately, archaeologists and decorative arts scholars are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever.  At the end of 2013 the Chipstone Foundation journal Ceramics in America will publish an extensive article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries based on this ongoing research.
This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, Marshall
