Here is the adapted version of your text, written for a native English speaker with a strong general vocabulary but no specialized art knowledge.

---

**James River Valley Pottery: A Story in Clay**

For more than fifty years, people who study the past (archaeologists) and people who collect old things have been fascinated by the pottery made in the James River Valley. Before the Civil War, there were over a dozen potteries along the James River and its smaller branches.

The potters who worked there came from many different places: New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England. They brought their own local styles with them. When they mixed these styles with the local clays—dug from places like Kaolin and Claymount—they created a rich, new tradition. The shapes and decorations on their pots were a blend of all their different backgrounds.

These pots are much more than just useful containers. Many of them are on display here for the first time. Together, they give us a remarkable look at a large and long-lasting center of pottery-making in eastern Virginia. When you see them all together, you can appreciate their bold, decorative style. This style is just as impressive as the famous pottery traditions found in other parts of Virginia and the rest of the country.

Unfortunately, many of the places where this pottery was made are now in danger. New development is threatening these historic sites. The good news is that archaeologists and scholars who study decorative arts are working quickly—ahead of the bulldozers—to document and understand this important legacy before it is lost forever. In late 2013, the journal *Ceramics in America* (published by the Chipstone Foundation) will release a detailed article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.
persona3_adapted:
An Irish schoolteacher. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A female pastel artist from several different countries. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. What do all these people have in common? They were all “lately from” somewhere else—meaning they had recently arrived—and they came to the American South during the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition shows objects made by these skilled workers, whose backgrounds spanned continents and oceans. Wherever possible, it links those objects to the makers’ own words—through letters, notes, autobiographies, business records, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, these items tell the story of their journeys, the communities they built in their new homes, how they responded to changing political times, and how they mixed traditions from different cultures to create something fresh and new.

CuratorialText: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.
persona3_adapted: Early Southerners drank a lot—almost twenty gallons of alcohol per person each year. They drank when they woke up, while they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how they drank mattered just as much as what they drank. This exhibit looks at the objects people used for drinking, from fancy imported wine served at polished wooden tables to local whiskey poured from simple clay jugs. These items show us what was important to early Southern drinkers.

CuratorialText: this exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 18th and early 19th centuries to explore how maps were made, used, and did work in the early American South. Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit includes two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s General Map of the Middle British Colonies produced at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.

**Adapted Text:**

This exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 1700s and early 1800s to show how maps were made, used, and what they did in the early American South. The show is called *Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South*. It includes maps from MESDA and Old Salem’s collections, plus major loans from private collections, the Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province.

Among the items on display are two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first printing of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s *General Map of the Middle British Colonies*, which were printed at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of land he owned along the Ohio River.

CuratorialText: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.
persona3_adapted:
**Alabama’s Pottery: A Story of People and Place**

Alabama’s pottery traditions come from a unique mix of people who were “thrown together” across the state. This exhibition focuses on the human hands that made, used, and saved these objects.

Long before Alabama became a state, Indigenous peoples—including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw—were making pottery. They followed traditions brought by their ancestors into the region more than 10,000 years ago. When European colonists and later American settlers arrived, they were both a danger and a source of new customers for these skilled potters. In response, potters created new styles that mixed their own traditional techniques and designs with what these foreign buyers wanted.

The shape of Alabama’s land also played a big role in how and where different potters and people came together. A long ridge of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north through the middle of the state. Most 19th-century potters who lived north of these mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. Those south of the mountains mostly came from Georgia or South Carolina. Along the Gulf Coast, French, Spanish, and British colonists first met and traded with Alabama’s Indigenous peoples.

Whether potters arrived from the north, east, or across the Atlantic Ocean, they often brought enslaved people of African descent with them—some of whom were skilled ceramic makers. Together, all these different hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) displays a wide range of items from the early American South. These include furniture, pottery, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles. Whether they are fine art or everyday folk art, these objects tell the stories of the many different makers and buyers who lived in the South for over four hundred years.

The early South is usually split into three main cultural areas: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry. The rivers, roads, and natural resources in each of these areas shaped how people lived and where they settled. Each region had its own mix of ethnic and religious groups: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed Christians, as well as free and enslaved people of African descent. The lively interactions among these different groups gave the decorative arts of the early South their unique character.

CuratorialText:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time

The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery holds the largest collection of pottery made in the American South before the Civil War. This includes pieces from Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Of all the decorative arts, pottery is the one most closely tied to a specific place. The southern pottery in this gallery shows how makers, buyers, culture, and the local landscape all influenced each other. These objects are a physical reminder of how potters used local materials to create containers that were both practical and beautiful, meeting the needs of their communities.

This virtual gallery gives you two ways to explore the southern ceramics on display in the Mariner Gallery. First, you can view a series of virtual exhibits curated by Robert Hunter, a well-known ceramics expert and editor of *Ceramics in America*. These exhibits explore important themes—such as tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—through the lens of southern pottery. Second, you can browse the objects by material: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline-glazed stoneware. On these pages, you can also filter the items by maker, location, and time period.

CuratorialText: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.

What started as a collection focused on Tom's home state of North Carolina gradually grew to include important texts from all seven southern states covered by the museum. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries—from 1585 to 1865—the Gray collection perfectly complements the museum's and Old Salem's collections. Items from the collection are displayed in the museum's galleries and are also available for researchers. A selection of highlights is shown below.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork collected from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia.  This collection was originally assembled by Frank L. Horton to serve as backdrops for period room installations.  Today, in addition to providing scale and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural collections are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right that that can tell stories of style, craftsmanship, migration, ethnicity, and race.

These items were originally gathered by Frank L. Horton to create realistic backgrounds for rooms set up to look like historical interiors. Today, these architectural pieces do more than just help show the size and feel of the rooms in our study galleries. They are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right, telling stories about style, skill, movement of people, ethnicity, and race.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making continues today through the talented craftsmen at the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. CuratorialText: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.

By the 1780s, Salem had its own orchestra, and brass bands often performed during religious services. They also played to announce important events like births and deaths in the town. 

The collection at Old Salem Museums & Gardens includes musical instruments and related items that show how important music was to this community. Other objects in the collection highlight the skill of musical instrument makers from other parts of the American South as well.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. He also has gold bullion epaulets on his shoulders. Under his coat, he wears a black inner collar over a white neckcloth. His hair is graying and tied back in a knot with a black ribbon.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, dark complexion and a shaven head, who is facing half-right with hands folded resting on a table . His right hand holds a quill pen and the left is holding a document with a name written thereon. He wears a white loose collar shirt with deep red robe and turban of the same red material, and sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown fabric with brass tacks. The background is a dark green drapery to the left and books on the right.
persona3_adapted:
This is a painting of a man. He has dark eyes, dark skin, and a shaved head. He is turned slightly to the right, with his hands resting on a table. In his right hand, he holds a feather pen. In his left hand, he holds a document with a name written on it.

He is wearing a white shirt with a loose collar, and over it, a deep red robe and a matching red turban. He sits in a chair covered in red-brown fabric, which is held in place by small brass nails.

The background is dark green fabric on the left side, and on the right, there are books.

CuratorialText: Bust portrait, oil on canvas, of woman wearing a pearl necklace and blue dress with lace trim, in mahogany architectural frame with cut-out corners.
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of a woman from the chest up. She is wearing a blue dress with lace trim and a pearl necklace. The painting is done in oil on canvas and is displayed in a dark brown, carved wooden frame with cut-out corners.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Richard Sprigg of Strawberry Hill, Maryland. Young man facing forward, his body is slightly to the right, his left hand in his vest, wearing a dark beige coat and light silk vest, with white stock. He has a ruddy complexion, dark eyes, brown hair. The background varies from greenish-gray to the lower right to very dark gray in upper left and has lunettes in the corners.
persona3_adapted: A painting in oil on canvas shows Richard Sprigg, a young man from Strawberry Hill, Maryland. He faces forward, with his body turned slightly to the right and his left hand tucked into his vest. He wears a dark beige coat and a light silk vest, with a white stock around his neck. His complexion is ruddy, and he has dark eyes and brown hair. The background shifts from greenish-gray on the lower right to very dark gray on the upper left, with small arched shapes in the corners.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman facing half-right. The subject has dark hair combed upward to expose her left ear, and pearls in her hair that hang down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk cross-bodice dress tied with a dark red sash. The pinned-up sleeves of the garment revealing a light under-garment. She is posed with her left arm upon a table, the right at her hip, and foliage painted in the background.
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of a woman, shown from the chest up, looking slightly to the right. Her dark hair is pulled up to show her left ear, and she has pearls woven into her hair that fall over her right shoulder. She is wearing a blue silk dress with a crisscross bodice, tied with a dark red sash. The sleeves are pinned up, revealing a lighter fabric underneath. She rests her left arm on a table and places her right hand on her hip. In the background, there are leaves and plants.

CuratorialText: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
persona3_adapted: A watercolor painting of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia, done entirely in shades of gray. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, who was the second Clerk of Court for Hanover County. In 1840, the property was passed down to his son, George William Pollard. The house stayed in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed this watercolor.

CuratorialText: Painting, done in oil on mahogany panel, of a young man facing half-right. The gentleman has dark hair and side-burns, and is dressed in a white shirt, tie and vest, and a black coat supporting a high collar. There is an inscription on the back of the panel.
persona3_adapted: This is a painting of a young man, shown from the chest up and turned slightly to the right. He has dark hair and sideburns, and is wearing a white shirt, a tie, a vest, and a black coat with a high collar. The painting was made with oil paint on a wooden panel made from mahogany. On the back of the panel, there is a handwritten note.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing half-left. The work is a half-length portrait. The subject has gray hair, dark gray eyes, wears a dark brown coat with a high turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and white stock at the neck.
persona3_adapted: A painting in oil on canvas shows a man turned slightly to the left. It is a portrait from the chest up. He has gray hair and dark gray eyes. He is wearing a dark brown coat with a high, folded-down collar, a yellow vest, and a white cloth tied at his neck.

CuratorialText: This painting depicts a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a scene that the artist returned to regularly. Another version of this composition is in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a curve in the Cumberland River, close to the Hermitage—the home of former U.S. president Andrew Jackson (1767–1845). The artist painted this view many times. A similar version of this scene is owned by the Greenville County Museum of Art.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Charles Town showing the city waterfront as seen from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship is in the left foreground, the opposite shore in foreground with various figures along the shore.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows the waterfront of Charles Town (now Charleston, South Carolina) as it looked from across the Cooper River. In the front left, you can see a large sailing ship. On the opposite shore, in the foreground, there are several people along the riverbank.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, waist-length portrait of a lady wearing a gray satin dress with gray stomacher in front and white lace at neck and sleeves, her hair is pulled tightly back, a loop of pearls in it on the left side of the portrait. Her right hand holds a pink rose.
persona3_adapted: This woman wears a gray satin dress with a fitted panel down the front and white lace at her neck and wrists. Her hair is pulled back tightly, with a loop of pearls on the left side of the painting. In her right hand, she holds a pink rose. This is an oil painting on canvas, showing her from the waist up.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of a man facing half-left and seated in a green painted chair. The man has a high foreheard, dark hair, dark blue eyes, ruddy complexion, and is wearing a dark coat mounted with gray buttons, a white stock, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is on his neck cloth. The painting is signed in the lower left corner.
persona3_adapted: This man sits in a green chair, turned slightly to the left. He has a high forehead, dark hair, and blue eyes. His skin is ruddy, meaning it has a reddish, healthy glow. He wears a dark coat with gray buttons, a white neckcloth, and a yellow vest. A pin with red and pearl colors is fastened to his neckcloth. The artist signed the painting in the bottom left corner.
